{{Short description|Act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom}} {{Use dmy dates|date=September 2024}} {{Use British English|date=September 2024}} {{Infobox UK legislation | short_title = Statute Law Revision Act 1875{{efn|Section 4.}} | type = Act | parliament = Parliament of the United Kingdom | long_title = An Act for further promoting the Revision of the Statute Law by repealing certain Enactments which have ceased to be in force or have become unnecessary. | year = 1875 | citation = [[38 & 39 Vict.]] c. 66 | introduced_by = | introduced_lords = [[Hugh Cairns, 1st Earl Cairns]] | territorial_extent = [[United Kingdom]] | royal_assent = 11 August 1875 | commencement = 11 August 1875{{efn|The [[Acts of Parliament (Commencement) Act 1793]].}} | repeal_date = | amends = See {{section link||Repealed enactments}} | replaces = See {{section link||Repealed enactments}} | amendments = {{ubli|[[Statute Law Revision Act 1878]]|[[Statute Law Revision Act 1894]]}} | related_legislation = {{ubli|[[Repeal of Obsolete Statutes Act 1856]]|See [[Statute Law Revision Act]]}} | repealing_legislation = | status = Partially_Repealed |millbankhansard = https://hansard.parliament.uk/search/Contributions?startDate=1875-01-01&endDate=1876-01-01&searchTerm=%22Statute%20Law%20Revision%22&partial=False&sortOrder=1 | original_text = https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/Vict/38-39/66/enacted | legislation_history = https://hansard.parliament.uk/search/Contributions?startDate=1875-01-01&endDate=1876-01-01&searchTerm=%22Statute%20Law%20Revision%22&partial=False&sortOrder=1 }}
The '''Statute Law Revision Act 1875''' ([[38 & 39 Vict.]] c. 66) is an [[Act of Parliament (United Kingdom)|act]] of the [[Parliament of the United Kingdom]] that repealed for the [[United Kingdom]] enactments from 1725 to 1868 which had ceased to be in force or had become necessary. The act was intended, in particular, to facilitate the preparation of the [[revised edition of the statutes]], then in progress.
Section 2 of, and schedule 2 to, the [[Statute Law Revision Act 1878]] ([[41 & 42 Vict.]] c. 79) revived several acts repealed by the act, including:
* [[Lunacy Act 1845]] ([[8 & 9 Vict.]] c. 100) * [[Lunatic Asylums (Ireland) Act 1846]] ([[9 & 10 Vict.]] c. 115) * [[Incumbered Estates (Ireland) Act 1852]] ([[16 & 17 Vict.]] c. 67)
Section 3 of the [[Statute Law Revision Act 1878]] ([[41 & 42 Vict.]] c. 79) replaced the text "The Schedule" in the partial repeal of the [[Industrial Schools Act 1866]] ([[29 & 30 Vict.]]) with "The First Schedule".
== Background == {{Main|Statute law revision}} In the [[United Kingdom]], [[Act of Parliament (United Kingdom)|acts of Parliament]] remain in force until expressly repealed. [[William Blackstone|Blackstone]]'s ''[[Commentaries on the Laws of England]]'', published in the late [[18th-century]], raised questions about the system and structure of the [[common law]] and the poor drafting and disorder of the existing [[statute book]].<ref name=":7">{{Cite journal |last=Farmer |first=Lindsay |date=2000 |title=Reconstructing the English Codification Debate: The Criminal Law Commissioners, 1833-45 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/744300 |journal=Law and History Review |volume=18 |issue=2 |pages=397–425 |doi=10.2307/744300 |jstor=744300 |issn=0738-2480|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
From 1810 to 1825, the ''[[The Statutes of the Realm]]'' was published, providing the first authoritative collection of acts.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book |last=Ilbert |first=Courtenay |author-link=Courtenay Ilbert |url=https://archive.org/details/legislativemetho00ilbeiala/page/56 |title=Legislative methods and forms |publisher=[[Clarendon Press]] |year=1901 |location=Oxford |pages=43–76 |language=en |access-date=9 September 2024}} {{Source-attribution}}</ref> The first [[statute law revision act]] was not passed until 1856 with the [[Repeal of Obsolete Statutes Act 1856]] (19 & 20 Vict. c. 64). This approach — focusing on removing obsolete laws from the statute book followed by consolidation — was proposed by [[Peter Locke King]] [[Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)|MP]], who had been highly critical of previous commissions' approaches, expenditures, and lack of results.<ref>{{Cite hansard|title=Supply—Miscellaneous Estimates|jurisdiction=Parliament of the United Kingdom|house=House of Commons|date=2 June 1856|volume=142|url=https://hansard.parliament.uk/Commons/1856-06-02/debates/941919a6-49da-4d77-862d-180d89f77c54/Supply%E2%80%94MiscellaneousEstimates|column_start=865|column_end=880}}</ref>
{| class="wikitable" |+Previous Acts !Year passed !Title !Citation !Effect |- |1861 |[[Statute Law Revision Act 1861]] |[[24 & 25 Vict.]] c. 101 |Repealed or amended over 800 enactments |- |1863 |[[Statute Law Revision Act 1863]] |[[26 & 27 Vict.]] c. 125 |Repealed or amended over 1,600 enactments for [[England and Wales]] |- |1867 |[[Statute Law Revision Act 1867]] |[[30 & 31 Vict.]] c. 59 |Repealed or amended over 1,380 enactments |- |1870 |[[Statute Law Revision Act 1870]] |[[33 & 34 Vict.]] c. 69 |Repealed or amended over 250 enactments |- |1871 |[[Promissory Oaths Act 1871]] |[[34 & 35 Vict.]] c. 48 |Repealed or amended almost 200 enactments |- |1871 |[[Statute Law Revision Act 1871]] |[[34 & 35 Vict.]] c. 116 |Repealed or amended over 1,060 enactments |- |1872 |[[Statute Law Revision Act 1872]] |[[35 & 36 Vict.]] c. 63 |Repealed or amended almost 490 enactments |- |1872 |[[Statute Law (Ireland) Revision Act 1872]] |[[35 & 36 Vict.]] c. 98 |Repealed or amended over 1,050 enactments |- |1872 |[[Statute Law Revision Act 1872 (No. 2)]] |[[35 & 36 Vict.]] c. 97 |Repealed or amended almost 260 enactments |- |1873 |[[Statute Law Revision Act 1873]] |[[36 & 37 Vict.]] c. 91 |Repealed or amended 1,225 enactments |- |1874 |[[Statute Law Revision Act 1874]] |[[37 & 38 Vict.]] c. 35 |Repealed or amended over 490 enactments |- |1874 |[[Statute Law Revision Act 1874 (No. 2)]] |[[37 & 38 Vict.]] c. 96 |Repealed or amended almost 470 enactments |}
== Passage == The Statute Law Revision Bill had its [[first reading]] in the [[House of Lords]] on 5 July 1875,<ref>{{Cite hansard|title=Minutes|jurisdiction=Parliament of the United Kingdom|house=House of Lords|date=5 July 1875|volume=225|url=https://hansard.parliament.uk/Lords/1875-07-05/debates/c19d3c83-9484-4e16-aeab-6d4a8593dd94/HouseOfLords|column=936}}</ref> introduced by the [[Lord Chancellor]], [[Hugh Cairns, 1st Earl Cairns]].<ref>{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YvhlAAAAcAAJ&pg=RA17-PA223 |title=Hansard's Parliamentary Debates: 1875 |date=1875 |language=en}}</ref> The bill had its [[second reading]] in the [[House of Lords]] on 26 June 1874 and was referred to a [[Committee of the Whole House (United Kingdom)|committee of the whole house]].<ref>{{Cite hansard|title=Minutes|jurisdiction=Parliament of the United Kingdom|house=House of Lords|date=26 July 1875|volume=226|url=https://hansard.parliament.uk/Lords/1875-07-26/debates/9748af6d-0ca4-4d52-91ad-990fba7ea52e/StatuteLawRevisionBill|column=?|column_start=7|column_end=9}}</ref> In his speech introducing the bill, the [[Lord Chancellor]], [[Hugh Cairns, 1st Earl Cairns]] mentioned that this was the eleventh volume of statute revision work and that further [[consolidation bills]] would be presented to Parliament for consideration during the [[Parliamentary Recess|parliamentary recess]]. The committee met and reported on 27 July 1875, without amendments.<ref>{{Cite hansard|title=Minutes|jurisdiction=Parliament of the United Kingdom|house=House of Lords|date=27 July 1875|volume=226|url=https://hansard.parliament.uk/Lords/1875-07-27/debates/e6c1aa0c-39c0-4117-af6c-0e6633b1774f/HouseOfLords|column=84}}</ref> The bill had its [[third reading]] in the [[House of Lords]] on 29 July 1874 and passed, without amendments.<ref>{{Cite hansard|title=Minutes|jurisdiction=Parliament of the United Kingdom|house=House of Lords|date=28 July 1875|volume=226|url=https://hansard.parliament.uk/Lords/1875-07-29/debates/be1a0f6c-61fa-496a-ae89-a28057ddddff/HouseOfLords|column=163}}</ref>
The bill its [[first reading]] in the [[House of Commons of the United Kingdom|House of Commons]] on 30 July 1875.<ref>{{Cite hansard|title=Minutes|jurisdiction=Parliament of the United Kingdom|house=House of Commons|date=30 July 1875|volume=226|url=https://hansard.parliament.uk/Commons/1875-07-30/debates/7ab8dd80-f9fb-47a4-af70-79d7d89e1d37/HouseOfCommons|column=215}}</ref> The bill had its [[second reading]] in the [[House of Commons of the United Kingdom|House of Commons]] on 2 August 1875 and was committed to a [[Committee of the Whole House (United Kingdom)|committee of the whole house]],<ref>{{Cite hansard|title=Minutes|jurisdiction=Parliament of the United Kingdom|house=House of Commons|date=2 August 1875|volume=226|url=https://hansard.parliament.uk/Commons/1875-08-02/debates/0f9ffef7-6bc1-4eff-99c5-f276173bf4bc/HouseOfCommons|column=370}}</ref> which met and reported without amendment on 3 August 1875.<ref>{{Cite hansard|title=Minutes|jurisdiction=Parliament of the United Kingdom|house=House of Commons|date=3 July 1875|volume=226|url=https://hansard.parliament.uk/Commons/1875-08-03/debates/3b53d4c2-2a41-4ecf-8c8d-3167d7fd3048/HouseOfCommons|column=443}}</ref> The bill had its [[third reading]] in the [[House of Commons of the United Kingdom|House of Commons]] on 4 August 1875 and passed, without amendments.<ref>{{Cite hansard|title=Minutes|jurisdiction=Parliament of the United Kingdom|house=House of Commons|date=4 August 1875|volume=226|url=https://hansard.parliament.uk/Commons/1875-08-04/debates/0cb8da8f-993a-4ad2-a05b-e33fb2dc4b3e/HouseOfCommons|column=508}}</ref>
The bill was granted [[royal assent]] on 11 August 1875.<ref>{{Cite hansard|title=Minutes|jurisdiction=Parliament of the United Kingdom|house=House of Lords|date=11 August 1875|volume=226|url=https://hansard.parliament.uk/Lords/1875-08-11/debates/b20316c9-627c-482d-af39-e78d252dbd8e/HouseOfLords|column=847}}</ref>
== Legacy == The act was intended, in particular, to facilitate the preparation of a [[revised edition of the statutes]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Britain |first=Great |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=eQMUAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA593 |title=The Public General Statutes |date=1875 |publisher=Eyre and Spottiswoode at the Queen's Printing Office. |pages=593–706 |language=en}}</ref>
This act was amended by sections 2 and 3 of the [[Statute Law Revision Act 1878]] ([[41 & 42 Vict.]] c. 79).
Sections 2 and 3 of, and the schedule to, the act, were repealed by section 1 of, and schedule 1 to, the [[Statute Law Revision Act 1894]] ([[57 & 58 Vict.]] c. 56).
The act was partly in force in [[Great Britain]] at the end of 2010.<ref>The [[Chronological Table of the Statutes]], 1235 - 2010. [[The Stationery Office]]. 2011. {{ISBN|978-0-11-840509-6}}. Part 1. Page 494, read with pages viii and x.</ref>
The enactments which were repealed (whether for the whole or any part of the United Kingdom) by this act were repealed so far as they extended to the [[Isle of Man]]<ref>The [[Statute Law Revision (Isle of Man) Act 1991]], sections [http://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1991/61/section/1 1(1)] and [http://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1991/61/section/2 2(2)] and [http://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/1991/61/schedule/1 Schedule 1]</ref> on 25 July 1991.<ref>The [[Interpretation Act 1978]], section 4(b)</ref>
The act was retained for the [[Republic of Ireland]] by [http://www.irishstatutebook.ie/2007/en/act/pub/0028/sec0002.html section 2(2)(a)] of, and Part 4 of Schedule 1 to, the [[Statute Law Revision Act 2007]].
As to repeals by this act, see ''Campbell v Cochrane''<ref>[https://books.google.com/books?id=3_1QAQAAMAAJ 1928 JC] 25; [https://books.google.com/books?id=7pcuAAAAIAAJ 1928 SLT] 394</ref> and ''[[Clarke v Bradlaugh]]''.<ref>(1881) 30 Weekly Reporter [https://books.google.com/books?id=CQNHAQAAIAAJ&pg=PA53 53]. See also Weekly Notes, 19 November 1881, [https://books.google.com/books?id=3agDAAAAQAAJ&pg=PA137 p 137]; John Mews, "Parliament", A Digest of the reported Decisions of all the Courts . . . during the Year 1882, Henry Sweet, 1883, [https://books.google.com/books?id=PvhCAQAAMAAJ&pg=PA317 col 317]; Cecil C M Dale, "Parliamentary Oath", An Analytical Digest of the Cases published in the New Series of the Law Journal Reports and other Contemporary Reports . . . from Michaelmas Sittings 1880 to Trinity Sittings 1885, Friend Edward Streeten, 1886, [https://books.google.com/books?id=QlU1AQAAMAAJ&pg=PA381 p 381]; [[Ephraim Arnold Jacob]], An Analytical Digest of the Law and Practice of the Courts of Common Law, Divorce, Probate, Admiralty and Bankruptcy, and of the High Court of Justice and the Court of Appeal of England, 1883, vol 10, [https://books.google.com/books?id=cRgtAQAAMAAJ&pg=PA16397 col 16397]; Stone (ed), The Law Reports, Digest of Cases, 1892, vol 3, [https://books.google.com/books?id=SaAyAQAAMAAJ&pg=PA4829 col 4829]; Ware, Hutchins and Baker, The Law Reports, 1881 to 1885, Digest of Cases, London, 1886, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p6xBAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA1131 col 1132].</ref><ref>[[Charles Bradlaugh]] was a party in this case.</ref> As to omission of parts of the [[Naval Discipline Act 1866]] ([[29 & 30 Vict.]] c. 109) repealed by the act from copies of that act, see section 7(2) of the [[Naval Discipline Act 1884]] ([[47 & 48 Vict.]] c. 39).<ref>Reprinted in The Victorian Statutes, Melbourne, 1890, vol 7, [https://books.google.com/books?id=fcZKAQAAMAAJ&pg=PA473 p 473]</ref>
== Repealed enactments == Section 1 of the act repealed 1,401 enactments, listed in the schedule to the act, across six categories:<ref>{{Cite book |last=Lords |first=Great Britain Parliament House of |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ZxpcAAAAQAAJ&pg=RA6-PA1 |title=Public Bills |date=1875 |language=en}}</ref>{{Efn|The Note of the bill, unlike the schedule, gives commentary on each act, noting any earlier repeals and the reason for the new repeal.}}
* Expired * Spent * Repealed in general terms * Virtually repealed * Superseded * Obsolete
Section 1 of the act included several safeguards to ensure that the repeal does not negatively affect existing rights or ongoing legal matters. Specifically, any legal rights, privileges, or remedies already obtained under the repealed laws, as well as any legal proceedings or principles established by them, remain unaffected. Section 1 of the act also ensured that repealed enactments that have been incorporated into other laws would continue to have legal effect in those contexts. Moreover, the repeal would not revive any former rights, offices, or jurisdictions that had already been abolished.<ref name=":0" />
Section 2 of the act repealed the [[Consecration of Bishops Abroad Act 1786]] ([[26 Geo. 3]] c. 84), effective from the passing of the [[Colonial Clergy Act 1874]] ([[37 & 38 Vict.]] c. 77), and provided that any previous legal protections or provisions that had been included in the original Act would not apply to this repeal.<ref name=":0" />
Section 3 of the act provided that section 25 of the [[Licensing (Scotland) Act 1828]] ([[9 Geo. 4]]. c. 58), which had been repealed by the [[Statute Law Revision Act 1873]] ([[36 & 37 Vict.]] c. 91), was revived, as from the repeal thereof, and that all proceedings taken thereunder since that repeal were as valid and effectual as if that section had not been repealed.<ref name=":0" /> {| class="wikitable" !Citation !Short title !Title !Extent of repeal |- |[[12 Geo. 1]]. c. 4 |[[Malt Duties, etc. Act 1725]] |An Act the title of which begins with the words,—An Act for continuing the Duties upon Malt,—and ends with the words,—Lottery Tickets and Orders, lost, burnt, or otherwise destroyed. |The whole act. |- |[[1 & 2 Geo. 4]]. c. 77 |[[Gaol Fees Abolition (Ireland) Act 1821]] |An Act to abolish the Payment by Prisoners in Ireland of Gaol Fees, and all other Fees relating to the Custody, Maintenance, Trial, or Discharge of such Prisoners, and to prevent Abuses by Gaolers, Bailiffs, and other Officers. |Section Eight. |- |[[3 Geo. 4]]. c. 18 |[[Duties on Malt Act 1822]] |An Act to lower the Excise Duty on Malt charged by an Act made in the Second Year of His present Majesty, to allow the said Duty on Malt in Stock, and to make Regulations for better securing the Duties on Malt. |The whole act. |- |[[6 Geo. 4]]. c. 50 |[[Juries Act 1825]] |An Act for consolidating and amending the Laws relative to Jurors and Juries. |Section Five from "and shall annex" to the end of that Section. Section Six to "upon any High Constable" and from "provided also, that where any Parish or Township" to the end of that Section. Section Ten, the words "to the High Constable and" and from "and the High Constable shall receive" to the end of that Section. Section Forty-seven. Section Forty-six, the words "to any High Constable, or". The Warrant for returning Lists of Jurors, and Precept for returning Lists of Jurors, in the Schedule. |- |[[7 Geo. 4]]. c. 74 |[[Prisons (Ireland) Act 1826]] |An Act for consolidating and amending the Laws relating to Prisons in Ireland. |Sections One hundred and eighteen to One hundred and twenty-two. Section One hundred and twenty-three so far as it relates to the Marshalsea of the Four Courts. Section One hundred and thirty-nine. |- |[[9 Geo. 4]]. c. 14 |[[Statute of Frauds Amendment Act 1828]] |An Act for rendering a written Memorandum necessary to the Validity of certain Promises and Engagements. |Section Five. |- |[[9 Geo. 4]]. c. 83 |[[Australian Courts Act 1828]] |An Act to provide for the Administration of Justice in New South Wales and Van Diemen's Land, and for the more effectual Government thereof, and for other Purposes relating thereto. |Section Thirty-eight. Repealed as to all Her Majesty's Dominions. |- |[[11 Geo. 4 & 1 Will. 4]]. c. 49 |[[Excise Act 1830]] |An Act to impose a Duty on Beer and Spirits. |The whole act. |- |[[2 & 3 Will. 4]]. c. 88 |[[Representation of the People (Ireland) Act 1832]] |An Act to amend the Representation of the People of Ireland. |Section Fifty-five from "and that all Elections" to the end of that Section. Section Sixty-eight from "and such Deputy" to the end of that Section. |- |[[3 & 4 Will. 4]]. c. 41 |[[Judicial Committee Act 1833]] |An Act for the better Administration of Justice in His Majesty's Privy Council. |Section Twenty-nine. |- |[[3 & 4 Will. 4]]. c. 74 |[[Fines and Recoveries Act 1833]] |An Act for the Abolition of Fines and Recoveries, and for the Substitution of more simple Modes of Assurance. |Section Eight, the words "and two persons shall in such cases be present". |- |[[4 & 5 Will. 4]]. c. 36 |[[Central Criminal Court Act 1834]] |An Act for establishing a new Court for the Trial of Offences committed in the Metropolis and Parts adjoining. |Section Thirteen. |- |[[4 & 5 Will. 4]]. c. 85 |[[Beerhouse Act 1834]] |An Act to amend an Act passed in the First Year of His present Majesty relating to the Sale of Beer and Cider by Retail in England. |Section Twenty from "and also with reference to the Sureties of such Persons", "and to the Sureties of all such Persons in respect of such Offences", and "and to the Sureties of such Persons". |- |[[5 & 6 Will. 4]]. c. 76 |[[Municipal Corporations Act 1835]] |An Act to provide for the Regulation of Municipal Corporations in England and Wales. |Section Four from "and also such Days and no other than fifteen Days after such Vacancy". Sections One hundred and twenty-two and One hundred and twenty-three. |- |[[6 & 7 Will. 4]]. c. 106 |[[Stannaries Act 1836]] |An Act to make Provision for the better and more expeditious Administration of Justice in the Stannaries of Cornwall, and for enlarging the Jurisdiction and improving the Practice and Proceedings in the Courts of the said Stannaries. |Section Eight, the words "shall not exceed". |- |[[7 Will. 4 & 1 Vict.]] c. 82 |[[County Fermanagh Baronies Act 1837]] |An Act to amend the Law relating to Grand Juries in Ireland, so far as respects the County of Fermanagh to reconstruct the Baronial Subdivisions of the said County. |Section Two from "and are hereby" to the end of that Section. |- |[[2 & 3 Vict.]] c. 47 |[[Metropolitan Police Act 1839]] |An Act for improving the Police in and near the Metropolis. |Section Twenty-five. |- |[[2 & 3 Vict.]] c. 70 |[[New South Wales and Van Diemen's Land Act 1839]] |An Act the title of which begins with the words,—An Act to amend an Act of King George the Fourth to provide for the Administration of Justice in New South Wales and Van Diemen's Land,—and ends with the words,—Parliament. |Repealed as to all Her Majesty's Dominions. |- |[[2 & 3 Vict.]] c. 75 |[[Constabulary (Ireland) Act 1839]] |An Act for the better Regulation of the Constabulary Force in Ireland. |Section Five, the word "Paymasters". The Schedule. |- | [[8 & 9 Vict.]] c. 11 || [[Sheriffs (Wales) Act 1845]]|| An Act for assigning Sheriffs in Wales. || Section Two. |- | [[8 & 9 Vict.]] c. 15 || [[Auctioneers Act 1845]]|| An Act to repeal the Duties of Excise on Sales by Auction, and to impose a new Duty on the Licence to be taken out by all Auctioneers in the United Kingdom. || Section One. Section Four from "that Auctioneers" to "last-mentioned Licence list". Section Seven from "The Authority of an Act passed in the Sixth and Seventh" to "Bill in Ireland", and so far as it relates to the repeal of part of c. 4. c. 53. * https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Talk:Donald_Trump/Archive_69#Archiving_of_live_links * https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Talk:Donald_Trump/Archive_74#Archive_links --> [[File:Steele_Dossier_page_1.jpg|thumb|upright=1.5|Steele Dossier (p. 1)]] {{Trump–Russia relations}} {{Donald Trump sidebar|expanded=Russia controversies}}
The '''Steele dossier''', also known as the '''Trump–Russia dossier''',<ref name="Allen_3/7/2020" /> is a controversial political [[opposition research]] report on the [[Donald Trump 2016 presidential campaign|2016 presidential campaign]] of [[Donald Trump]] compiled by [[counterintelligence]] specialist [[Christopher Steele]].<ref name="Harding_Sabbagh_11/1/2019" /> It was published without permission in 2017 as an unfinished {{Nowrap|35-page}} compilation of "unverified, and potentially unverifiable"{{efn |''BuzzFeed'' said the information included "specific, unverified, and potentially unverifiable allegations of contact between Trump aides and Russian operatives".<ref name="Bensinger_1/10/2017" />}} memos that were considered by Steele to be "[[raw intelligence]] – not established facts, but a starting point for further investigation".<ref name="Shane_Goldman_Rosenberg_4/19/2019" /><ref name="Gross_Simpson_Fritsch_11/26/2019" /><ref name="Kessler_10/29/2017" /> The dossier was written from June to December 2016 as reports from his sources were received, and contains allegations of misconduct, conspiracy, and cooperation between Trump's presidential campaign and the government of Russia prior to and during the [[2016 United States presidential election|2016 election]] campaign.<ref name="Lucas_8/23/2017" /> U.S. intelligence agencies have reported that Putin personally ordered the whole [[Russian interference in the 2016 United States elections|Russian election interference operation]],<ref name="Barnes_Goldman_Sanger_9/9/2019" /> that the Russians codenamed ''Project Lakhta''.<ref name="Schick_2020" /><ref name="Lakhta_member_charged" /><ref name="Ross_Schwartz_Meek_12/15/2016" /><ref name="Hosenball_8/19/2020" />
While the dossier played a significant role in initially highlighting the general friendliness between Trump and the Putin administration, the [[#Corroboration status of specific allegations|corroboration status of specific allegations]] is highly variable. The following allegations have been publicly corroborated by U.S. intelligence agencies, the [[Assessing Russian Activities and Intentions in Recent US Elections|January 2017 ODNI report]],<ref name="ODNI_1/6/2017" /> and the [[Mueller report]]:<ref name="Wood_8/12/2020" /> "that the Russian government was working to get Mr. Trump elected";{{efn| name=Rosenberg}} that Russia sought "to cultivate people in Trump's orbit";{{efn| name=Rosenberg}} that Trump campaign officials and associates had [[Links between Trump associates and Russian officials|secretive contacts with Russian officials and agents]];<ref name="Yourish_Buchanan_1/26/2019" /><ref name="Leonnig_Helderman_5/17/2019" /><ref name="Sciutto_Perez_2/10/2017" /> that Putin favored Trump over [[Hillary Clinton]];<ref name="ODNI_1/6/2017" /><ref name="Levine_1/12/2018" /> that Putin personally ordered<ref name="Barnes_Goldman_Sanger_9/9/2019" /> an "influence campaign" to harm Clinton's campaign and to "undermine public faith in the US democratic process";<ref name="ODNI_1/6/2017" /> and that he ordered cyberattacks on both parties.<ref name="ODNI_1/6/2017" /> Some other allegations are plausible but not specifically confirmed,<ref name="Lee_12/26/2017" /><ref name="Farhi_11/12/2021" /> and some are dubious in retrospect but not strictly disproven.<ref name="MSNBC_5/22/2018" /><ref name="Hutzler_8//16/2018" /><ref name="Grant_Rosenberg_12/14/2018_2" />
The dossier was based on reports from initially anonymous sources known to Steele<ref name="Shane_Confessore_Rosenberg_1/12/2017" /> and his "primary sub-source", [[Igor Danchenko]].<ref name="Kranish_11/23/2021" /> Steele, a former head of the Russia Desk for [[Secret Intelligence Service|British intelligence]] (MI6), wrote the report for the private investigative firm [[Fusion GPS]], that was paid by [[Hillary Clinton 2016 presidential campaign|Hillary Clinton's campaign]] and the [[Democratic National Committee]] (DNC).<ref name="Entous_Barrett_Helderman_10/24/2017" /> The dossier's 17 reports allege that there was a "well-developed conspiracy" of "cooperation" between Trump campaign members and Russian operatives to aid Russia's election interference efforts to benefit Trump.<ref name="Sumter_11/16/2017" /> It also alleges that Russia sought to damage Hillary Clinton's candidacy.<ref name="Sipher_1/11/2018" /> It was published by ''[[BuzzFeed News]]'' on January 10, 2017, without Steele's permission.<ref name="Bensinger_1/10/2017" /> Their decision to publish the reports without verifying the allegations was criticized by journalists.<ref name="Bump_1/11/2017" /><ref name="Zurawik_1/11/2017" /> However, a judge defended ''BuzzFeed''{{'}}s action on the basis that the dossier was part of an official proceeding, and therefore "protected by [[fair comment|fair reporting]] privilege".<ref name="Johnson_12/19/2018" />
The United States intelligence community and most experts have treated the dossier with caution due to its unverified allegations.<ref name="Breuninger_1/13/2018" /> While compiling the dossier, Steele passed his findings to both British and American [[intelligence agencies]].<ref name="Levine_1/12/2018" /><ref name="Sengupta_1/13/2017" /> The U.S. intelligence community took the allegations seriously,<ref name="Chait_12/7/2017" /> and the [[Federal Bureau of Investigation]] (FBI) investigated every line of the dossier and identified and spoke with at least two of Steele's sources.<ref name="Shane_Goldman_Rosenberg_4/19/2019" /> The [[Mueller report]] contained passing references to some of the dossier's allegations but little mention of its more sensational claims.<ref name="Shane_Goldman_Rosenberg_4/19/2019" /> Both the 2019 [[Inspector General report on FBI and DOJ actions in the 2016 election|OIG report]]<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|172}} and the 2023 [[Durham special counsel investigation#Final report|Durham report]]<ref name="Durham_5/12/2023" />{{rp|99}} raised doubts about the dossier's reliability and sources, with the latter stating that "the FBI was not able to corroborate a single substantive allegation contained in the Steele Reports".<ref name="Durham_5/12/2023" />{{rp|99}}
While the dossier played a central and essential role in the seeking of [[FISA]] warrants on [[Carter Page]],<ref name="Sanchez_12/11/2019" /> according to [[James Clapper]], [[John O. Brennan|John Brennan]], and [[Robert S. Litt]], it "played no role" in the January 6, 2017, [[Russian interference in the 2016 United States elections#January 2017 Intelligence Community Assessment|intelligence community assessment]] of the Russian actions in the 2016 election,<ref name="Samuels_2/4/018" /><ref name="Bertrand_10/27/2017" /><ref name="Benner_Barnes_12/19/2019" /> and according to the Senate Intelligence Committee, the dossier was not used to "support any of [the assessment's] analytic judgments".<ref name="Jalonick_Tucker_4/21/2020" /> Also, it was not the trigger for the opening of the [[Crossfire Hurricane (FBI investigation)|Russia investigation]] into whether the Trump campaign was coordinating with the Russian government's interference in the 2016 presidential election.<ref name="Goldman_Savage_11/22/2019" /> The dossier is a factor in several [[List of conspiracy theories promoted by Donald Trump|conspiracy theories promoted by Trump]]<ref name="Kruzel_7/23/2018" /> and his supporters.<ref name="Goldman_Savage_11/22/2019" /><ref name="Rupar_03/22/2019" /> Many mainstream sources have described the dossier as "discredited".{{efn|name="discredited"}}
== History == === Two research operations and confusion between them ===
The opposition research conducted by Fusion GPS on Donald Trump occurred in two distinct phases, each with different clients: * The client for the '''first phase''' was ''The Washington Free Beacon'', a conservative news outlet, which funded research into multiple Republican candidates, including Trump. This phase, from October 2015 to May 2016, was focused on Trump's domestic business and entertainment activities; was performed by Fusion GPS and used public sources and [[Investigative journalism|investigative reporter]] [[Wayne Barrett]]'s files. : Immediately after the publication of the dossier, the media sometimes falsely assumed that the dossier started as a product of this research, so the ''Free Beacon'' released this statement: "none of the work product that the Free Beacon received appears in the Steele dossier".<ref name="Leary_10/28/2017" /><ref name="Goldfarb_12/12/2017">{{cite web |author=Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence, U.S. House of Representatives |date=December 12, 2017 |title=Interview of Michael Goldfarb, December 12, 2017 | website=[[Office of the Director of National Intelligence]] |url=https://www.odni.gov/files/HPSCI_Transcripts/2019-05-01-MichaelGo-MTR.pdf |access-date=March 27, 2023 |pages=39, 44|quote=I've read the dossier. I feel very confident that no material that was produced and delivered to us appears in that dossier. It was all new information to me when I read it. And beyond that, I think the dossier itself makes pretty clear that the information was gathered after the time that we ceased working with Fusion on matters related to Donald Trump, to my recollection. There's markers in the dossier about when meetings happened, and when information was gathered and this kind of thing that post-dates our Trump research. And I personally see zero overlap in the work product. ... In my view, I feel confident in saying that there is no overlap between the work provided to us and the work that appears in that document.}} [https://d3i6fh83elv35t.cloudfront.net/static/2020/05/mg17.pdf Searchable version]</ref> * The clients for the '''second phase''' were the Democratic Party and Clinton campaign. This phase, from April to December 2016, was focused on Trump's Russian connections; it was subcontracted by Fusion GPS to Steele and Orbis Business Intelligence and used Steele's own source network and public sources. Only this second phase produced the dossier.<ref name="Borger_1/11/2017" /><ref name="Lima_10/27/2017">{{cite web |last=Lima |first=Cristiano |title=Conservative Free Beacon originally funded firm that created Trump–Russia dossier |newspaper=[[Politico]] |date=October 27, 2017 |url=https://www.politico.com/story/2017/10/27/trump-russia-washington-free-beacon-fusion-gps-244265 |access-date=February 11, 2018}}</ref>
From April to early May 2016, ''The Washington Free Beacon'' and the DNC/Clinton Campaign were independently both clients of Fusion GPS. This overlap contributed to the media's confusion.
==== ''The Washington Free Beacon'' operation does not produce the dossier ====
In October 2015, before the official start of the [[2016 Republican Party presidential primaries|2016 Republican primary campaign]], the founders of Fusion GPS were seeking political work and wrote an email to "a big conservative donor they knew who disliked Trump, [and] they were hired". He arranged for them to use ''The Washington Free Beacon'', an American [[Conservatism in the United States|conservative]] political journalism website, for their general opposition research on several Republican presidential candidates, including Trump.<ref name="Mayer_11/25/2019">{{cite magazine |last=Mayer |first=Jane |author-link=Jane Mayer |title=The Inside Story of Christopher Steele's Trump Dossier |magazine=[[The New Yorker]] |date=November 25, 2019 |url=https://www.newyorker.com/news/our-columnists/the-inside-story-of-christopher-steeles-trump-dossier |access-date=November 27, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Vogel_Haberman_10/27/2017" /> It is primarily funded by Republican donor [[Paul Singer (businessman)|Paul Singer]].<ref name="Vogel_Haberman_10/27/2017">{{cite news |first1=Kenneth P. |last1=Vogel |author-link1=Kenneth P. Vogel |first2=Maggie |last2=Haberman |author-link2=Maggie Haberman |title=Conservative Website First Funded Anti-Trump Research by Firm That Later Produced Dossier |date=October 27, 2017 |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/10/27/us/politics/trump-dossier-paul-singer.html |access-date=April 1, 2018}}</ref> The ''Free Beacon'' and Singer were "part of the conservative [[Stop Trump movement|never-Trump movement]]".<ref name="Isikoff_Corn_3/17/2018" /> Although Singer was a big supporter of [[Marco Rubio]], Rubio denied any involvement in Fusion GPS's initial research and hiring.<ref name="Leary_10/28/2017" />
This phase used public sources, but early in their investigation, they also received help from Wayne Barrett who gave them his files on Trump. They contained findings about "Trump's past dealings, including tax and bankruptcy problems, potential ties to organized crime, and numerous legal entanglements. They also revealed that Trump had an unusually high number of connections to Russians with questionable backgrounds."<ref name="Mayer_11/25/2019" />
For months, Fusion GPS gathered information about Trump, focusing on his business and entertainment activities. When Trump became the presumptive nominee on May 3, 2016,<ref name="Johnson_5/4/2016">{{cite news |last=Johnson |first=Jenna |title=The night Donald Trump became the presumptive nominee |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=May 4, 2016 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/post-politics/wp/2016/05/04/the-night-donald-trump-became-the-presumptive-nominee/ |access-date=April 10, 2018}}</ref> the conservative donor stopped funding the research on him.<ref name="Shane_Confessore_Rosenberg_1/12/2017">{{cite news |last1=Shane |first1=Scott |author-link1=Scott Shane |last2=Confessore |first2=Nicholas |author-link2=Nicholas Confessore |last3=Rosenberg |first3=Matthew |author-link3=Matthew Rosenberg |title=How a Sensational, Unverified Dossier Became a Crisis for Donald Trump |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=January 12, 2017 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/01/11/us/politics/donald-trump-russia-intelligence.html |access-date=February 11, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Borger_1/12/2017">{{cite news |last=Borger |first=Julian |title=How the Trump dossier came to light: secret sources, a retired spy and John McCain |newspaper=[[The Guardian]] |date=January 12, 2017 |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2017/jan/11/trump-russia-report-opposition-research-john-mccain |access-date=February 11, 2018}}</ref>
Due to media confusion over who produced the dossier, the ''Free Beacon'' issued a statement in October 2017 to debunk some false assumptions:<ref name="Leary_10/28/2017">{{cite news |last=Leary |first=Alex |title=Marco Rubio backer's money initially funded anti-Trump research |newspaper=[[Tampa Bay Times]] |date=October 28, 2017 |url=https://www.tampabay.com/florida-politics/buzz/2017/10/28/marco-rubio-backers-money-initially-funded-anti-trump-research/ |access-date=September 16, 2021}}</ref> {{blockquote| All of the work that Fusion GPS provided to the Free Beacon was based on public sources, and none of the work product that the Free Beacon received appears in the Steele dossier. The Free Beacon had no knowledge of or connection to the Steele dossier, did not pay for the dossier, and never had contact with, knowledge of, or provided payment for any work performed by Christopher Steele. Nor did we have any knowledge of the relationship between Fusion GPS and the Democratic National Committee, Perkins Coie, and the Clinton campaign.}}
Although the source of the Steele dossier's funding had already been reported correctly over a year before,<ref name="Shane_Confessore_Rosenberg_1/12/2017" /><ref name="Borger_1/12/2017" /><ref name="Robertson_2/7/2018">{{cite web |last=Robertson |first=Lori |title=Q&A on the Nunes Memo | website=[[FactCheck.org]] |date=February 7, 2018 |url=https://www.factcheck.org/2018/02/qa-nunes-memo/ |access-date=February 12, 2018 |quote="There is a Russia investigation without a dossier," Gowdy said. "So to the extent the memo deals with the dossier and the FISA process, the dossier has nothing to do with the meeting at Trump Tower. The dossier has nothing to do with an email sent by Cambridge Analytica. The dossier really has nothing to do with George Papadopoulos' meeting in Great Britain. It also doesn't have anything to do with obstruction of justice. So there's going to be a Russia probe, even without a dossier."}}</ref> and the ''Free Beacon'' had issued a statement to this effect in October 2017,<ref name="Leary_10/28/2017" /> a February 2, 2018, story by the [[Associated Press]] (AP) contributed to confusion about its funding by stating that the dossier "was initially funded" by ''The Washington Free Beacon'', so the AP posted a correction the next day: "Though the former spy, Christopher Steele, was hired by a firm that was initially funded by the Washington Free Beacon, he did not begin work on the project until after Democratic groups had begun funding it."<ref name="Tucker_Jalonick_Day_2/3/2018_correction">{{cite news |last1=Tucker |first1=Eric |last2=Jalonick |first2=Clare |last3=Day |first3=Chad |title=Correction: Trump–Russia Probe story |date=February 3, 2018 |agency=[[Associated Press]] |url=https://www.apnews.com/63c883156e314b68b86209d3b63890f5 |access-date=April 15, 2018}}</ref>
By the spring of 2016, researchers at Fusion GPS had become so alarmed by what they had already learned about Trump that they felt the need "to do what they could to keep Trump out of the White House".<ref name="Nicholas_11/21/2019" />
==== Democratic Party operation produces dossier ==== ===== Hiring and initial reports =====
The second operation of opposition research was indirectly funded by the Democratic National Committee and the Clinton campaign, working through their attorney of record, [[Marc Elias]] of [[Perkins Coie]].<ref name="Entous_Barrett_Helderman_10/24/2017">{{cite news |last1=Entous |first1=Adam |last2=Barrett |first2=Devlin |last3=Helderman |first3=Rosalind |author3-link=Rosalind S. Helderman |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/national-security/clinton-campaign-dnc-paid-for-research-that-led-to-russia-dossier/2017/10/24/226fabf0-b8e4-11e7-a908-a3470754bbb9_story.html |title=Clinton campaign, DNC paid for research that led to Russia dossier |date=October 24, 2017 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |access-date=October 25, 2017}}</ref> In an October 2017 letter, Perkins Coie general counsel Matthew Gehringer described how, in March 2016, Fusion GPS approached Perkins Coie and, knowing the Clinton campaign and the DNC were its clients, inquired whether its clients wished to pay Fusion GPS "to continue research regarding then-presidential candidate Donald Trump, research that Fusion GPS had conducted for one or more other clients during the Republican primary contest."<ref name="Watkins_Merica_Polantz_10/25/2017">{{cite news |last1=Watkins |first1=Eli |last2=Merica |first2=Dan |last3=Polantz |first3=Katelyn |title=Clinton campaign, DNC helped fund dossier research |work=[[CNN]] |date=October 25, 2017 |url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/10/24/politics/fusion-gps-clinton-campaign/index.html |access-date=September 9, 2018}}</ref> In April 2016, Elias hired Fusion GPS to perform opposition research on Trump.<ref name="Entous_Barrett_Helderman_10/24/2017" /><ref name="Watkins_Merica_Polantz_10/25/2017" />
Fusion GPS co-founder [[Glenn R. Simpson]] had some reservations, as he did not like the idea of helping Hillary Clinton. In an email, Simpson said, "The only way I could see working for HRC is if it is against Trump."<ref name="Walsh_11/22/2019">{{cite magazine |last=Walsh |first=James D. |title=Fusion GPS Lights a Candle for the 'Pee Tape' |magazine=[[New York (magazine)|New York]] |date=November 22, 2019 |url=https://nymag.com/intelligencer/2019/11/fusion-gps-interview-glenn-simpson-on-the-steele-dossier.html |access-date=November 24, 2019}}</ref>
In June 2016,<ref name="Shane_Confessore_Rosenberg_1/12/2017" /> as part of its work for Perkins Coie, Fusion GPS hired [[Orbis Business Intelligence]], a private British intelligence firm, to look into connections between Trump and Russia. Orbis co-founder [[Christopher Steele]], a retired British [[Secret Intelligence Service|MI6]] officer with expertise in Russian matters,<ref name="Shane_Confessore_Rosenberg_1/12/2017" /> was hired as a subcontractor to do the job.<ref name="Johnson_Kelly_1/9/2018">{{cite news |last1=Johnson |first1=Kevin |last2=Kelly |first2=Erin |title=Dossier author was told FBI had a source inside Trump Organization |newspaper=[[USA Today]] |date=January 9, 2018 |url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/2018/01/09/dossier-author-told-fbi-had-source-inside-trump-organization/1017938001/ |access-date=January 10, 2018}}</ref> Prior to and during some of his work on the dossier, Steele had been a paid [[confidential human source]] (CHS) for the [[Federal Bureau of Investigation|FBI]]<ref name="Winter_8/3/2018">{{cite news |last=Winter |first=Tom |title=FBI releases documents showing payments to Trump dossier author Steele |work=[[NBC News]] |date=August 3, 2018 |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/news/crime-courts/fbi-releases-documents-showing-payments-trump-dossier-author-steele-n897506 |access-date=April 18, 2019}}</ref> for information unrelated to the Russia investigation.<ref name="Philips_2/24/2018">{{cite news |last1=Philips |first1=Amber |title=Read the Democratic rebuttal to the Nunes memo, annotated |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/the-fix/wp/2018/02/24/read-the-democratic-rebuttal-to-the-nunes-memo-annotated/ |date=February 24, 2018 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |access-date=April 3, 2019}}</ref>
The DNC and Clinton campaign treasurers reported they paid Perkins Coie a total of $12.4 million for legal and compliance services during the 2016 campaign.<ref name="Johnson_9/10/2018">{{cite magazine |last=Johnson |first=Ted |title=Omarosa Reveals New Recording of Trump Blaming Hillary Clinton for Collusion |magazine=[[Variety (magazine)|Variety]] |date=September 10, 2018 |url=https://variety.com/2018/politics/news/omarosa-trump-the-view-1202934826/ |access-date=September 11, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Ballhaus_10/25/2017">{{cite news |last=Ballhaus |first=Rebecca |author-link=Rebecca Ballhaus |title=Clinton Campaign, DNC Helped Fund Trump–Russia Dossier |newspaper=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |date=October 25, 2017 |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/clinton-campaign-dnc-helped-fund-trump-russia-dossier-1508942615 |access-date=December 9, 2018}}</ref> This led Trump to claim the dossier had cost $12 million,<ref name="Hosenball_11/1/2017" /> although the actual cost was far less.<ref name="Price_11/2/2017">{{cite news |last=Price |first=Greg |title=Trump's Golden Shower Dossier Cost Nowhere Near as Much as He Claimed on Twitter |date=November 2, 2017 |work=[[Newsweek]] |url=https://www.newsweek.com/trump-dossier-cost-millions-699816 |access-date=September 9, 2018}}</ref> According to Fusion GPS, Perkins Coie paid them $1.02 million in fees and expenses, and Fusion GPS paid $168,000 to Steele's firm, Orbis Business Intelligence, to produce the dossier.<ref name="Hosenball_11/1/2017">{{cite news |last=Hosenball |first=Mark |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-trump-russia-dossier/ex-british-spy-paid-168000-for-trump-dossier-u-s-firm-discloses-idUSKBN1D15XH |title=Ex-British spy paid $168,000 for Trump dossier, U.S. firm discloses |date=November 1, 2017 |work=[[Reuters]] |access-date=November 7, 2017}}</ref><ref name="Khan_11/2/2017">{{cite web |last=Khan |first=Shehab |title=Former British spy paid more than £125,000 to detail Donald Trump Russia links |work=[[MSN]] |date=November 2, 2017 |url=https://www.msn.com/en-gb/news/world/former-british-spy-paid-more-than-%C2%A3125000-to-detail-donald-trump-russia-links/ar-AAukWi7 |access-date=September 9, 2018}}</ref> Despite that, Trump and his son Donald Trump Jr. continued to claim for more than a year that Steele was paid "millions of dollars" for his work.<ref name="Edelman_10/12/2018">{{cite news |last=Edelman |first=Adam |title=Clinton strikes back at Trump for saying she colluded with Russia |work=[[NBC News]] |date=October 12, 2018 |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/politics/hillary-clinton/clinton-strikes-back-trump-after-he-charged-she-colluded-russia-n919566 |access-date=October 13, 2018}}</ref>
Orbis was hired between June and November 2016, and Steele produced 16 reports during that time, with a 17th report added in December.<ref name="Borger_4/28/2017" /> The reports were like "prepublication notes" based on information from Steele's sources and were not released as a fully [[vetting|vetted]] and "finished news article".<ref name="Flegenheimer_1/8/2018">{{cite news |last=Flegenheimer |first=Matt |title=Fusion GPS Founder Hauled From the Shadows for the Russia Election Investigation |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=January 8, 2018 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/01/08/us/politics/fusion-gps-glenn-simpson.html |access-date=March 25, 2018}}</ref> Steele believes 70–90 percent of the dossier is accurate,<ref name="Borger_11/15/2017" /> a view shared by Simpson.<ref name="Flegenheimer_1/8/2018" /> In litigation, it was revealed that:<ref name="Gubarev_Buzzfeed_1:17-cv-60426-UU">{{Cite web |title=Gubarev v. Buzzfeed, Inc., 340 F. Supp. 3d 1304, Case No. 1:17-cv-60426-UU |url=https://casetext.com/case/gubarev-v-buzzfeed-inc-2 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220402015031/https://casetext.com/case/gubarev-v-buzzfeed-inc-2 |url-status=dead |archive-date=April 2, 2022 |access-date=August 7, 2024 |website=casetext.com}}</ref> {{blockquote| Steele took the information he found credible and wrote a total of seventeen reports, which became the Dossier. Id. ¶ 9. In deciding what he found credible, Steele weighed the possibility that his sources might try to provide false information.}}
Steele delivered his reports individually to Fusion GPS as one- to three-page numbered reports.<ref name="Shane_Confessore_Rosenberg_1/12/2017" /> The first report, dated June 20, 2016, was sent to Washington by courier and hand-delivered to Fusion GPS. The names of the sources were redacted and replaced with descriptions to help Fusion GPS judge their credibility.<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018">{{cite magazine |last=Mayer |first=Jane |author-link=Jane Mayer |title=Christopher Steele, the Man Behind the Trump Dossier |magazine=[[The New Yorker]] |date=March 12, 2018 |url=https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2018/03/12/christopher-steele-the-man-behind-the-trump-dossier |access-date=March 6, 2018}}</ref>
Luke Harding wrote:<ref name="Harding_11/15/2017" /> {{blockquote| At first, obtaining intelligence from Moscow went well. For around six months—during the first half of the year—Steele was able to make inquiries in Russia with relative ease. It got harder from late July, as Trump's ties to Russia came under scrutiny. Finally, the lights went out. Amid a Kremlin cover-up, the sources went silent and information channels shut down.}}
===== Passing reports to intelligence agencies =====
Steele has said he soon found "troubling information indicating connections between Trump and the Russian government". According to his sources, "there was an established exchange of information between the Trump campaign and the Kremlin of mutual benefit".<ref name="Corn_10/31/2016" /> According to Harding, "Steele was shocked by the extent of collusion his sources were reporting", and told his friends: "For anyone who reads it, this is a life-changing experience."<ref name="Borger_11/15/2017">{{cite news |last=Borger |first=Julian |title=Christopher Steele believes his dossier on Trump–Russia is 70–90% accurate |newspaper=[[The Guardian]] |date=November 15, 2017 |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2017/nov/15/christopher-steele-trump-russia-dossier-accurate |access-date=January 22, 2018}}</ref> Steele felt what he had unearthed "was something of huge significance, way above party politics".<ref name="Blum_3/30/2017">{{cite web |last=Blum |first=Howard |title=How Ex-Spy Christopher Steele Compiled His Explosive Trump–Russia Dossier | website=[[Vanity Fair (magazine)|Vanity Fair]] |date=March 30, 2017 |url=https://www.vanityfair.com/news/2017/03/how-the-explosive-russian-dossier-was-compiled-christopher-steele |access-date=December 24, 2017}}</ref> American reporter [[Howard Blum]] described Steele's rationale for becoming a [[whistleblower]]: "The greater good trumps all other concerns."<ref name="Blum_3/30/2017" />
On his own initiative, Steele decided to also pass the information to British and American intelligence services because he believed the findings were a matter of [[national security]] for both countries.<ref name="Levine_1/12/2018" /><ref name="Sengupta_1/13/2017">{{cite news |last=Sengupta |first=Kim |title=Ex-MI6 agent so worried by his Donald Trump discoveries he started working without pay |newspaper=[[The Independent]] |date=January 13, 2017 |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/donald-trump-russia-dossier-file-investigation-hacking-christopher-steele-mi6-a7526901.html |access-date=March 3, 2018}}</ref> In 2018, Steele told a UK parliamentary investigation that [[Theresa May]]'s British government covered up the evidence he provided them of Trump's Russian ties and took no actions, and that [[Boris Johnson]] suppressed a report about the intelligence in the dossier that was prepared by Parliament's [[Intelligence and Security Committee of Parliament|Intelligence and Security Committee]].<ref name="Sabbagh_6/22/2020">{{cite news |last=Sabbagh |first=Dan |title=Johnson and May ignored claims Russia had 'likely hold' over Trump, ex-spy alleges |newspaper=[[The Guardian]] |date=June 22, 2020 |url=http://www.theguardian.com/world/2020/jun/22/boris-johnson-theres-may-ignored-claims-russia-had-likely-hold-over-donald-trump-ex-spy-christopher-steele-claims |access-date=September 30, 2021}}</ref> After long delay, the report was published on July 21, 2020.<ref name="Sabbagh_7/21/2020">{{cite news |last=Sabbagh |first=Dan |title=Russia report reveals UK government failed to investigate Kremlin interference |newspaper=[[The Guardian]] |date=July 21, 2020 |url=http://www.theguardian.com/world/2020/jul/21/russia-report-reveals-uk-government-failed-to-address-kremlin-interference-scottish-referendum-brexit |access-date=September 30, 2021}}</ref>
According to Simpson's testimony, Steele, who enjoyed a good working reputation "for the knowledge he had developed over nearly 20 years working on Russia-related issues for [[British intelligence]],<ref name="Hamburger_2/28/2017" /> approached the [[FBI]] because he was concerned that Trump, then a candidate, was being [[blackmail]]ed by Russia,<ref name="Herb_Raju_Cohen_1/10/2018">{{cite news |last1=Herb |first1=Jeremy |last2=Raju |first2=Manu |last3=Cohen |first3=Marshall |date=January 10, 2018 |url=https://www.cnn.com/2018/01/09/politics/feinstein-releases-glenn-simpson-transcript/index.html |title=Fusion co-founder: Dossier author feared Trump was being blackmailed |work=[[CNN]] |access-date=January 21, 2018}}</ref> and he became "very concerned about whether this represented a national security threat".<ref name="Levine_1/12/2018" /> Steele believed the intelligence community "needed urgently to know—if it didn't already—that the next possible U.S. president was potentially under the sway of Russia".<ref name="Simpson_Fritsch_11/26/2019">{{cite book |last1=Simpson |first1=Glenn |last2=Fritsch |first2=Peter |title=Crime in Progress: Inside the Steele Dossier and the Fusion GPS Investigation of Donald Trump |publisher=[[Random House]] |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-593-13416-0}}</ref>
In early July 2016, Steele called seasoned FBI agent Michael Gaeta, who was stationed in Rome, and asked him to come to London so he could show him his findings. Because he was assigned to the U.S. embassy in Rome, Gaeta sought and was granted approval for the trip from [[Victoria Nuland]], who was then the Assistant Secretary of State for European and Eurasian Affairs. When he arrived in London on July 5, 2016, he met with Steele at his office,<ref name="Herb_Raju_Cohen_1/10/2018" /> and he was given a copy of Steele's first report, dated June 20, 2016 (Report 80).<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|95}} His reaction was "shock and horror".<ref name="Kessler_1/9/2018" /><ref name="Herb_Raju_Cohen_1/10/2018" /> Alarmed by what he read, Gaeta remarked, "I have to show this to headquarters".<ref name="Roulette">{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Tzc3DwAAQBAJ |title=Russian Roulette: The Inside Story of Putin's War on America and the Election of Donald Trump |last1=Isikoff |first1=Michael |author-link1=Michael Isikoff |last2=Corn |first2=David |author-link2=David Corn |year=2018 |publisher=[[Grand Central Publishing]] |isbn=978-1-5387-2874-1}}</ref>
Shortly after, in July, the report was sent to an agent with expertise in criminal organizations and organized crime at the FBI's New York field office—essentially, the wrong person to handle a counterintelligence investigation.<ref name="Levine_9/18/2018">{{cite news |last=Levine |first=Mike |title=Trump 'dossier' stuck in New York, didn't trigger Russia investigation, sources say |work=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] |date=September 18, 2018 |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/trump-dossier-stuck-york-trigger-russia-investigation-sources/story?id=57919471 |access-date=January 13, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Roulette" /> According to Nancy LeTourneau, political writer for the ''[[Washington Monthly]]'', the report "was languishing in the FBI's New York field office" for two months, and "was finally sent to the counterintelligence team investigating Russia at FBI headquarters in Washington, D.C." in mid-September 2016.<ref name="LeTourneau_9/19/2018" /><ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" />{{rp|897}}
In August 2016, the FBI asked Steele for "all information in his possession and for him to explain how the material had been gathered and to identify his sources".<ref name="Harding_11/15/2017" /><ref name="Corn_10/31/2016" /> In October 2016, Steele "described the sources' access, but did not provide names" to the FBI.<ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" />{{rp|897}} By August 22, 2017, Steele had provided them with the names of the sources for the allegations in the dossier.<ref name="Ross_Mosk_Schwartz_8/22/2017">{{cite news |last1=Ross |first1=Brian |last2=Mosk |first2=Matthew |last3=Schwartz |first3=Rhonda |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/glenn-simpson-key-figure-million-dollar-dossier-face/story?id=49348102 |title=Glenn Simpson key figure behind million-dollar dossier to face questions |date=August 22, 2017 |work=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] |access-date=August 23, 2017}}</ref>
Meanwhile, in the July to September time frame, according to ''The Washington Post'', [[Central Intelligence Agency|CIA]] Director [[John O. Brennan|John Brennan]] had started an investigation with a secret task force "composed of several dozen analysts and officers from the [[CIA]], the [[National Security Agency|NSA]] and the FBI". At the same time, he was busy creating his own dossier of material documenting that "Russia was not only attempting to interfere in the 2016 election, they were doing so in order to elect Donald Trump. ... [T]he entire intelligence community was on alert about this situation at least two months before [the dossier] became part of the investigation." The "Steele dossier has so far proven to be fairly accurate", LeTourneau wrote.<ref name="LeTourneau_9/19/2018">{{cite magazine |url=https://washingtonmonthly.com/2018/09/19/trump-should-be-more-worried-about-the-brennan-dossier/ |title=Trump should be more worried about the Brennan dossier |last=LeTourneau |first=Nancy |date=September 19, 2018 |magazine=[[Washington Monthly]] |access-date=September 22, 2018}}</ref>
In early August, which was after the Crossfire Hurricane investigation was opened,<ref name="Scheuermann_Schmitt_2/7/2019" /> Steele was summoned to Rome where he gave a full briefing to four American FBI officials about the report.<ref name="Hamburger_Helderman_2/6/2018" /><ref name="Kessler_1/9/2018">{{cite news |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/fact-checker/wp/2018/01/09/what-you-need-to-know-about-christopher-steele-the-fbi-and-the-dossier/ |title=What you need to know about Christopher Steele, the FBI and the Trump 'dossier' |last=Kessler |first=Glenn |author-link=Glenn Kessler (journalist) |date=January 9, 2018 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |access-date=January 22, 2018}}</ref> At that time, he handed over the June 20 and July 26 reports.<ref name="Scheuermann_Schmitt_2/7/2019" />
During its intense questioning of Steele, the FBI mentioned their own discoveries of connections between the Trump campaign and Russia<ref name="Hamburger_Helderman_2/6/2018" /> and asked Steele about [[George Papadopoulos|Papadopoulos]], but he said he knew nothing about him.<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" /> The agents "raised the prospect of paying Steele to continue gathering intelligence after Election Day",<ref name="Hamburger_Helderman_2/6/2018" /> but Steele "ultimately never received payment from the FBI for any 'dossier'-related information".<ref name="Philips_2/24/2018" /> In October 2022, during questioning from Special Counsel [[John Durham]], Brian Auten, a supervisory counterintelligence analyst with the FBI, testified that, shortly before the 2016 election, the FBI offered Steele "up to $1 million" if he could corroborate allegations in the dossier, but that Steele could not do so.<ref name="Cohen_10/11/2022">{{cite news | last=Cohen | first=Marshall | title=FBI offered Christopher Steele $1 million to prove dossier claims, senior FBI analyst testifies | date=October 11, 2022 | url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/10/11/politics/steele-dossier-fbi-durham-danchenko/index.html | access-date=October 13, 2022}}</ref><ref name="Rizzo_10/12/2022">{{cite news |last=Rizzo |first=Salvador |title=FBI supervisor describes learning he was in crosshairs of Durham probe |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/national-security/2022/10/12/igor-danchenko-durham-trial-auten/ |date=October 12, 2022 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |access-date=October 13, 2022}}</ref> Steele has disputed this description: "And to correct the Danchenko trial record, we were not offered $1 million by the FBI to 'prove up' our Trump-Russia reporting. Rather, we were told there were substantial funds to resettle sources in the US if they were prepared to testify in public. Understandably they were not."<ref name="Steele_10/19/2022">{{cite tweet | user=Chris_D_Steele | last=Steele | first=Christopher | author-link=Christopher Steele | title=Steele tweet | date=October 19, 2022 | number=1582861423742713856 | via=[[Twitter]] | access-date=October 29, 2022}}</ref>
===== After public release =====
The subsequent public release of the dossier in January 2017 stopped discussions between Steele and the FBI.<ref name="Hamburger_2/28/2017">{{cite news |last1=Hamburger |first1=Tom |author-link1=Tom Hamburger |last2=Helderman |first2=Rosalind S. |author-link2=Rosalind S. Helderman |title=FBI once planned to pay former British spy who authored controversial Trump dossier |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/fbi-once-planned-to-pay-former-british-spy-who-authored-controversial-trump-dossier/2017/02/28/896ab470-facc-11e6-9845-576c69081518_story.html |date=February 28, 2017 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |access-date=March 1, 2017}}</ref> The Inspector General's report later confirmed that the FBI had initially offered to pay Steele $15,000 for his trip to Rome, but when the FBI dropped Steele as a CHS because he had shared information with a third party "in late October 2016" (''[[Mother Jones (magazine)|Mother Jones]]'' magazine), the payment was halted.<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|173}}<ref name="Robertson_2/7/2018" /> [[Peter Strzok]] reported that:<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|173}} {{blockquote| the FBI closed Steele "because he was a control problem. We did not close him because we thought he was [a] fabricator." According to Strzok, Steele's decisions to discuss his reporting with the media and to disclose his relationship with the FBI were "horrible and it hurt what we were doing, and no question, he shouldn't have done it."}}
In September, Steele met with Jonathan Winer, who was then the U.S. deputy assistant secretary of state for international law enforcement, whom he had known since 2009. In a 2018 editorial for ''The Washington Post'',<ref name="Winer_2/9/2018">{{cite news |last=Winer |first=Jonathan M. |title=Devin Nunes is investigating me. Here's the truth. |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=February 9, 2018 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/devin-nunes-is-investigating-me-heres-the-truth/2018/02/08/cc621170-0cf4-11e8-8b0d-891602206fb7_story.html |access-date=January 13, 2019}}</ref> Winer recounted that during their meeting in Washington, he was allowed to review Steele's reports, but not to keep a copy: "I prepared a two-page summary and shared it with [Victoria] Nuland, who indicated that, like me, she felt that the secretary of state needed to be made aware of this material", he wrote. Later in September, Winer discussed the report with [[Sidney Blumenthal]], who revealed he had received similar information from Cody Shearer, a controversial political activist and former journalist who was close to the Clinton White House in the 1990s. Winer met with Steele again in late September and gave him a copy of Shearer's report, later known as the "second dossier".<ref>Multiple sources: * {{cite news |last1=Siegel |first1=Benjamin |last2=Karl |first2=Jonathan |last3=Turner |first3=Trish |title=Congressional Russia investigators interested in 2nd Trump–Russia dossier |work=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] |date=February 9, 2018 |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/congressional-russia-investigators-interested-2nd-dossier-trump-russia/story?id=52931740 |access-date=March 7, 2019 |ref=none}} * {{cite web |last=Ward |first=Alex |title=There's a second Trump–Russia dossier | website=[[Vox (website)|Vox]] |date=January 30, 2018 |url=https://www.vox.com/world/2018/1/30/16951490/trump-russia-dossier-cody-shearer-memo-nunes |access-date=March 7, 2019 |ref=none}} * {{cite news |last1=Herb |first1=Jeremy |last2=Borger |first2=Gloria |last3=Gaouette |first3=Nicole |title=GOP puts Sidney Blumenthal in spotlight |work=[[CNN]] |date=February 7, 2018 |url=https://www.cnn.com/2018/02/07/politics/republicans-sidney-blumenthal-russia-dossier/index.html |access-date=March 7, 2019 |ref=none}}</ref>
On September 19, 2016,{{efn|name="9/19/2016"}} after the report had been "languishing in the FBI's New York field office" for two months,<ref name="LeTourneau_9/19/2018" /><ref name="Levine_9/18/2018" /> Steele's handling agent sent six of Steele's reports (80, 94, 95, 100, 101, and 102) to the [[Operation Crossfire Hurricane|Crossfire Hurricane]] team, that had been operational since July 31, 2016. This was the first time{{efn|name="9/19/2016"}} they and their leader, Deputy Assistant Director of the [[FBI Counterintelligence Division#Organization|FBI's Counterespionage Section]], [[Peter Strzok]], received any of Steele's reporting.<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|100}}{{efn|name="9/19/2016"|"On September 19, 2016, the Crossfire Hurricane team received the Steele reporting for the first time when Handling Agent 1 emailed SSA 1 six reports for SSA 1 to upload himself to the sub-file: Reports 80 and 94, and four additional reports (Reports 95, 100, 101, and 102) that Handling Agent 1 had since received from Steele. FBI officials we interviewed told us that the length of time it took for Steele's election reporting to reach FBI Headquarters was excessive and that the reports should have been sent promptly after their receipt by the Legat. Members of the Crossfire Hurricane team told us that their assessment of the Steele election reporting could have started much earlier if the reporting had been made available to them."<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|100}}}}<ref name="Levine_8/29/2018">{{cite news |last=Levine |first=Mike |title=The Russia probe: A timeline from Moscow to Mueller |work=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] |date=August 29, 2018 |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/russia-probe-timeline-moscow-mueller/story?id=57427441 |access-date=August 30, 2018}}</ref><ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" />{{rp|896}} Strzok cautioned his colleagues: "[The dossier] 'should be viewed as intended to influence as well as to inform' and whoever commissioned it was 'presumed to be connected to the [Clinton] campaign in some way.{{'"}}<ref name="Savage_5/17/2023">{{cite web | last=Savage | first=Charlie |author-link=Charlie Savage (author) | title=Analysis: Durham Report Failed to Deliver After Years of Political Hype | website=[[The New York Times]] | date=May 17, 2023 | url=https://www.nytimes.com/2023/05/17/us/politics/durham-report-trump-russia.html | access-date=May 19, 2023}}</ref> Some reports referred to members of Trump's inner circle. After that point, he continued to share information with the FBI.<ref name="Corn_10/31/2016" /><ref name="Harding_11/15/2017" /> The IG Report says this material "became an important part of the Crossfire Hurricane investigation and the FBI seeking FISA authority targeting one of the Crossfire Hurricane subjects, Carter Page".<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|80}}
On October 28, 2016, days before the election, Comey notified Congress that the FBI had started looking into newly discovered Hillary Clinton emails. Simpson and Fritsch described their reaction: "Comey's bombshell prompted the Fusion partners to decide they needed to do what they could to expose the FBI's probe of Trump and Russia. It was Hail Mary time."<ref name="Simpson_Fritsch_11/26/2019" /> The founders of Fusion GPS were very upset by a misleading November 1, 2016, ''New York Times'' article "published a week before the election with the headline: 'Investigating Donald Trump, FBI Sees No Clear Link to Russia'. In fact, Russia ''was'' [[Russian interference in the 2016 United States elections|meddling in the election]] to help Trump win, the U.S. intelligence community would later conclude."<ref name="Nicholas_11/21/2019" />
Simpson later said that "Steele severed his contacts with [the] FBI before the election following public statements by the FBI that it had found no connection between the Trump campaign and Russia and concerns that [the FBI] was being 'manipulated for political ends by the Trump people'."<ref name="Jalonick_1/9/2018">{{cite news |last=Jalonick |first=Mary |title=Democratic report warns of Russian meddling in Europe, US |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=January 9, 2018 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/federal_government/author-of-trump-dossier-had-concerns-about-russian-blackmail/2018/01/09/a893fce2-f5a0-11e7-9af7-a50bc3300042_story.html |access-date=January 22, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180123072443/https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/federal_government/author-of-trump-dossier-had-concerns-about-russian-blackmail/2018/01/09/a893fce2-f5a0-11e7-9af7-a50bc3300042_story.html |archive-date=January 23, 2018 |url-status=dead}}</ref> Steele had become frustrated with the FBI, whom he believed failed to investigate his reports, choosing instead to focus on the [[Hillary Clinton email controversy|investigation into Clinton's emails]]. According to ''[[The Independent]]'', Steele came to believe there was a "[[cabal]]" inside the FBI, particularly its New York field office linked to Trump advisor [[Rudy Giuliani]], because it blocked any attempts to investigate the links between Trump and Russia.<ref name="Sengupta_1/13/2017" /> He "grew concerned that there was a cover-up in progress".<ref name="Borger_1/12/2017" />
=== What the DNC, Clinton campaign, and Steele knew ===
According to Fusion GPS's co-owners, Glenn Simpson and Peter Fritsch, they did not tell Steele who their ultimate clients were, only that Steele was "working for a law firm",<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" /> and they "gave him no specific marching orders beyond this basic question: 'Why did Mr. Trump repeatedly seek to do deals in a notoriously corrupt police state that most serious investors shun?{{'"}}<ref name="Simpson_Fritsch_1/2/2018" /> In testimony to the [[United States Senate Select Committee on Intelligence|Senate Intelligence Committee]], Simpson said that "it was Fusion GPS's idea to pursue overseas ties—that research was not directed by Perkins Coie, the DNC, or the Clinton Campaign".<ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020">{{cite book | title=Report ... on Russian Active Measures Campaigns and Interference in the 2016 U.S. Election | date=August 18, 2020 | volume=5: Counterintelligence Threats and Vulnerabilities | publisher=Senate Intelligence Committee | url=https://www.intelligence.senate.gov/sites/default/files/documents/report_volume5.pdf | access-date=December 27, 2023 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210122003727/https://www.intelligence.senate.gov/sites/default/files/documents/report_volume5.pdf | archive-date=January 22, 2021 | url-status=live}} See also the article [[Senate Intelligence Committee report on Russian interference in the 2016 United States presidential election]].</ref>{{rp|847}}
Jane Mayer reported that when the Clinton campaign "indirectly employed" Steele, Elias created a "legal barrier" by acting as a "firewall" between the campaign and Steele. Thus, any details were protected by [[attorney–client privilege]]<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" /> and [[Work-product doctrine|work-product privileges]].<ref name="Entous_Barrett_Helderman_10/24/2017" /> "Fusion briefed only Elias on the reports, Simpson sent Elias nothing on paper—he was briefed orally", Mayer reported.<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" /> In its application for a FISA warrant to survey Carter Page, the Department of Justice told the [[United States Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Court|FISC]] that Simpson had not informed Steele of the motivation behind the research into Trump's ties with Russia.<ref name="Philips_2/24/2018" /> Steele testified to Congress that he did not know the Clinton campaign was the source of the payments "because he was retained by Fusion GPS".<ref name="Rocah_Goldman_McQuade_7/25/2018">{{cite web |last1=Rocah |first1=Mimi |last2=Goldman |first2=Daniel S. |last3=McQuade |first3=Barbara |title=FBI Would've Been Derelict Not to Use Steele Dossier for the Carter Page FISA Warrant | website=[[The Daily Beast]] |date=July 25, 2018 |url=https://www.thedailybeast.com/fbi-would-have-been-derelict-not-to-use-the-steele-dossier-for-the-carter-page-fisa-warrant |access-date=October 13, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Raju_Herb_10/26/2017">{{cite news |last1=Raju |first1=Manu |last2=Herb |first2=Jeremy |title=In Hill interviews, top Dems denied knowledge of payments to firm behind Trump dossier |work=[[CNN]] |date=October 26, 2017 |url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/10/26/politics/john-podesta-debbie-wasserman-schultz-trump-dossier/index.html |access-date=August 3, 2019}}</ref> By "late July 2016",<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|93–94}} "several months" after signing the contract with Fusion GPS, Steele became aware that the DNC and the Clinton campaign were the ultimate clients.<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" />
A spokesperson for the DNC said neither [[Tom Perez]] nor "the new leadership of the DNC were ... involved in any decision-making regarding Fusion GPS, nor were they aware that Perkins Coie was working with the organization."<ref name="Watkins_Merica_Polantz_10/25/2017" /> A spokesperson for Perkins Coie said the campaign and the DNC were unaware Fusion GPS "had been hired to conduct the research".<ref name="Vogel_10/24/2017" /> ''The Washington Post'' reported that it is not clear how much of the research Elias received from Fusion GPS he shared with the campaign and the DNC. It is also not clear who in those organizations knew about the roles of Fusion GPS and Steele, but one person "close to the matter" said the organizations were "not informed by the law firm of Fusion GPS's role".<ref name="Entous_Barrett_Helderman_10/24/2017" /> ''[[The New York Times]]'' revealed that earlier in 2017, "Mr. Elias had denied that he had possessed the dossier before the election."<ref name="Vogel_10/24/2017">{{cite news |last=Vogel |first=Kenneth P. |author-link=Kenneth P. Vogel |title=Clinton Campaign and Democratic Party Helped Pay for Russia Trump Dossier |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=October 24, 2017 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/10/24/us/politics/clinton-dnc-russia-dossier.html |access-date=September 9, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Watkins_Merica_Polantz_10/25/2017" /> The Clinton campaign did not know about Steele or that he was sharing his findings with the FBI, and "one top Clinton campaign official" told Jane Mayer that "If I'd known the F.B.I. was investigating Trump, I would have been shouting it from the rooftops!"<ref name="Mayer_11/25/2019" />
The firewall was reportedly so effective even campaign principals [[John Podesta]] and [[Robby Mook]] did not know Steele was on the Democratic payroll until ''Mother Jones'' reported on the issue on October 31, 2016, in an article that did not name Steele.<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" /> When the ''Mother Jones'' story broke, John Podesta, chairman of the Clinton campaign, said he was "stunned by the news that the FBI had launched a full-blown investigation into Trump, especially one that was informed by research underwritten by the Clinton campaign." Although they knew Perkins Coie had spent money for opposition research, neither Podesta nor campaign manager Robby Mook knew Steele was on the Democratic payroll. Mayer said they both maintain they "didn't read the dossier until ''BuzzFeed'' posted it online".<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" /> When [[Donna Brazile]], interim DNC chair, later heard rumors of the research on Russia, she asked Elias about it. According to Brazile, he brushed her off and said "You don't want to know."<ref name="Isikoff_Corn_3/17/2018" /> Mayer also said that "the Clinton campaign never learned that Christopher Steele was on their payroll until it [the dossier] was in the press."<ref name="NPR_3/6/2018">{{cite news |title=Journalist Charts The 'Bizarre Twists And Turns' Of The Trump–Russia Dossier |work=[[NPR]] |date=March 6, 2018 |url=https://www.npr.org/2018/03/06/591130207/journalist-charts-the-bizarre-twists-and-turns-of-the-trump-russia-dossier |access-date=October 5, 2018}}</ref> "Far from a secret campaign weapon, Steele turned out to be a secret kept from the campaign."<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" /> In their 2019 book, the founders of Fusion GPS wrote "that no one from Fusion ever met or talked with Clinton and that she herself 'had no idea who they were'."<ref name="Nicholas_11/21/2019" /><ref name="Mayer_11/25/2019" />
Philip Bump wrote that the dossier "has never been shown to have informed the Clinton campaign's approach and that was not made public until shortly before Trump was inaugurated."<ref name="Bump_8/3/2020">{{cite news |last=Bump |first=Philip |title=Of course Trump's campaign can't reject the idea that Trump might seek foreign assistance |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=August 3, 2020 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2020/08/03/course-trumps-campaign-cant-reject-idea-that-trump-might-seek-foreign-assistance/ |access-date=August 4, 2020}}</ref>
On February 15, 2022, ''The Washington Post'' reported: "So far, there is no evidence that the Clinton campaign directly managed the Steele reporting or leaks about it to the media."<ref name="Kessler_2/15/2022">{{cite news | last=Kessler | first=Glenn |author-link=Glenn Kessler (journalist) | title=Fact Checker: Analysis – Here's why Trump once again is claiming 'spying' by Democrats | newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] | date=February 15, 2022 | url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2022/02/15/heres-why-trump-once-again-is-claiming-spying-by-democrats/ | access-date=February 18, 2022}}</ref>
=== Hints of existence ===
When Fusion GPS first hired Steele, "they thought that 'no one would ever find out' about the discreet work Steele would perform. That prediction proved naive",<ref name="Nicholas_11/21/2019" /> and the founders of Fusion GPS have described how they soon did not hide the fact that they were researching Trump and Russia: "Fusion and Steele tried to alert U.S. law enforcement and the news media to the material they'd uncovered. The firm's office in Washington's Dupont Circle neighborhood became 'something of a public reading room' for journalists seeking information about Trumpworld."<ref name="Nicholas_11/21/2019" /> In September they arranged private meetings at the Tabard Inn, in Washington, D.C., between Steele and reporters from ''The Washington Post'', ''The New York Times'', ''The New Yorker'', ABC News, and other outlets.<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" /> The meetings were "at staggered times, to ensure that the journalists didn't bump into one another".<ref name="Roig-Franzia_10/7/2024" /> Jane Mayer attended one of the meetings. None of these news organizations ran any stories about the allegations at that time.<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" />
Although ''BuzzFeed'' was not invited to these meetings, [[Ben Smith (journalist)|Ben Smith]], editor of ''BuzzFeed'', wrote that "We were hardly the first journalists to get the document—but we may have been the first to get it without promising to keep it secret ...
