[the journalists at the Tabard Inn meetings] had promised Simpson that they wouldn't write about the dossier itself, its author, or its path through the American government, [and] they couldn't report on these things either."<ref name="Smith_4/20/2023">{{cite web | last=Smith | first=Ben | title=After All That, I Would Still Publish the Dossier | website=[[The Atlantic]] | date=April 20, 2023 | url=https://12ft.io/proxy?q=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.theatlantic.com%2Fideas%2Farchive%2F2023%2F04%2Fben-smith-trump-dossier-buzzfeed%2F673794%2F | access-date=April 23, 2023}}</ref> [[BBC]] correspondent Paul Wood was allowed to see "a dozen" pages of the dossier in October 2016.<ref name="Wood_8/12/2020" />
Before the election, only two news sources mentioned allegations that came from dossier reports. Steele had been in contact with both authors. These were a September 23, 2016, ''[[Yahoo! News]]'' article by [[Michael Isikoff]] that focused on Carter Page,<ref name="Isikoff_9/23/2016" /> and an article by [[David Corn]] on October 31, 2016, a week before the election, in ''Mother Jones'' magazine.<ref name="Corn_10/31/2016" />
At the time, the FBI had assumed that the source for the ''Yahoo!'' article was someone, other than Steele, "who had received a copy of the dossier". The 2019 DOJ report by Michael Horowitz criticized the FBI for failing to ask Steele if he had a role in that Yahoo! article.<ref name="Savage_Goldman_4/12/2021">{{cite news |last1=Savage |first1=Charlie |author1-link=Charlie Savage (author) |last2=Goldman |first2=Adam |title=Subpoenaing the Brookings Institution, Durham Focuses on Trump-Russia Dossier |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=April 12, 2021 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2021/04/12/us/politics/durham-investigation-brookings-institution.html |access-date=April 13, 2021}}</ref>
==== ''Mother Jones'' story ====
By the third quarter of 2016, many news organizations knew about the existence of the dossier that had been described as an "open secret" among journalists but chose not to publish information they could not confirm.<ref name="Shane_Confessore_Rosenberg_1/12/2017" /><ref name="Ember_Grynbaum_1/10/2017">{{cite news |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/01/10/business/buzzfeed-donald-trump-russia.html |title=BuzzFeed Posts Unverified Claims on Trump, Igniting a Debate |first1=Sydney |last1=Ember |first2=Michael M. |last2=Grynbaum |date=January 10, 2017 |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=April 2, 2018}}</ref> ''Mother Jones'' was the first to report the existence of the dossier and that it was exclusively funded by Democrats.<ref name="Blake_10/25/2017">{{cite news |last=Blake |first=Aaron |title=The Clinton camp and DNC funded what became the Trump-Russia dossier: Here's what it means |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=October 25, 2017 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/the-fix/wp/2017/10/25/the-clinton-camp-and-the-dnc-helped-pay-for-that-trump-russia-dossier-heres-what-it-means/ |access-date=December 17, 2019}}</ref><ref name="O'Brien_10/26/2017">{{cite news |last=O'Brien |first=Timothy L. |title=Don't Be Distracted by the Trump Dossier Fuss |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |date=October 26, 2017 |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2017-10-26/don-t-be-distracted-by-the-trump-dossier-fuss |access-date=December 17, 2019}}</ref>
By October 2016, Steele had compiled 33 pages (16 reports), and he then passed on what he had discovered to [[David Corn]], a reporter from ''Mother Jones'' magazine. On October 31, 2016, a week before the election, ''Mother Jones'' reported that a former intelligence officer, whom they did not name, had produced a report based on Russian sources and turned it over to the FBI.<ref name="Corn_10/31/2016">{{cite magazine |last=Corn |first=David |author-link=David Corn |date=October 31, 2016 |title=A Veteran Spy Has Given the FBI Information Alleging a Russian Operation to Cultivate Donald Trump |magazine=[[Mother Jones (magazine)|Mother Jones]] |url=https://www.motherjones.com/politics/2016/10/veteran-spy-gave-fbi-info-alleging-russian-operation-cultivate-donald-trump/ |access-date=December 24, 2017}}</ref> The article disclosed some of the dossier's allegations:<ref name="Corn_10/31/2016" /> {{blockquote| The first memo, based on the former intelligence officer's conversations with Russian sources, noted, "Russian regime has been cultivating, supporting and assisting TRUMP for at least 5 years. Aim, endorsed by PUTIN, has been to encourage splits and divisions in western alliance." It maintained that Trump "and his inner circle have accepted a regular flow of intelligence from the Kremlin, including on his Democratic and other political rivals". It claimed Russian intelligence had "compromised" Trump during his visits to Moscow and could "blackmail him". It also reported that Russian intelligence had compiled a dossier on Hillary Clinton based on "bugged conversations she had on various visits to Russia and intercepted phone calls".}}
=== Post-election events ===
After Trump's election on November 8, 2016, the Democratic client stopped paying for the investigation, but Steele continued working on the dossier for Fusion GPS.<ref name="Shane_Confessore_Rosenberg_1/12/2017" /> According to ''The Independent'', at that time, Simpson "reportedly spent his own money to continue the investigation".<ref name="Sampathkumar_8/23/2017">{{cite news |newspaper=[[The Independent]] |title=Trump–Russia dossier sources revealed to the FBI by Christopher Steele |first=Mythili |last=Sampathkumar |date=August 23, 2017 |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/trump-russia-dossier-sources-christopher-steele-fbi-senate-judiciary-robert-mueller-a7908946.html |access-date=April 1, 2018}}</ref> According to ''The New York Times'', after the election, Steele's dossier became one of Washington's "worst-kept secrets", and journalists worked to verify the allegations.<ref name="Shane_Confessore_Rosenberg_1/12/2017" />
On November 18, 2016, Republican Senator [[John McCain]], who had been informed about the alleged links between the Kremlin and Trump, met with former British ambassador to Moscow [[Andrew Wood (diplomat)|Sir Andrew Wood]] at the [[Halifax International Security Forum]] in Canada. Wood told McCain about the existence of the collected materials about Trump,<ref name="Edwards_1/11/2017">{{cite web |last=Edwards |first=Jim |title=Timeline: That Russian Trump blackmail dossier has been making the rounds for months—here is how it finally came to light | website=[[Business Insider]] |date=January 11, 2017 |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/timeline-russia-trump-sex-blackmail-golden-shower-dossier-2017-1 |access-date=April 7, 2018}}</ref> and also vouched for Steele's professionalism and integrity.<ref name="Sengupta_1/12/2017">{{cite news |last=Sengupta |first=Kim |title=Former British ambassador played key role in leaked Trump documents |newspaper=[[The Independent]] |date=January 12, 2017 |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/donald-trump-russia-dossier-leak-sir-andrew-wood-john-mccain-british-ambassador-spy-a7524931.html |access-date=April 7, 2018}}</ref>
According to Simpson's August 22, 2017, testimony to the Senate Judiciary Committee, Steele and [[David J. Kramer]], a longtime McCain aide and former U.S. State Department official working at [[Arizona State University]], met each other at the Halifax forum and discussed the dossier. Kramer told Steele that McCain wanted to "ask questions about it at the FBI. ... All we sort of wanted was for the government to do its job and we were concerned about whether the information that we provided previously had ever, you know, risen to the leadership level of the FBI."<ref name="Cheney_1/9/2018" />
Steele had agreed with Fusion GPS to deliver a hard copy of all 16 reports to McCain,<ref name="Borger_4/28/2017" /> that McCain received in early December from Kramer.<ref name="Shane_Confessore_Rosenberg_1/12/2017" /> On December 9, McCain met personally with FBI Director [[James Comey]] and gave him a copy of the dossier,<ref name="Edwards_1/11/2017" /><ref name="Borger_1/11/2017" /><ref name="Resnick_5/9/2018">{{cite web |last=Resnick |first=Gideon |title=McCain Defends Giving Trump Dossier to Comey: Duty Demanded I Do It | website=[[The Daily Beast]] |date=May 9, 2018 |url=https://www.thedailybeast.com/mccain-confirms-he-gave-trump-dossier-to-comey-duty-demanded-i-do-it |access-date=May 12, 2018}}</ref> which did not yet include the last "Report 166", dated December 13, 2016.<ref name="Gubarev_Buzzfeed_1:17-cv-60426-UU" /> On March 25, 2019, Senator [[Lindsey Graham]], a close friend of McCain's, contradicted Trump's attacks against McCain and revealed that when McCain showed him the dossier, he had encouraged McCain to give the dossier to the FBI and that McCain acted appropriately.<ref name="Sonmez_3/25/2019">{{cite news |last=Sonmez |first=Felicia |title=Sen. Lindsey Graham says he told John McCain to give Trump–Russia dossier to FBI |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=March 25, 2019 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/sen-lindsey-graham-says-he-told-john-mccain-to-give-trump-russia-dossier-to-fbi/2019/03/25/f35e28ca-4f26-11e9-a3f7-78b7525a8d5f_story.html |access-date=March 25, 2019}}</ref> Graham described how he confronted Trump: "Senator McCain deserves better. There were some McCain people who took a piece of garbage and tried to go after Trump after the election. But I told the president it was not John McCain."<ref name="Dennis_3/25/2019">{{cite news |last=Dennis |first=Steven T. |title=Graham Contradicts Trump Attacks on McCain Over Disputed Dossier |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |date=March 25, 2019 |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2019-03-25/graham-contradicts-trump-attacks-on-mccain-over-disputed-dossier |access-date=March 26, 2019}}</ref> Comey later confirmed that counterintelligence investigations were underway into possible links between Trump associates and Moscow.<ref name="Borger_4/28/2017" />
After delivering his 16 reports to McCain, Steele received more information and composed the two-page "December memo", dated December 13. It mostly contained allegations against Trump's personal attorney, Michael Cohen, that Cohen later denied.<ref name="Harding_5/10/2017" /><ref name="Cormier_5/5/2017" /> In an April 2017 court filing, Steele revealed previously unreported information that he had given a copy of his last report to a "senior UK government national security official acting in his official capacity, on a confidential basis in hard copy form", because it "had implications for the national security of the US and the UK".<ref name="Borger_4/28/2017" />
Both Simpson and Steele have denied providing the dossier to ''BuzzFeed''.<ref name="Donn_6/29/2018" /> Unsealed documents from the discovery process in Russian entrepreneur Aleksej Gubarev's defamation lawsuit revealed that David Kramer, an associate of John McCain, gave the dossier to ''BuzzFeed'' "in December 2016, weeks after the election",<ref name="Cohen_Collier_3/16/2019">{{cite news |last1=Cohen |first1=Marshall |last2=Collier |first2=Kevin |title=Unsealed documents shed new light on efforts to verify Trump–Russia dossier |work=[[CNN]] |date=March 16, 2019 |url=https://www.cnn.com/2019/03/15/politics/unsealed-documents-trump-russia-dossier/index.html |access-date=March 16, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Gross_Simpson_Fritsch_11/26/2019">{{cite news |last1=Gross |first1=Terry |author-link1=Terry Gross |last2=Simpson |first2=Glenn |author-link2=Glenn R. Simpson |last3=Fritsch |first3=Peter |title=Fusion GPS Founders On Russian Efforts To Sow Discord: 'They Have Succeeded' |work=[[NPR]] |date=November 26, 2019 |url=https://www.npr.org/2019/11/26/782908119/fusion-gps-founders-on-russian-efforts-to-sow-discord-they-have-succeeded |department=[[Fresh Air]]}}</ref> in what Fritsch has called an "ill-advised" [[Hail Mary pass]].<ref name="Gross_Simpson_Fritsch_11/26/2019" /> Ben Smith, editor of ''BuzzFeed'' has described how they got the dossier:<ref name="Smith_4/20/2023" /> {{blockquote| On December 29, the Republican foreign-policy expert David Kramer invited Ken [Bensinger] to his office at the McCain Institute. He then did something careful Washington insiders do: He left Ken alone in the room with the document for 20 minutes, without, in Ken's view, giving clear instructions about whether he could make a copy. Ken took a picture of every page. (Kramer later denied that he'd allowed Ken to copy it ...)}}
It was never Steele's intention that the dossier be public because it was unfinished raw intelligence and could have "compromised sources and methods".<ref name="Gross_Simpson_Fritsch_11/26/2019" /> Steele was also worried about the safety of his source network, and he expressed concern that if Trump won the election, the new FBI director, and other agency heads appointed by Trump, might be more loyal to Trump "and could decide to take action against Steele and his source network".<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|275}}
==== Briefings of Obama and Trump ====
On January 5, 2017, the chiefs of four U.S. intelligence agencies briefed President [[Barack Obama]] and Vice President [[Joe Biden]] about the Russian interference in the election and the existence of the dossier and its allegations.<ref name="Hensch_1/12/2017" /><ref name="Nowicki_1/13/2017">{{cite news |last=Nowicki |first=Dan |title=John McCain intrigue grows in Donald Trump dossier affair |newspaper=[[The Arizona Republic]] |date=January 13, 2017 |url=https://www.azcentral.com/story/news/politics/arizona/2017/01/12/john-mccain-intrigue-grows-donald-trump-dossier-affair/96498178/ |access-date=February 11, 2018}}</ref><ref name="AOL_1/12/2017">{{cite news |title=Biden: Obama and I were told about Trump dossier ahead of leaks |work=[[AOL]] |date=January 12, 2017 |url=https://www.aol.com/article/news/2017/01/12/biden-obama-and-i-were-told-about-trump-dossier-ahead-of-leaks/21654013/ |access-date=April 4, 2018}}</ref>
On the afternoon<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|180}} of January 6, 2017, President-elect Trump and his transition team received a similar briefing in Trump Tower.<ref name="Kosoff_7/20/2017">{{cite web |last1=Kosoff |first1=Maya |title=Trump Thinks Comey Was Trying to Blackmail Him | website=[[Vanity Fair (magazine)|Vanity Fair]] |date=July 20, 2017 |url=https://www.vanityfair.com/news/2017/07/trump-thinks-comey-was-trying-to-blackmail-him |access-date=April 7, 2018}}</ref> All four of the top intelligence chiefs met with Trump and his transition team. They were Director of National Intelligence James Clapper, FBI Director James Comey, CIA Director John Brennan, and NSA Director Admiral [[Michael S. Rogers|Mike Rogers]]. They informed Trump of the Russian election interference,<ref name="Perez_Sciutto_Tapper_Bernstein_1/10/2017" /><ref name="Chan_4/16/2018" /> and Comey told them of "a piece of Steele's reporting that indicated Russia had files of derogatory information on both Clinton and the President-elect".<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|180}} Trump later testified under oath "that he was first made aware of the existence of the Dossier by then FBI Director James Comey on 6 January 2018[sic], at a meeting that took place during the transition period following his election".<ref name="TrumpvOrbis_2/1/2024" />
Then, according to a pre-arranged plan, Brennan, Clapper, and Rogers left, and Comey then asked to speak with Trump alone. Comey then informed Trump of the dossier and its allegations about salacious tapes held by the Russians. Comey later reported he was very nervous. The previous day, the secretary of the Department of Homeland Security told Comey to "be very careful", "choose your words carefully", and then "get outta there". Trump became very defensive, and Comey described the meeting as "really weird".<ref name="Chan_4/16/2018">{{cite web |last=Chan |first=Tara Francis |title='Be very careful:' Comey was the first person to tell Trump about the Steele dossier before the inauguration—he couldn't have been more nervous | website=[[Business Insider]] |date=April 16, 2018 |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/when-was-trump-told-about-the-steele-dossier-2018-4 |access-date=April 19, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Perez_Sciutto_Tapper_Bernstein_1/10/2017" /> Trump later expressed that he felt James Comey was trying to blackmail him at the meeting in Trump Tower, held two weeks before the inauguration.<ref name="Kosoff_7/20/2017" /> In April 2018, Comey said he did not inform Trump the dossier was partly funded by Democrats because that "wasn't necessary for my goal, which was to alert him that we had this information".<ref name="Stracqualursi_4/13/2018">{{cite news |last=Stracqualursi |first=Veronica |title=Comey: 'I don't know' if Trump was with prostitutes in Moscow |work=[[CNN]] |date=April 13, 2018 |url=https://www.cnn.com/2018/04/13/politics/james-comey-trump-dossier/index.html |access-date=April 15, 2018}}</ref><ref name="McCarthy_4/13/2018">{{cite news |last=McCarthy |first=Tom |title='Slime ball': Trump attacks Comey after new book likens president to mafia boss |newspaper=[[The Guardian]] |date=April 13, 2018 |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2018/apr/13/trump-attacks-james-comey-fbi-director-book |access-date=April 15, 2018}}</ref>
On December 14, 2018, the FBI released a document called "Annex A", that was "part of [the] Russia dossier summary" used to brief Trump and Obama.<ref name="Gerstein_Cheney_12/14/2018">{{cite web |last1=Gerstein |first1=Josh |last2=Cheney |first2=Kyle |title=FBI releases part of Russia dossier summary used to brief Trump, Obama |newspaper=[[Politico]] |date=December 14, 2018 |url=https://www.politico.com/story/2018/12/14/russia-dossier-fbi-trump-obama-1066643 |access-date=November 12, 2019}}</ref> The FBI withheld parts of the synopsis on the grounds that it remained classified and "because it pertains to ongoing investigations or court proceedings, originated with a confidential source or describes confidential investigative techniques or procedures".<ref name="Gerstein_Cheney_12/14/2018" />
Two days after the publication of the dossier on January 10, 2017, [[James Clapper]] issued a statement describing the leaks in the press about their Trump Tower meeting with Trump as damaging to U.S. national security.<ref name="Hartmann_1/12/2017">{{cite magazine |last1=Hartmann |first1=Margaret |date=January 12, 2017 |title=Clapper Denounces 'Corrosive and Damaging' Trump Dossier Leak |url=https://nymag.com/daily/intelligencer/2017/01/clapper-denounces-corrosive-damaging-trump-dossier-leak.html |magazine=[[New York (magazine)|New York]] |access-date=January 12, 2017}}</ref> The statement also included the non-committal wording that the U.S. intelligence community "has not made any judgment that the information in this document is reliable, and we did not rely upon it in any way for our conclusions".<ref name="Weisman_Steinhauer_1/12/2017" /> This contradicted Trump's previous claim that Clapper had said the information in the dossier was false; Clapper's statement actually said the intelligence community had made no judgment on the truth of the information.<ref name="Weisman_Steinhauer_1/12/2017">{{cite news |last1=Weisman |first1=Jonathan |last2=Steinhauer |first2=Jennifer |title=Intelligence Chief's Olive Branch to Trump Gets Twisted in Translation |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/01/12/us/politics/donald-trump-transition.html |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=January 12, 2017 |access-date=April 1, 2018}}</ref>
James Comey disagreed with Clapper's wording, but Clapper's wording remained unchanged. Comey later told the Office of the Inspector General of his concerns at that time, because he believed the dossier to be more reliable than indicated in Clapper's non-committal statement:<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|181}} {{blockquote| I worry that it may not be best to say "The IC has not made any judgment that the information in the document is reliable." I say that because we HAVE concluded that the source [Steele] is reliable and has a track record with us of reporting reliable information; we have some visibility into his source network, some of which we have determined to be sub-sources in a position to report on such things; and much of what he reports in the current document is consistent with and corroborative of other reporting included in the body of the main IC report. That said, we are not able to sufficiently corroborate the reporting to include in the body of the [ICA] report.}}
=== Publication and reactions ===
On January 10, 2017, CNN reported that classified documents presented to Obama and Trump the previous week included allegations that Russian operatives possess "compromising personal and financial information" about Trump. CNN said it would not publish specific details on the reports because it had not "independently corroborated the specific allegations".<ref name="Perez_Sciutto_Tapper_Bernstein_1/10/2017">{{cite news |first1=Evan |last1=Perez |first2=Jim |last2=Sciutto |author-link2=Jim Sciutto |first3=Jake |last3=Tapper |author-link3=Jake Tapper |first4=Carl |last4=Bernstein |author-link4=Carl Bernstein |title=Intel chiefs presented Trump with claims of Russian efforts to compromise him |date=January 10, 2017 |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2017/01/10/politics/donald-trump-intelligence-report-russia/index.html |work=[[CNN]] |access-date=January 11, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Wemple_1/10/2017">{{cite news |first=Erik |last=Wemple |author-link=Erik Wemple |title=BuzzFeed's ridiculous rationale for publishing the Trump–Russia dossier |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=January 10, 2017 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/blogs/erik-wemple/wp/2017/01/10/buzzfeeds-ridiculous-rationale-for-publishing-the-trump-russia-dossier/ |access-date=April 4, 2018}}</ref> Following the CNN report,<ref name="Sutton_1/11/2017" /> ''[[BuzzFeed]]'' published a {{Nowrap|35-page}} draft dossier that it said was the basis for the briefing, including unverified claims that Russian operatives had collected "embarrassing material" involving Trump that could be used to blackmail him. ''BuzzFeed'' said the information included "specific, unverified, and potentially unverifiable allegations of contact between Trump aides and Russian operatives".
<ref name="Bensinger_1/10/2017">{{cite news |last1=Bensinger |first1=Ken |last2=Elder |first2=Miriam |last3=Schoofs |first3=Mark |title=These Reports Allege Trump Has Deep Ties To Russia |work=[[BuzzFeed News]] |date=January 10, 2017 |url=https://www.buzzfeednews.com/article/kenbensinger/these-reports-allege-trump-has-deep-ties-to-russia |access-date=December 24, 2017 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250926150328/https://www.buzzfeednews.com/article/kenbensinger/these-reports-allege-trump-has-deep-ties-to-russia | archive-date=September 26, 2025 | url-status=dead }}</ref><ref name="Stein_1/10/2017" /><ref name="Mak_Suebsaeng_Weiss_ 1/10/2017">{{cite web |title=U.S. Spies Warn Trump and GOP: Russia Could Get You Next |url=https://www.thedailybeast.com/articles/2017/01/10/u-s-spies-warn-trump-and-gop-russia-could-get-you-next.html | website=[[The Daily Beast]] |date=January 10, 2017 |first1=Tim |last1=Mak |first2=Asawin |last2=Suebsaeng |first3=Michael |last3=Weiss |access-date=January 11, 2017}}</ref><ref name="Shane_Goldman_Rosenberg_1/10/2017">{{cite news | last1=Shane | first1=Scott | last2=Goldman | first2=Adam | last3=Rosenberg | first3=Matthew | date=January 10, 2017 | title=Trump Received Unsubstantiated Report That Russia Had Damaging Information About Him | newspaper=[[The New York Times]] | url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/01/10/us/politics/donald-trump-russia-intelligence.html | access-date=January 11, 2017}}</ref>
''The New York Times'' commented: "Mr. Steele has made clear to associates that he always considered the dossier to be [[raw intelligence]]—not established facts, but a starting point for further investigation."<ref name="Shane_Goldman_Rosenberg_4/19/2019">{{cite news |last1=Shane |first1=Scott |author-link1=Scott Shane |last2=Goldman |first2=Adam |last3=Rosenberg |first3=Matthew |author-link3=Matthew Rosenberg |title=Mueller Report Likely to Renew Scrutiny of Steele Dossier |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=April 19, 2019 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/04/19/us/politics/steele-dossier-mueller-report.html |access-date=April 20, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Kessler_10/29/2017" /> Judge Craig Karsnitz described the purpose of the dossier: "An intelligence report is simply a report of information potentially relevant to an investigation. It can take many forms, be true or false, and can be used as opposition research and an intelligence report."<ref name="Chase_2/14/2021">{{cite news |last=Chase |first=Randall |date=February 14, 2021 |title=Judge tosses Page defamation suit against Verizon company |url=https://apnews.com/article/donald-trump-carter-page-lawsuits-delaware-elections-d811f86697c2bb53b0132e0965754f57 |agency=[[Associated Press]]}}</ref> Christopher Burrows, co-founder of Orbis Business Intelligence, does not consider it a "dossier", but "information that is referred to as 'raw intelligence' in intelligence circles. ... a mixture of knowledge, rumor and hearsay. ... [A]n intelligence agency would enrich the findings with data, test probabilities and write analyses. It's an elaborate process. But Steele is not an intelligence agency."<ref name="Scheuermann_Schmitt_2/7/2019" />
''BuzzFeed''{{'s}} decision to publish the dossier was immediately criticized by many major media outlets for releasing the draft dossier without verifying its allegations.<ref name="Gezari_1/11/2017">{{cite web | last=Gezari | first=Vanessa M | title=BuzzFeed was right to publish Trump-Russia files | website=[[Columbia Journalism Review]] | date=January 11, 2017 | url=https://www.cjr.org/criticism/buzzfeed_trump_russia_memos.php | access-date=January 31, 2023}}</ref><ref name="Wemple_1/10/2017" /><ref name="Bump_1/11/2017">{{cite news |last1=Bump |first1=Philip |title=BuzzFeed, the Russia dossier and the problem of too much information |date=January 11, 2017 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/the-fix/wp/2017/01/11/the-problem-of-too-much-information/ |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |access-date=July 29, 2017}}</ref><ref name="Zurawik_1/11/2017">{{cite news |last=Zurawik |first=David |title=BuzzFeed undermines all journalists with Trump 'dossier' |date=January 11, 2017 |access-date=January 10, 2018 |url=https://www.baltimoresun.com/entertainment/tv/z-on-tv-blog/bal-buzzfeed--trump-dossier-journalism-debased-20170111-story.html |newspaper=[[The Baltimore Sun]] |archive-date=January 7, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180107211312/http://www.baltimoresun.com/entertainment/tv/z-on-tv-blog/bal-buzzfeed--trump-dossier-journalism-debased-20170111-story.html |url-status=dead}}</ref><ref name="Harkiolakis_2020">{{cite journal | last=Harkiolakis | first=Tatiana | title=BuzzFeed publishing the unverified Steele dossier: a brief case study | journal=International Journal of Teaching and Case Studies | publisher=Inderscience | volume=11 | issue=1 | year=2020 | issn=1749-9151 | doi=10.1504/ijtcs.2020.108168 | page=65}}</ref> ''Washington Post'' columnist [[Margaret Sullivan (journalist)|Margaret Sullivan]] called it "scurrilous allegations dressed up as an intelligence report meant to damage Donald Trump",<ref name="Sullivan_1/11/2017">{{cite web |last=Sullivan |first=Margaret |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/lifestyle/style/how-buzzfeed-crossed-the-line-in-publishing-salacious-dossier-on-trump/2017/01/11/957b59f6-d801-11e6-9a36-1d296534b31e_story.html |title=How BuzzFeed crossed the line in publishing salacious 'dossier' on Trump |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=January 11, 2017 |access-date=July 31, 2017}}</ref> while ''The New York Times'' noted that the publication sparked a debate centering on the use of unsubstantiated information from anonymous sources.<ref name="Smith_1/23/2017">{{cite news |first=Ben |last=Smith |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/01/23/opinion/why-buzzfeed-news-published-the-dossier.html |title=Why BuzzFeed News Published the Dossier |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=January 23, 2017 |access-date=February 11, 2017}}</ref> ''BuzzFeed''{{'}}s executive staff said the materials were newsworthy because they were "in wide circulation at the highest levels of American government and media" and argued that this justified public release.<ref name="Graham_1/11/2017">{{cite magazine |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2017/01/why-did-buzzfeed-publish-the-trump-dossier/512771/ |title=The Trouble With Publishing the Trump Dossier |first=David A. |last=Graham |magazine=[[The Atlantic]] |date=January 11, 2017 |access-date=January 10, 2018}}</ref> A judge in the [[Superior Court of the District of Columbia]] agreed with this reasoning when he threw out a libel suit against Steele and Orbis Business Intelligence.<ref name="Maddow_8/20/2018">{{cite episode |last1=Maddow |first1=Rachel |date=August 20, 2018 |title=Russia's Alfa Bank fails in lawsuit over Steele's Trump dossier |url=https://www.msnbc.com/rachel-maddow/watch/russia-s-alfa-bank-fails-in-lawsuit-over-steele-s-trump-dossier-1303085635995 |access-date=August 20, 2018 |type=video |publisher=[[MSNBC]] |series=[[The Rachel Maddow Show]]}}</ref><ref name="Wood_8/21/2018">{{cite news |last=Wood |first=Paul |author-link=Paul Wood (journalist) |date=August 21, 2018 |title=Trump dossier author lawsuit dismissed |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-45251255 |access-date=November 24, 2019 |work=[[BBC News]]}}</ref><ref name="Harding_8/21/2018">{{cite news |last1=Harding |first1=Luke |author-link1=Luke Harding |date=August 21, 2018 |title=Author of Trump–Russia dossier wins libel case in US court |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2018/aug/21/author-of-trump-russia-dossier-wins-libel-case-in-us-court-christopher-steele |access-date=August 23, 2018 |newspaper=[[The Guardian]]}}</ref>
Although the ''[[Columbia Journalism Review]]'' had originally (January 11, 2017) backed ''BuzzFeed''{{'s}} publication of the dossier,<ref name="Gezari_1/11/2017" /> and editor [[Kyle Pope]] had tweeted his support of that decision,<ref name="Pope_1/11/2017">{{cite web | title=Pope tweets support of CJR decision | date=January 11, 2017 | last=Pope | first=Kyle | via=[[Twitter]] | url=https://twitter.com/kylepope/status/819311225674887169 | access-date=January 31, 2023 | quote=We defend BuzzFeed's decision to publish}}</ref> he later (November 17, 2021) described it as "a document that was never designed to meet the standards of good journalism", noting that its credibility had collapsed, and concluding that it was the source of "a lot of nonsense and misdirection" in subsequent media coverage and should not have been published at all.<ref name="Pope_11/17/2021">{{cite magazine |last=Pope |first=Kyle |title=The media's belated rush to judgment on the Trump dossier |magazine=[[Columbia Journalism Review]] |date=November 17, 2021 |url=https://www.cjr.org/opinion/the-medias-belated-rush-to-judgment-on-the-trump-dossier.php |access-date=November 19, 2021}}</ref> Sara Fischer, media reporter for ''[[Axios (website)|Axios]]'', was critical of the initial press coverage of the dossier before it had been adequately scrutinized.<ref name="Fischer_11/14/2021">{{cite web | last=Fischer | first=Sara | title=The media's epic fail on the Steele dossier | website=[[Axios (website)|Axios]] | date=November 14, 2021 | url=https://www.axios.com/2021/11/14/steele-dossier-discredited-media-corrections-buzzfeed-washington-post | access-date=August 23, 2022}}</ref>
The draft dossier's publication by ''BuzzFeed'' has always been defended by [[Jack Shafer]],<ref name="Shafer_1/11/2017">{{cite web |last=Shafer |first=Jack |author-link=Jack Shafer |title=The Coup Before the Inauguration |magazine=[[Politico]] |date=January 11, 2017 |url=https://www.politico.com/magazine/story/2017/01/donald-trump-intelligence-report-buzzfeed-cnn-coup-214623/ |access-date=May 9, 2020}}</ref> ''[[Politico]]''{{'}}s senior media writer, as well as by [[Richard Tofel]] of ''[[ProPublica]]'' and the ''Columbia Journalism Review''. Shafer defended the public's right to know about the allegations against Trump, and saw a parallel in Judge Ungaro's ruling in the defamation suit filed by Aleksej Gubarev.<ref name="Shafer_12/22/2018">{{cite web |last=Shafer |first=Jack |author-link=Jack Shafer |date=December 22, 2018 |title=Week 83: BuzzFeed Takes a Victory Lap |url=https://www.politico.com/magazine/story/2018/12/22/week-83-buzzfeed-takes-a-victory-lap-223556/ |access-date=December 23, 2018 |magazine=[[Politico]]}}</ref><ref name="Peiser_12/19/2018">{{cite news |last=Peiser |first=Jaclyn |date=December 19, 2018 |title=BuzzFeed Wins Defamation Lawsuit Filed by Executive Named in Trump Dossier |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/12/19/business/media/buzzfeed-dossier-lawsuit-trump-steele-russia.html |access-date=December 22, 2018 |newspaper=[[The New York Times]]}}</ref> Ungaro wrote that the "privilege exists to protect the media while they gather information needed for the public to exercise effective oversight of the government". She also noted that, before the FBI received any reports from Steele, they had "already opened a counterintelligence investigation into links between Russia and the Trump campaign".<ref name="Johnson_12/19/2018">{{cite magazine |last=Johnson |first=Ted |title=Judge Sides With BuzzFeed Over Publication of Steele Dossier |magazine=[[Variety (magazine)|Variety]] |date=December 19, 2018 |url=https://variety.com/2018/politics/news/buzzfeed-steele-dossier-trump-1203093603/ |access-date=March 26, 2019}}</ref>
In relation to a defamation lawsuit filed by Gubarev against ''BuzzFeed'' regarding their publication of the draft dossier, [[Master (judiciary)|Senior Master]] [[Barbara Fontaine]] said Steele was "in many respects in the same position as a whistle-blower" because of his actions "in sending part of the dossier to Senator John McCain and a senior government national security official, and in briefing sections of the US media". She said that "it was not known who provided the dossier to ''BuzzFeed'' but Mr Steele's evidence was that he was 'horrified and remains horrified' that it was published at all, let alone without substantial redactions."<ref name="Express_3/21/2018">{{cite web |title=Former British spy to provide evidence for BuzzFeed libel trial in US | website=[[Media of Jersey|Bailiwick Express]] |date=March 21, 2018 |url=https://gsy.bailiwickexpress.com/gsy/uk/former-british-spy-provide-evidence-buzzfeed-libel-trial-us/ |access-date=June 2, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180630190008/https://gsy.bailiwickexpress.com/gsy/uk/former-british-spy-provide-evidence-buzzfeed-libel-trial-us/ |archive-date=June 30, 2018 |url-status=dead}}</ref>
The founders of Fusion GPS felt so alarmed by what sources reported to Steele that they have defended the fact that they and Steele used intermediaries to pass dossier content to the authorities, but, regarding the publication by ''BuzzFeed'', if it had been up to them, "Steele's reporting never would have seen the light of day."<ref name="Ewing_11/22/2019" /> It was published without Steele's permission,<ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" />{{rp|927}}<ref name="Ewing_11/22/2019" /><ref name="Snyder_2/2/2020" /><ref name="Scheuermann_Schmitt_2/7/2019" /><ref name="CNS_7/20/2020">{{cite news |date=July 20, 2020 |title=Russian Tech Exec Sues Steele Dossier Author in UK Court |url=https://www.courthousenews.com/russian-tech-exec-sues-steele-dossier-author-in-uk-court/ |access-date=July 21, 2020 |website=[[Courthouse News Service]] |quote=Steele and his company said they had nothing to do with Buzzfeed's 'unauthorized online publication.'<br>The defendants 'did not intend the December memorandum or its content to be made public (and) did not provide the December memorandum to BuzzFeed or any other media organization.' |agency=[[Agence France-Presse]]}}</ref> and Christopher Burrows said: "We didn't expect the findings on Russia to reach the public."<ref name="Scheuermann_Schmitt_2/7/2019">{{cite news |last1=Scheuermann |first1=Christoph |last2=Schmitt |first2=Jörg |title=Much Has Been Confirmed in Dossier at Heart of Donald Trump Scandal |work=[[Der Spiegel]] |date=February 7, 2019 |url=https://www.spiegel.de/international/much-has-been-confirmed-in-dossier-at-heart-of-trump-scandal-a-1251551.html |access-date=November 22, 2019}}</ref>
They were also alarmed that the publication of the draft dossier would endanger sources, and Glenn Simpson immediately phoned Ken Bensinger at ''BuzzFeed'': "Take those fucking reports down right now! You are going to get people killed!"<ref name="Snyder_2/2/2020">{{cite web |last=Snyder |first=Dook |title=Book Review: 'Crime in Progress', an attempt to set the record straight, offers other side of story | website=The Berkshire Edge |date=February 2, 2020 |url=https://theberkshireedge.com/book-review-crime-in-progress-an-attempt-to-set-the-record-straight-offer-other-side-of-story/ |access-date=February 5, 2020}}</ref>
Steele wrote:<ref name="Johnson_7/22/2020">{{cite news |last=Johnson |first=Jamie |title=Former spy Christopher Steele shared explosive dossier on Donald Trump and Russia with British authorities because of 'serious risks to national security' |newspaper=[[The Daily Telegraph]] |date=July 22, 2020 |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/2020/07/22/former-spy-christopher-steele-shared-explosive-dossier-donald/ |access-date=September 3, 2020}}</ref> {{blockquote| I wonder if BuzzFeed have reflected on the lives and livelihoods they put at risk by publishing the dossier, or the shutter it has drawn down on any further collection efforts on this issue and others by anybody or any government agency. In my view BuzzFeed did the Kremlin's work for them because they were determined not to lose the scoop entirely after CNN broke the original story. One of the most irresponsible journalistic acts.}}
Six years after publishing the dossier, Ben Smith, editor of ''BuzzFeed'', has described his regrets about how it was done:<ref name="Smith_4/20/2023" /> {{blockquote| If I had to do it again, I would publish the dossier—we couldn't suppress it ... But I would hold more tightly to the document ... I wouldn't simply publish it as a PDF, destined to float free from our earnest caveats. At best, we could have published the document as screenshots attached to the context we had and the context we would learn. Perhaps in some small way, this would have limited its transformation from a set of claims into a banner of the 'resistance.' But I'm not under the illusion that journalists could have contained its wildfire spread, any more than I think we could have concealed it.}}
Steele does not like the term "dossier":<ref name="Mosk_Bruggeman_Donovan_10/18/2021_80522891">{{cite web | last1=Mosk | first1=Matthew | last2=Bruggeman | first2=Lucien | last3=Donovan | date=October 18, 2021 | title=Out of the Shadows: Christopher Steele defiant on dossier, says Trump still 'potential' threat | website=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] | url=https://abcnews.go.com/US/shadows-christopher-steele-defiant-dossier-trump-potential-threat/story?id=80522891 | access-date=October 9, 2023}}</ref><ref name="Stephanopoulos_Steele_10/18/2021">{{cite web |author1=[[George Stephanopoulos|Stephanopoulos, George]] |author2=[[Christopher Steele|Steele, Christopher]] |title=Out of the Shadows: The Man Behind the Steele Dossier | website=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] | date=October 18, 2021 | url=https://www.hulu.com/series/out-of-the-shadows-the-man-behind-the-steele-dossier-d53aac4a-d053-4675-a7c1-4e262ee397a8 | access-date=October 8, 2023}}</ref> {{blockquote| because it wasn't a dossier. It's a series of reports on a live issue, the election campaign, running through time. These reports were not collated and presented in one offering, nor were they analyzed in detail by us. Effectively, it was a running commentary. It wasn't a dossier.}}
=== Format ===
When ''BuzzFeed'' published the {{Nowrap|35-page}} dossier in January 2017, the individual reports were one to three pages long, numbered, and page numbers 1–35 had been handwritten at the bottom. All but one had a typed date at the bottom. Each of the first 16 reports (pre-election memoranda<ref name="Judiciary.uk_10/30/2020" />) was assigned a typed number in the heading between 80 and 135, but the numeric order didn't always match the chronological order. The 17th report, known as the "December memo",<ref name="Judiciary.uk_10/30/2020">{{cite web |date=October 30, 2020 |title=''Gubarev v Orbis'' Case No: QB-2017-002808 |publisher=[[Judiciaries of the United Kingdom|Courts and Tribunals Judiciary]] |url=https://www.judiciary.uk/wp-content/uploads/2020/10/Gubarev-v-Orbis-judgment.pdf |access-date=December 18, 2020}}</ref> was numbered 166.<ref name="Bump_10/25/2017">{{cite news |last=Bump |first=Philip |title=What the Trump dossier says—and what it doesn't |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=October 25, 2017 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/politics/wp/2017/10/25/what-the-trump-dossier-says-and-what-it-doesnt/ |access-date=May 9, 2018}}</ref> Of the original reports numbered 1–166, only certain reports were used for the dossier, and it is unknown what happened with the content of the other reports: "For example, the first report is labeled as '080', with no indication given as to where the original 79 antecedents might have gone. The second report is then labeled '086', creating yet another mystery as to 81 through 85, and what content they might contain that would otherwise bolster or contextualize what came before or what follows."<ref name="Weiss_Fitzpatrick_7/25/2018">{{cite web |last1=Weiss |first1=Michael |last2=Fitzpatrick |first2=Catherine A. |title=Dissecting the Trump–Russia Dossier | website=[[Coda Media|Coda Story]] |date=July 25, 2018 |url=https://codastory.com/disinformation-crisis/information-war/dissecting-the-trump-russia-dossier |access-date=August 21, 2018}}</ref> In September 2017, Steele revealed he "intentionally left sequential gaps in the numbering of his election-related reports to obscure the actual number of reports being produced on the subject as well as to obscure the timing of the reports."<ref name="FBI_Steele_9/18-19/2017">{{cite web | title=FBI interview of Christopher Steele at Grosvenor Hotel, London, September 18 & 19, 2017 | url=https://justthenews.com/sites/default/files/2025-04/2025.04.10%20-%20Christopher%20Steele%20Binder.pdf | access-date=April 14, 2025 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411141350/https://justthenews.com/sites/default/files/2025-04/2025.04.10%20-%20Christopher%20Steele%20Binder.pdf | archive-date=April 11, 2025 | url-status=live }}</ref>{{rp|16}}
Each report started with a page heading in the same style as the first one shown here: {{quote box | border = 2px | align = left | bgcolor = white | title = | halign = left | quote = {{center|CONFIDENTIAL/SENSITIVE SOURCE}}<br /><br /> COMPANY INTELLIGENCE REPORT 2016/080<br /><br /> US PRESIDENTIAL ELECTION: REPUBLICAN CANDIDATE DONALD TRUMP'S<br /> ACTIVITIES IN RUSSIA AND COMPROMISING RELATIONSHIP WITH THE<br /> KREMLIN<ref name="Harding_11/15/2017" /> |salign = left}} {{clear}} Each report was in two parts, starting with a Summary section, followed by a Detail section. The Summary contained summary paragraphs based on numbered and expanded content and findings in the following Detail section.
In September 2017, the FBI interviewed Steele, revealing that:
# "The commentary labeled 'Company Comments' was generated by FUSION GPS."<ref name="FBI_Steele_9/18-19/2017" />{{rp|15}} # Report 2016/086: The date "26 July 2015", was a typo and the year should have been 2016.<ref name="FBI_Steele_9/18-19/2017" />{{rp|23}} # Report 2016/086: "STEELE advised that this cyber-related report in the dossier had been tasked by the FBI. It had been tasked by MICHAEL GAETA and was passed to the FBI."<ref name="FBI_Steele_9/18-19/2017" />{{rp|23}} # He gave the reports in the "original form" he prepared them "to the client, SIMPSON. The handwritten dates on the face of each report are the dates that they were received by the handling" Assistant Legal Attaché, FBI agent Gaeta.<ref name="FBI_Steele_9/18-19/2017" />{{rp|175}} # The interview includes unredacted copies of most of the dossier reports.<ref name="FBI_Steele_9/18-19/2017" />{{rp|113–134, 139}} # The interview includes content from Cody Shearer that includes descriptions of other alleged sexual ''kompromat''.<ref name="FBI_Steele_9/18-19/2017" />{{rp|134–138}}
=== Legal status and comparison to Trump Tower meeting ===
{{main|Trump Tower meeting}} The legal status of the dossier has been questioned,<ref name="Bump_8/6/2018">{{cite news |last=Bump |first=Philip |title=Why the Trump Tower meeting may have violated the law—and the Steele dossier likely didn't |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=August 6, 2018 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/politics/wp/2018/08/06/why-the-trump-tower-meeting-may-have-violated-the-law-and-the-steele-dossier-likely-didnt/ |access-date=August 4, 2020}}</ref> but, because of the legal difference between an [[Campaign finance in the United States|"expenditure" ''by'' a campaign and a "contribution" ''to'' a campaign]], it does not run afoul of [[Federal Election Commission]] laws (52 U.S. Code § 30121) forbidding foreign nationals from contributing to or aiding political campaigns, and that applies to any form of aid, not just cash donations.<ref name="Bump_8/6/2018" /> The dossier (prepared by a British citizen indirectly hired by the Clinton campaign and DNC) and the 2016 [[Trump Tower meeting]] (involving a direct offer of aid by the Russian government to the Trump campaign) are frequently contrasted and conflated in this regard.<ref name="Bump_8/6/2018" /><ref name="CLC_10/30/2017">{{cite web | title=Fact Check: Sarah Sanders Inaccurately States Law Regarding Campaigns' Ability to Finance Opposition Research | website=[[Campaign Legal Center]] | date=October 30, 2017 | url=https://campaignlegal.org/press-releases/fact-check-sarah-sanders-inaccurately-states-law-regarding-campaigns-ability-finance | access-date=November 12, 2023}}</ref>
Philip Bump has explained "why the Trump Tower meeting may have violated the law—and the Steele dossier likely didn't":<ref name="Bump_8/6/2018" /> "Hiring a foreign party to conduct research is very different, including in legal terms, than being given information by foreign actors seeking to influence the election. What's more, Trump's campaign did accept foreign assistance in 2016, as the investigation by special counsel Robert S. Mueller III determined."<ref name="Bump_8/3/2020" /><ref name="Editorial_Board_8/19/2020">{{cite news |author=<!--The Editorial Board--> |date=August 19, 2020 |title=The Trump Campaign Accepted Russian Help to Win in 2016. Case Closed.<!--omit standfirst – headline only--> |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/08/19/opinion/trump-russia-2016-report.html |access-date=August 19, 2022 |quote=The simplicity of the scheme has always been staring us in the face: Donald Trump's 2016 campaign sought and maintained close contacts with Russian government officials who were helping him get elected. The Trump campaign accepted their offers of help. The campaign secretly provided Russian officials with key polling data. The campaign coordinated the timing of the release of stolen information to hurt Hillary Clinton's campaign.}}</ref>
The Trump Tower meeting involved a voluntary offer of aid ("a campaign contribution")<ref name="Bump_8/6/2018" /> to the Trump campaign from the Russian government, and the offer was thus illegal to accept in any manner. Already before the meeting, the Trump campaign knew the source and purpose of the offer of aid, still welcomed the offer, successfully hid it for a year, and when the meeting was finally exposed, Trump issued a deceptive press release about it.<ref name="Pilkington_8/1/2017">{{cite news| last=Pilkington |first=Ed |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2017/jul/31/trump-statement-donald-trump-jr-russia-meeting |title=Trump personally crafted son's misleading account of Russia meeting |date=August 1, 2017|newspaper=[[The Guardian]] |access-date=August 19, 2022}}</ref><ref name="Dolak_Faulders_7/31/2017">{{cite news |last1=Dolak |first1=Kevin |last2=Faulders |first2=Katherine |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/trump-dictated-sons-misleading-statement-meeting-russian-lawyer/story?id=48958619 |title=Trump dictated son's misleading statement on meeting with Russian lawyer |date=July 31, 2017 |work=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] |access-date=August 19, 2022}}</ref>
By contrast, Steele's work was a legal campaign expense<ref name="Bump_8/6/2018" /> and did not involve any voluntary offer of aid to the Clinton campaign from the Russian government. [[Federal Election Commission|FEC]] law allows such campaign expenditures (properly declared), even if the aid is performed by foreigners.<ref name="Bump_8/6/2018" />
Bump explains that:<ref name="Bump_8/6/2018" /> {{blockquote| President Trump has deliberately and regularly conflated the two, arguing that the former meeting was innocuous and that the real malfeasance—the real collusion—was between Clinton's campaign and those Russians who were speaking to Steele. Trump is incorrect. There is no reason to think that Clinton's campaign is culpable for any illegal act related to the employment of Steele and good reason to think that the law was broken around the meeting at Trump Tower—and that members of the Trump team might face legal consequences.}}
In September 2018, the Virginia-based entity ''Coolidge Reagan Foundation'' filed a complaint at the FEC against the Clinton campaign, DNC, Perkins Coie, and Fusion GPS, for having "violated campaign finance law by conspiring with foreign nationals, including current and former members of the Russian government, to manipulate the results of the 2016 election".<ref name="CRFtoDCcourt">{{cite web |url=https://www.fec.gov/resources/cms-content/documents/crf_complaint.pdf |title=Verified complaint |access-date=May 5, 2024 |date=May 22, 2019 |first=Dan |last=Backer }}</ref> The complaint contained seven points, mostly about the alleged conspiracy with foreigners, but also about hiding the alleged crimes by misrepresenting the costs as 'legal expenses' to Perkins Coie, and of the latter acting as a middle man for these expenses.<ref name="CRFtoDCcourt" />{{rp|25−32}}
After due investigation, and some conciliation negotiation, in March 2022, the FEC dismissed most of the charges (including all alleged violations of 52 U.S. Code § 30121).<ref name=FECtoCRF_2022>{{cite web |url=https://static1.squarespace.com/static/6243b3f8b001843f2379a673/t/624486ac6da88f37bd43e98d/1648658094980/MUR+7449+closing+letter+to+Coolidge+Reagan+Foundation.pdf |title=RE: MUR 7449 |work=Letter to the Coolidge Reagan Foundation |first=Richard L. |last=Weiss |access-date=May 5, 2024 |date=March 29, 2022}}</ref>{{unreliable source?|certain=y|reason=Squarespace is a generic host, anyone can put anything there. Link to a reliable source's landing page for the file instead|date=March 2025}} However, the FEC did find "probable cause to believe" that the DNC and the Clinton campaign (and their treasurers) had "[misreported] the purpose of certain disbursements".<ref name=FECtoCRF_2022/> In a settlement, the FEC fined the DNC $105,000 and the Clinton campaign $8,000 for misreporting those fees and expenses as "legal services" and "legal and compliance consulting" rather than "opposition research". The DNC and the Clinton campaign agreed not to contest the fines, but did not admit having broken the rules.<ref name="Scott_3/30/2022">{{cite news | last=Scott | first=Eugene | title=FEC fines DNC, Clinton for violating rules in funding Steele dossier | newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] | date=March 30, 2022 | url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2022/03/30/fec-dnc-clinton-campaign/ | access-date=March 31, 2022}}</ref><ref name="Cohen_3/30/2022">{{cite news | last=Cohen | first=Marshall | title=FEC fines Hillary Clinton campaign and DNC over Trump-Russia dossier research | work=[[CNN]] | date=March 30, 2022 | url=https://www.cnn.com/2022/03/30/politics/clinton-dnc-steele-dossier-fusion-gps/index.html | access-date=March 31, 2022}}</ref>
[[Stephen Holmes (CIA)|Steven L. Hall]], former CIA chief of Russia operations, has contrasted Steele's methods with those of Donald Trump Jr., who sought information from a Russian attorney at a meeting in Trump Tower in June 2016: "The distinction: Steele spied against Russia to get info Russia did not want released; Don Jr took a mtg to get info Russians wanted to give."<ref name="Carter_10/27/2017">{{cite news |last=Carter |first=Brandon |title=CIA's ex-Russia chief: Unlike Steele, Trump Jr. took info Russia wanted to give |newspaper=[[The Hill (newspaper)|The Hill]] |date=October 27, 2017 |url=https://thehill.com/blogs/blog-briefing-room/news/357602-ex-cia-russia-chief-unlike-trump-dossier-russia-wanted-to-give |access-date=December 27, 2017}}</ref>
[[Jane Mayer]] referred to the same meeting and contrasted the difference in reactions to Russian attempts to support Trump: When Donald Trump Jr. was offered "dirt" on Clinton as "part of Russia and its government's support for Mr. Trump", instead of "going to the F.B.I., as Steele had" when he learned Russia was helping Trump, Trump's son accepted the support by responding: "If it's what you say, I love it."<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" />
=== Legacy ===
Glenn Simpson believes the dossier interrupted a planned renewal of [[Russia–United States relations|relationships between the United States and Russia]] that was "not in the interest of the United States"; that it supported the existing FBI investigation into Russian interference; and that it furthered understanding of the hidden relationship between the Russian government and the Trump campaign.<ref name="Burleigh_12/18/2019" /> Jane Mayer believes the dossier is "perhaps the most controversial opposition research ever to emerge from a Presidential campaign",<ref name="Mayer_11/25/2019" /> and [[Julian Borger]] described it as "one of the most explosive documents in modern political history".<ref name="Borger_10/7/2017" />
T. A. Frank has described the dossier as a "case study" in [[media manipulation]].<ref name="Frank_7/14/2021">{{cite magazine |first=T. A. |last=Frank |title=The Steele Dossier Was a Case Study in How Reporters Get Manipulated |date=July 14, 2021 |magazine=[[New York (magazine)|New York]] |url=https://nymag.com/intelligencer/2021/07/steele-dossier-was-case-study-in-journalistic-manipulation.html |access-date=October 3, 2021 |quote=[A] real driving force for me in writing the book was taking on the Steele dossier as a case study in how reporters can get manipulated or allow themselves to be manipulated and the havoc that results from that.}}</ref> In his 2021 book ''Spooked: The Trump Dossier, Black Cube, and the Rise of Private Spies'', former ''New York Times'' journalist Barry Meier commented: "media organizations didn't conduct internal postmortems or public reconstructions about how they handle the dossier story [because] it would have required them to disclose the toxic relationship that had developed between journalists and private spies."<ref name="Meier_Spooked">{{cite book |last=Meier |first=Barry |title=Spooked: The Trump Dossier, Black Cube, and the Rise of Private Spies |publisher=[[Harper (publisher)|Harper]] |year=2021 |isbn=978-0062950680}}</ref>{{rp|242}}
Journalist David Corn of ''Mother Jones'' described the dossier as "a convenient foil, their false flag" for the "Trump gang":<ref name="Corn_11/19/2021" /> {{blockquote| It was a clever ploy on the part of the Trump gang: Deny the unfounded—that Trump was caught on tape consorting with urinating prostitutes and that he conspired directly with Putin—to sidestep the damning reality that Trump and his aides betrayed the nation by both encouraging the Russian attack and trying to cover up Putin's sinister intervention.}}
According to [[Tommy Vietor]], "once a narrative enters the media ether, it can become uncontrollable ... once a rumor gets some traction, it's almost impossible to fix it, even if it is false. The problem with the pee tape allegation is it is so graphic, it is so memorable, that it doesn't matter how many times you knock it down – people are going to remember it."<ref name="Parker_4/13/2018">{{cite news | last=Parker | first=Ashley | title=Real or 'fake news'? Either way, allegations of lewd tape pose challenge for Trump | newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] | date=April 13, 2018 | url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/real-or-fake-news-either-way-lewd-tape-allegations-pose-a-challenge-for-trump/2018/04/13/098cdedc-3f2b-11e8-8d53-eba0ed2371cc_story.html | access-date=December 20, 2023}}</ref>
As long as it is not released, the alleged pee tape has a lasting effect. When Steele was asked why the Russians had not released the tape, he replied "It hasn't needed to be released. ... I think the Russians felt they'd got pretty good value out of Donald Trump when he was president of the U.S."<ref name="Bruggeman_Mosk_10/17/2021" />
The founders of [[Fusion GPS]] have written: "Ultimately, whether the incident detailed in the dossier is true or not is likely not of paramount importance. The Russians had ample ''[[kompromat]]'' against Trump and his top aides with or without any pee tape."<ref name="Simpson_Fritsch_11/26/2019" />
== Authorship and sources ==
The dossier is based on information from witting and unwitting [[Source (journalism)#Anonymous source|anonymous sources]] known to counterintelligence specialist [[Christopher Steele]].<ref name="Harding_Sabbagh_11/1/2019" /> Some were later revealed.
=== Christopher Steele ===
When [[CNN]] reported the existence of the dossier on January 10, 2017,<ref name="Perez_Sciutto_Tapper_Bernstein_1/10/2017" /><ref name="Nichols_Dilanian_1/11/2017">{{cite news |last1=Nichols |first1=Hans |author-link1=Hans Nichols |last2=Dilanian |first2=Ken |author-link2=Ken Dilanian |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/news/us-news/former-british-spy-christopher-steele-prepared-explosive-trump-memo-n705891 |title=Former British Spy Christopher Steele Prepared Explosive Trump Memo |work=[[NBC News]] |date=January 11, 2017 |access-date=January 12, 2017}}</ref> it did not name the author of the dossier, but revealed that he was British. Steele concluded that his anonymity had been "fatally compromised", and, realizing it was "only a matter of time until his name became public knowledge", fled into hiding with his family in fear of "a prompt and potentially dangerous backlash against him from Moscow".<ref name="Staff_1/12/2017">{{cite news |author=<!--Not stated--> |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2017/jan/12/christopher-steele-ex-mi6-officer-named-as-author-of-trump-dossier |title=Christopher Steele, ex-MI6 officer, named as author of Trump dossier |date=January 12, 2017 |newspaper=[[The Guardian]] |access-date=January 18, 2017}}</ref><ref name="Wood_1/12/2017">{{cite news |last=Wood |first=Paul |author-link=Paul Wood (journalist) |title=Trump memos ex-MI6 man 'in hiding' |work=[[BBC News]] |date=January 12, 2017 |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-38591382 |access-date=February 11, 2018}}</ref> His friends later reported that he feared assassination by Russians.<ref name="Riley-Smith_2/20/2020">{{cite news |last=Riley-Smith |first=Ben |title=Exclusive: Christopher Steele feared Russian assassination after discovering explosive Trump-Kremlin claims |newspaper=[[The Daily Telegraph]] |date=February 20, 2020 |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/2020/02/20/exclusive-christopher-steele-feared-russian-assassination-discovering/ |access-date=February 21, 2020}}</ref> ''[[The Wall Street Journal]]'' revealed Steele's name the next day, on January 11.<ref name="Hope_Rothfeld_Cullison_1/11/2017">{{cite news |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/christopher-steele-ex-british-intelligence-officer-said-to-have-prepared-dossier-on-trump-1484162553 |title=Christopher Steele, Ex-British Intelligence Officer, Said to Have Prepared Dossier on Trump |date=January 11, 2017 |first1=Bradley |last1=Hope |first2=Michael |last2=Rothfeld |author-link2=Michael Rothfeld |first3=Alan |last3=Cullison |newspaper=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |access-date=January 18, 2017}}</ref> Orbis Business Intelligence Ltd, for whom Steele worked at the time the dossier was authored, and its director Christopher Burrows, a counterterrorism specialist,<ref name="Isikoff_Corn_3/17/2018" /> would not confirm or deny that Orbis had produced the dossier.<ref name="Nichols_Dilanian_1/11/2017" /><ref name="Shane_Confessore_Rosenberg_1/12/2017" /> On March 7, 2017, as some members of the [[U.S. Congress]] were expressing interest in meeting with or hearing testimony from Steele, he reemerged after weeks in hiding, appearing publicly on camera and stating, "I'm really pleased to be back here working again at the Orbis's offices in London today."<ref name="Withnall_3/7/2017">{{cite news |last=Withnall |first=Adam |title=Ex-British spy Christopher Steele breaks silence over Trump Russia dossier |newspaper=[[The Independent]] |date=March 7, 2017 |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/home-news/donald-trump-russia-dossier-christopher-steele-ex-spy-british-a7616326.html |access-date=March 26, 2017}}</ref>
Called by the media a "highly regarded Kremlin expert" and "one of MI6's greatest Russia specialists", Steele formerly worked for the British intelligence agency [[MI6]] for 22 years, including four years as "an undercover agent"<ref name="Scheuermann_Schmitt_2/7/2019" /> at the British embassy in Moscow,<ref name="Harding_Sabbagh_11/1/2019">{{cite news |last1=Harding |first1=Luke |author-link1=Luke Harding |last2=Sabbagh |first2=Dan |title=Trump–Russia dossier author gave evidence to UK intrusion inquiry |newspaper=[[The Guardian]] |date=November 1, 2019 |url=https://www.theguardian.com/uk-news/2019/nov/01/trump-russia-dossier-author-gave-evidence-to-uk-intrusion-inquiry |access-date=November 1, 2019}}</ref> and headed MI6's Russia Desk for three years at the end of his MI6 career. He entered MI6 in 1987, directly after his graduation from [[Cambridge University]].<ref name="Hughes_Robson_1/12/2017">{{cite news |url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/news/uk-news/picture-ex-mi6-officer-chris-9607903 |title=First picture of British spy behind Donald Trump 'dirty dossier' revealed |newspaper=[[Daily Mirror]] |last1=Hughes |first1=Chris |last2=Robson |first2=Steve |date=January 12, 2017 |access-date=February 11, 2018}}</ref> He currently works for Orbis Business Intelligence Ltd, a private intelligence company he co-founded in London.<ref name="Neubert_Dilanian_Vinograd_Connor_1/13/2017">{{cite news |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/news/us-news/christopher-steele-trump-dossier-author-real-life-james-bond-n706376 |title=The ex-spy who wrote the Trump dossier is nicknamed James Bond |first1=Michele |last1=Neubert |first2=Ken |last2=Dilanian |author-link2=Ken Dilanian |first3=Cassandra |last3=Vinograd |first4=Tracy |last4=Connor |date=January 13, 2017 |work=[[NBC News]] |access-date=February 11, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Behar_1/11/2017">{{cite magazine |last=Behar |first=Richard |title=Could This Be The British Ex-MI6 Agent Behind The Trump FBI Memos? |magazine=[[Forbes]] |date=January 11, 2017 |url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/richardbehar/2017/01/11/could-this-be-the-british-mi6-agent-behind-the-trump-fbi-memos/ |access-date=February 11, 2018}}</ref>
Sir Andrew Wood, the former British ambassador to Moscow, has vouched for Steele's reputation.<ref name="Sengupta_1/13/2017" /> He views Steele as a "very competent professional operator. ... I take the report seriously. I don't think it's totally implausible." He also said "the report's key allegation—that Trump and Russia's leadership were communicating via secret back channels during the presidential campaign—was eminently plausible".<ref name="Harding_Hopkins_1/13/2017">{{cite news |last1=Harding |first1=Luke |author-link1=Luke Harding |last2=Hopkins |first2=Nick |author-link2=Nick Hopkins |date=January 13, 2017 |title=UK's former Moscow ambassador in spotlight over Trump dossier |newspaper=[[The Guardian]] |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2017/jan/13/uk-british-former-moscow-ambassador-andrew-wood-trump-dossier |access-date=January 18, 2017}}</ref> FBI investigators reportedly treat Steele "as a peer", whose experience as a trusted Russia expert has included assisting the [[United States Department of Justice|Justice Department]], British prime ministers, and at least one U.S. president.<ref name="Hamburger_Helderman_2/6/2018">{{cite news |last1=Hamburger |first1=Tom |author-link1=Tom Hamburger |last2=Helderman |first2=Rosalind S. |author-link2=Rosalind S. Helderman |title=Hero or hired gun? How a British former spy became a flash point in the Russia investigation |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=February 6, 2018 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/hero-or-hired-gun-how-a-british-former-spy-became-a-flash-point-in-the-russia-investigation/2018/02/06/94ea5158-0795-11e8-8777-2a059f168dd2_story.html |access-date=March 10, 2018}}</ref>
Steele's biases and motivations toward Trump appear to have changed over time. Starting in 2007, many years before he started his opposition research on Trump, he repeatedly met [[Ivanka Trump]] over several years, had a "friendly relationship" with her, and was "favorably disposed" to the Trump family. They even discussed the possibility of the Trump Organization using the services of Orbis Business Intelligence, but no arrangements were made.<ref name="Macfarlane_12/9/2019">{{cite news |last=Macfarlane |first=Julia |title='Dossier' author Chris Steele met Ivanka Trump years before Russia scandal, source says |work=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] |date=December 9, 2019 |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/dossier-author-chris-steele-met-ivanka-trump-years/story?id=67597270 |access-date=December 11, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Szalai_12/10/2019">{{cite news |last=Szalai |first=Jennifer |title='Crime in Progress' Tells the Story Behind the Steele Dossier |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=December 10, 2019 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/12/10/books/review-crime-in-progress-steele-dossier-fusion-gps-donald-trump.html |access-date=December 11, 2019}}</ref> Simpson has also confirmed that "there was no pre-existing animus toward Trump by Steele or Fusion".<ref name="Burleigh_12/18/2019">{{cite news |last=Burleigh |first=Nina |date=December 18, 2019 |title=Fusion GPS' Glenn Simpson on the Steele Dossier, the FBI's Trump Investigation and Life as a Fox News Pinata |work=[[Newsweek]] |url=https://www.newsweek.com/fusion-gps-glenn-simpson-steele-dossier-fbis-trump-investigation-life-fox-news-pinata-1478091 |access-date=December 19, 2019}}</ref>
Later, as Steele was preparing the dossier before the 2016 election, [[Bruce Ohr]] said Steele, based on what he learned during his research,<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|280}} told him he "was desperate that Donald Trump not get elected and was passionate about him not being president" (a quote disputed by Steele<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />),<ref name="Riley-Smith_Sabur_2/2/2018">{{cite news |last1=Riley-Smith |first1=Ben |last2=Sabur |first2=Rozina |title=Secret memo on FBI states British spy was desperate to stop Donald Trump getting elected |newspaper=[[The Daily Telegraph]] |date=February 2, 2018 |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/2018/02/02/donald-trump-approves-release-controversial-memo/ |access-date=December 11, 2019}}</ref> attitudes that have been described by [[Julian Sanchez (writer)|Julian Sanchez]] as "entirely natural, not suggestive of preexisting bias", considering Steele believed his own reporting.<ref name="Sanchez_12/11/2019">{{cite web |last=Sanchez |first=Julian |title=The Crossfire Hurricane Report's Inconvenient Findings |publisher=[[New York University School of Law|Just Security]] |date=December 11, 2019 |url=https://www.justsecurity.org/67691/the-crossfire-hurricane-reports-inconvenient-findings/ |access-date=December 23, 2019}}</ref> Steele has disputed Ohr's statement, and he told interviewers for Inspector General Horowitz that Ohr's wording was a paraphrase of his sentiments and not an exact quote, and the IG Report continues: "Steele told us that based on what he learned during his research he was concerned that Trump was a national security risk and he had no particular animus against Trump otherwise."<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|280}} Steele told the FBI that he "did not begin his investigation with any bias against Trump, but based on the information he learned during the investigation became very concerned about the consequences of a Trump presidency."<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|193}}
Steele first became a CHS for the FBI in 2013 in connection with the investigation in the [[2015 FIFA corruption case]], but he considered the relationship as contractual. He said the relationship "was never really resolved and both sides turned a blind eye to it. It was not really ideal."<ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" />{{rp|904}} Later, the [[Inspector General report on the Crossfire Hurricane investigation]] discusses "divergent expectations about Steele's conduct in connection with his election reporting", as Steele considered his first duty to his paying clients, and not to the FBI. The Inspector General's report states that "Steele contends that he was never a CHS for the FBI but rather that his consulting firm had a contractual relationship with the FBI." Steele said "he never recalled being told that he was a CHS and that he never would have accepted such an arrangement". This divergence in expectations was a factor that "ultimately resulted in the FBI formally closing Steele as a CHS in November 2016 (although ... the FBI continued its relationship with Steele through Ohr)."<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|88}}
On May 3, 2021, ''[[The Daily Telegraph]]'' reported that Steele and Orbis Business Intelligence, using new sources not used for the original dossier, continued to supply the FBI with raw intelligence during the Trump presidency. During an interview with the FBI in September 2017, Steele informed the FBI that Orbis had "four discrete, 'hermetically-sealed' main agent networks". His primary sub-source for the dossier was no longer "active" at the time of the interview with FBI agents, but that another "main agent network is up and running and is now starting to get good information".<ref name="Mendick_Fisher_5/3/2021">{{cite news |last1=Mendick |first1=Robert |last2=Fisher |first2=Lucy |title=MI6 spy Christopher Steele 'produced second dossier on Donald Trump for FBI' |newspaper=[[The Daily Telegraph]] |date=May 3, 2021 |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/2021/05/03/mi6-spy-christopher-steele-produced-second-dossier-donald-trump/ |access-date=May 5, 2021}}</ref> This resulted in "a second dossier for the FBI on Donald Trump". It included further claims of Russian election meddling; "alleged Russian interference linked to Mr Trump and his associates"; claims about the "existence of further sex tapes"; and "further details of Mr Manafort's alleged Russian contacts".<ref name="Mendick_Fisher_5/3/2021" />
=== Steele's sources ===
{{see also|#Discrepancies between sources and their allegations}} The Inspector General's report stated that "Steele himself was not the originating source of any of the factual information in his reporting."<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019">{{cite web |author=Office of the Inspector General U.S. Department of Justice |date=December 9, 2019 |title=Review of Four FISA Applications and Other Aspects of the FBI's Crossfire Hurricane Investigation | website=[[United States Department of Justice|Justice Department]] | url=https://www.justice.gov/storage/120919-examination.pdf |access-date=December 9, 2019 |author-link=United States Department of Justice Office of the Inspector General}}</ref>{{rp|186}} Instead, the report found that Steele relied on a "Primary Sub-source", later revealed as [[Igor Danchenko]],<ref name="Kranish_11/23/2021">{{cite news | last=Kranish | first=Michael | title=Steele dossier sources, methods face new scrutiny after indictment of key figure | newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] | date=November 23, 2021 | url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2021/11/23/steele-dossier-igor-danchenko-indictment-scrutiny/ | access-date=December 14, 2021}}</ref> who "used a network of [further] sub-sources to gather the information that was relayed to Steele".<ref name="Sobel_4/24/2020">{{cite web |last=Sobel |first=Nathaniel |title=What Is and Isn't New in the Unredacted Footnotes From the Inspector General's Russia Report | website=[[Lawfare (website)|Lawfare]] |date=April 24, 2020 |url=https://www.lawfaremedia.org/what-and-isnt-new-unredacted-footnotes-inspector-generals-russia-report |access-date=June 13, 2020}}</ref>
District Judge [[Anthony Trenga]] analyzed the question of Steele's sources and acknowledged that Steele had other sources than Danchenko. He countered Special Counsel [[John Durham]]'s contention "that Danchenko was Steele's primary source of information for the Steele Reports writ large" by noting that Steele used other sources than Danchenko: "Nor is there any evidence that ... Steele only, or almost entirely, used Danchenko as his source for the Reports."<ref name="USvDanchenko_10/4/2022">{{cite web | date=October 4, 2022 | title=ORDER: United States of America v. Igor Y. Danchenko, Case No. 1:21-cr-245-AJT-1 | website=[[United States District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia]], Alexandria Division | url=https://storage.courtlistener.com/recap/gov.uscourts.vaed.515692/gov.uscourts.vaed.515692.101.0_3.pdf}}</ref>
In an Alfa-Bank lawsuit, Steele revealed that he "did not rely on Danchenko alone, instead obtaining information from "one main source and a couple of subsidiary sources". According to Steele: "The dossier was {{sic|comprised |hide=y|of}} intelligence obtained from 3 sources and approximately 20 sub-sources."<ref name="Kranish_11/23/2021" /><ref name="Maddow_8/20/2018" />
Paul Wood, writing in ''[[The Spectator]]'', wrote: "Steele had '20 to 30' sources for the dossier and in two decades as a professional intelligence officer he had never seen such complete agreement by such a wide range of sources."<ref name="Wood_8/12/2020">{{cite magazine |last=Wood |first=Paul |author-link=Paul Wood (journalist) |title=Was the 'pee tape' a lie all along? |magazine=[[The Spectator]] |date=August 12, 2020 |url=https://www.spectator.co.uk/article/was-the-pee-tape-a-lie-all-along- |access-date=August 14, 2020}}</ref>
Simpson has said that, to his knowledge, Steele did not pay any of his sources.<ref name="Raju_Herb_Polantz_11/16/2017">{{cite news |last1=Raju |first1=Manu |last2=Herb |first2=Jeremy |last3=Polantz |first3=Katelyn |date=November 16, 2017 |title=Fusion GPS co-founder: Steele didn't pay sources for dossier on Trump |work=[[CNN]] |url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/11/15/politics/russia-investigation-fusion-gps-glenn-simpson-dossier/index.html |access-date=November 19, 2017}}</ref><ref name="Simpson_Fritsch_1/2/2018" /><ref name="Williams_Beavers_1/18/2018">{{cite news |last1=Williams |first1=Katie Bo |last2=Beavers |first2=Olivia |title='Steele dossier' firm suspected Trump–Russia money laundering |url=https://thehill.com/policy/national-security/369654-steele-dossier-firm-suspected-trump-russia-money-laundering |newspaper=[[The Hill (newspaper)|The Hill]] |date=January 18, 2018 |access-date=April 12, 2018}}</ref> According to investigative reporter [[Jane Mayer]] of ''[[The New Yorker]]'', Orbis has a large number of paid "collectors" (also called subsources) whose information came from a network of often unwitting sub-subsources. Since payment of these sub-subsources can be seen as bribery or might encourage exaggeration, they are unpaid.<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" /> Steele testified that these sub-subsources "were not paid and were not aware that their information was being passed to Orbis or Fusion GPS".<ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" />{{rp|865}}
According to British journalist [[Luke Harding]], Steele's sources were not new, but trusted, proven, and familiar sources.<ref name="NPR.org_11/21/2017">{{cite news |title=Journalist Investigating Trump And Russia Says 'Full Picture Is One Of Collusion' |work=[[NPR]] |date=November 21, 2017 |url=https://www.npr.org/2017/11/21/565654507/journalist-investigating-trump-and-russia-says-full-picture-is-one-of-collusion |access-date=February 17, 2018}}</ref> Howard Blum said Steele leaned on sources "whose loyalty and information he had bought and paid for over the years".<ref name="Blum_3/30/2017" /> Steele later informed the Inspector General's investigators that this "source network did not involve sources from his time as a former foreign government employee and was developed entirely in the period after he retired from governmental service." (Footnote 214)<ref name="Declassified_footnotes">{{cite web | last=Grenell | first=Richard A. | title=Declassified OIG Footnotes | url=https://www.grassley.senate.gov/imo/media/doc/2020-04-15%20ODNI%20to%20CEG%20RHJ%20(FISA%20Footnote%20Declassification).pdf | access-date=May 22, 2024}}</ref><ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|93}}
==== Igor Danchenko ====
In January 2017, the Primary Sub-source, later identified as Ukrainian-born and Russian-trained attorney [[Igor Danchenko]],<ref name="Goldman_Savage_7/25/2020" /> was contacted by the FBI for an interview. About a week and a half later, in exchange for [[legal immunity]], he agreed to answer questions about his working relationship with Steele, as well as his opinion on the accuracy of the Steele dossier.<ref name="Goldman_Savage_7/24/2020">{{cite news |last1=Goldman |first1=Adam |last2=Savage |first2=Charlie |author-link2=Charlie Savage (author) |date=July 24, 2020 |title=The F.B.I. Pledged to Keep a Source Anonymous. Trump Allies Aided His Unmasking | newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/07/25/us/politics/igor-danchenko-steele-dossier.html |access-date=July 25, 2020 |quote=The report blacked out Mr. Danchenko's name and other identifying information. But within two days, a post on a newly created blog entitled 'I Found the Primary Subsource' identified him, citing clues left visible in the F.B.I. document.}}</ref> He was interviewed for three days by the FBI<ref name="Savage_Goldman_7/17/2020">{{cite news |last1=Savage |first1=Charlie |author-link1=Charlie Savage (author) |last2=Goldman |first2=Adam |title=F.B.I. Agent in Russia Inquiry Saw Basis in Early 2017 to Doubt Dossier |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/07/17/us/politics/steele-dossier-peter-strzok.html |date=July 17, 2020 |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=July 18, 2020}}</ref> and said that Steele misstated or exaggerated certain information.<ref name="Savage_Goldman_7/17/2020" /> Danchenko has said "he did not know who Mr. Steele's client was at the time and considered himself a nonpartisan analyst and researcher".<ref name="Goldman_Savage_11/4/2021" />
