|publisher=Monoray |location=United Kingdom |isbn=978-1-913183-52-3 |pages=60–75}}</ref><ref name="Lakhta_member_charged">{{cite press release |date=September 10, 2020 |title=Russian Project Lakhta Member Charged with Wire Fraud Conspiracy |url=https://www.justice.gov/opa/pr/russian-project-lakhta-member-charged-wire-fraud-conspiracy |access-date=September 5, 2021 |website=justice.gov |archive-date=September 5, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210905220841/https://www.justice.gov/opa/pr/russian-project-lakhta-member-charged-wire-fraud-conspiracy |url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="Ross_Schwartz_Meek_12/15/2016">{{cite news |first1=Brian |last1=Ross |first2=Rhonda |last2=Schwartz |first3=James Gordon |last3=Meek |work=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/officials-master-spy-vladimir-putin-now-directly-linked/story?id=44210901 |title=Officials: Master Spy Vladimir Putin Now Directly Linked to US Hacking |date=December 15, 2016 |access-date=December 15, 2016 |archive-date=October 25, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201025114732/https://abcnews.go.com/International/officials-master-spy-vladimir-putin-now-directly-linked/story?id=44210901 |url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="Hosenball_8/19/2020">{{Cite news |last=Hosenball |first=Mark |author-link=Mark Hosenball |date=August 19, 2020 |title=Factbox: Key findings from Senate inquiry into Russian interference in 2016 U.S. election |work=[[Reuters]] |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-trump-russia-senate-findings-fact-idUSKCN25E2OY |access-date=September 5, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210905211317/https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-trump-russia-senate-findings-fact-idUSKCN25E2OY |archive-date=September 5, 2021 |url-status=live}}</ref> including an "influence campaign" to harm Clinton's electoral chances and "undermine public faith in the US democratic process", as well as ordering cyber attacks on the Democratic and Republican parties.<ref name="ODNI_1/6/2017">{{cite report | title=Background to 'Assessing Russian Activities and Intentions in Recent US Elections': The Analytic Process and Cyber Incident Attribution |url=https://www.dni.gov/files/documents/ICA_2017_01.pdf |date=January 6, 2017 |publisher=[[Office of the Director of National Intelligence]] |access-date=April 1, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Gaouette_1/10/2017">{{cite web | last=Gaouette | first=Nicole | title=FBI's Comey: Republicans also hacked by Russia | website=[[CNN]] | date=January 10, 2017 | url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/01/10/politics/comey-republicans-hacked-russia/index.html | access-date=March 21, 2023}}</ref> John Brennan and James Clapper testified to Congress that Steele's dossier "played no role" in the intelligence community assessment about Russian interference in the 2016 election,<ref name="Benner_Barnes_12/19/2019">{{cite web |last1=Benner |first1=Katie |author-link1=Katie Benner |last2=Barnes |first2=Julian E. |title=Durham Is Scrutinizing Ex-C.I.A. Director's Role in Russian Interference Findings |website=[[The New York Times]] |date=December 19, 2019 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/12/19/us/politics/durham-john-brennan-cia.html |access-date=December 22, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Samuels_2/4/018">{{cite web |last=Samuels |first=Brett |title=Ex-CIA chief: Steele dossier played no role in intelligence assessment on Russia's election interference |website=[[The Hill (newspaper)|The Hill]] |date=February 4, 2018 |url=https://thehill.com/homenews/sunday-talk-shows/372222-ex-cia-chief-steele-dossier-played-no-role-whatsoever-in/ |access-date=June 5, 2019 |archive-date=June 5, 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190605113908/https://thehill.com/homenews/sunday-talk-shows/372222-ex-cia-chief-steele-dossier-played-no-role-whatsoever-in |url-status=live }}</ref> testimony which was reaffirmed by an April 2020 bipartisan Senate Intelligence Committee report that found the dossier was not used to "support any of its analytic judgments".<ref name="Jalonick_Tucker_4/21/2020">{{cite news |last1=Jalonick |first1=Mary Clare |last2=Tucker |first2=Eric |title=Senate panel backs assessment that Russia interfered in 2016 |url=https://apnews.com/d094918c0421b872eac7dc4b16e613c7 |access-date=April 22, 2020 |agency=[[Associated Press]] |date=April 21, 2020}}</ref>
There were conflicting opinions between the FBI and CIA on whether to include any of the dossier's allegations in the body of the ICA report, with the FBI pushing for inclusion, and the CIA countering that the dossier "was not completely vetted and did not merit inclusion in the body of the report". After much discussion, the CIA prevailed,<!-- The following citation from the right-wing Washington Examiner is used here on a carefully evaluated case-by-case basis. This report is very sober, non-partisan, and contains no right-wing conspiracy theories, so it can be used. --><ref name="Dunleavy_12/9/2019">{{cite web | last=Dunleavy | first=Jerry | title=Comey and McCabe fought to include Steele dossier in intelligence assessment on Russian interference | website=[[Washington Examiner]] | date=December 9, 2019 | url=https://www.washingtonexaminer.com/news/663694/comey-and-mccabe-fought-to-include-steele-dossier-in-intelligence-assessment-on-russian-interference/ | access-date=September 26, 2024}}</ref> and the final ICA report only included a short summary of Steele's reporting in the "highly classified" Annex A.<ref name="Knutson_4/21/2020">{{cite web | last=Knutson | first=Jacob | title=Senate Intelligence Committee affirms that Russia interfered to help Trump in 2016 | website=[[Axios (website)|Axios]] | date=April 21, 2020 | url=https://www.axios.com/2020/04/21/senate-inteligence-committee-russia-trump | access-date=September 25, 2024 | quote=Worth noting: The report finds that U.S. intelligence agencies did not use information from the infamous Steele dossier to support its findings. The dossier was included in a highly classified annex to the assessment, which was in line with President Obama's directive.}}</ref><ref name="SICv4">{{cite web | publisher=[[Senate Intelligence Committee report on Russian interference in the 2016 United States presidential election|Senate Intelligence Committee]] (SIC) | date=August 18, 2020 | title=Volume 4: Review of the Intelligence Community Assessment | website=intelligence.senate.gov | url=https://www.intelligence.senate.gov/sites/default/files/documents/Report_Volume4.pdf | access-date=September 24, 2024 | quote=The Committee found that the information provided by Christopher Steele to FBI was not used in the body of the ICA or to support any of its analytic judgments. However, a summary of this material was included in Annex A as a compromise to FBI's insistence that the information was responsive to the presidential tasking.}}</ref>{{rp|7}}
There were also other reasons to not include it, and CNN wrote that:<ref name="Perez_Prokupecz_Brown_10/25/2017">{{cite web |last1=Perez |first1=Evan |last2=Prokupecz |first2=Shimon |last3=Brown |first3=Pamela |url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/10/05/politics/special-counsel-russia-dossier-christopher-steele/index.html |title=Mueller's team met with Russia dossier author |publisher=[[CNN]] |date=October 25, 2017 |accessdate=November 5, 2017}}</ref> {{blockquote| The intelligence agencies, particularly the CIA, and the FBI took Steele's research seriously enough that they kept it out of a publicly-released January report on Russian meddling in the election in order to not divulge which parts of the dossier they had corroborated and how. ... And if that report included the dossier allegations, the intelligence community would have to say which parts it had corroborated and how. That would compromise sources and methods, including information shared by foreign intelligence services, intelligence officials believed.}}
{{external media| float = right| video1 = [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=H4MZVBD8uWM 'Absurd': Fmr. CIA Director Brennan slams Trump's 'Russiagate' allegations],<br/>July 31, 2025, [[MSNBC]]<ref name="Brennan_MSNBC_7/31/2025">{{cite web | title='Absurd': Fmr. CIA Director Brennan slams Trump's 'Russiagate' allegations | publisher=[[MSNBC]] | via=[[YouTube]] | date=July 31, 2025 | url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=H4MZVBD8uWM | access-date=August 2, 2025}}</ref>}} In July 2025, when the [[Second presidency of Donald Trump|second Trump administration]] [[United States Department of Justice investigation into Crossfire Hurricane|resumed attacks]] on the dossier, John Brennan, and others who contributed to the ODNI assessment, Brennan and Clapper responded in ''The New York Times'':<ref name="i529">{{cite web | last1=Brennan | first1=John O. | last2=Clapper | first2=James | title=Brennan and Clapper: Let's Set the Record Straight on Russia and 2016 | website=[[The New York Times]] | date=July 30, 2025 | url=https://www.nytimes.com/2025/07/30/opinion/trump-obama-treason-russia-miller.html | access-date=August 2, 2025}}</ref> {{blockquote| [T]he dossier was not used as a source or taken into account for any of its analysis or conclusions. At the Federal Bureau of Investigation's insistence, a short summary of the dossier was added as a separate annex only to the most highly classified version of the document that contained the assessment. That annex also explained why the dossier was not used in the assessment.}}
Chuck Grassley provided that explanation: "We have only limited corroboration of source's [Steele's] reporting in this case and did not use it to reach the analytic conclusions of the CIA/FBI/NSA assessment."<ref name="Docs_25085">{{cite web | title=Documents related to 2017 ICA Assessment, Annex A. (John Ratcliffe response to Grassley and Johnson) | website=DocumentCloud | url=https://legacy.www.documentcloud.org/documents/26040686-250805-ica-annex/ | access-date=August 21, 2025}}</ref>
The ''New York Times'' said "Parts of the dossier have proved prescient."<ref name="Rosenberg_3/14/2019" /> {{blockquote| Its main assertion – that the Russian government was working to get Mr. Trump elected – was hardly an established fact when it was first laid out by Mr. Steele in June 2016. But it has since been backed up by the United States' own intelligence agencies – and Mr. Mueller's investigation. The dossier's talk of Russian efforts to cultivate some people in Mr. Trump's orbit was similarly unknown when first detailed in one of Mr. Steele's reports, but it has proved broadly accurate as well.}}
''[[Newsweek]]'' said "the dossier's main finding, that Russia tried to prop up Trump over Clinton, was confirmed" by the ODNI assessment.<ref name="Price_12/21/2017" /> [[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] stated that "some of the dossier's broad implications—particularly that Russian President Vladimir Putin launched an operation to boost Trump and sow discord within the U.S. and abroad—now ring true."<ref name="Levine_1/12/2018" />
The [[Mueller Report]] backed "Steele's central claim that the Russians ran a 'sweeping and systematic' operation ... to help Trump win".<ref name="Harding_Sabbagh_11/1/2019" /> James Comey said:<ref name="Bump_5/10/2019">{{cite news |last=Bump |first=Philip |title=The Deep State strikes back: Former FBI leaders rebut questions about the Russia investigation |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=May 10, 2019 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2019/05/10/deep-state-strikes-back-former-fbi-leaders-rebut-questions-about-russia-investigation/ |access-date=November 13, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Donn_6/29/2018" /> {{blockquote| The bureau began an effort after we got the Steele dossier to try and see how much of it we could replicate. That work was ongoing when I was fired. Some of it was consistent with our other intelligence, the most important part. The Steele dossier said the Russians are coming for the American election. It's a huge effort. It has multiple goals. ... And that was true.}}
''[[Lawfare (website)|Lawfare]]'' has noted that the "Mueller investigation has clearly produced public records that confirm pieces of the dossier. And even where the details are not exact, the general thrust of Steele's reporting seems credible in light of what we now know about extensive contacts between numerous individuals associated with the Trump campaign and Russian government officials."<ref name="Grant_Rosenberg_12/14/2018_2">{{cite web |last1=Grant |first1=Sarah |last2=Rosenberg |first2=Chuck |title=The Steele Dossier: A Retrospective | website=[[Lawfare (website)|Lawfare]] |date=December 14, 2018 |url=https://www.lawfaremedia.org/steele-dossier-retrospective |access-date=December 29, 2019}}</ref>
In ''[[The New Yorker]]'', Jane Mayer said the allegation that Trump was favored by the Kremlin, and that they offered Trump's campaign dirt on Clinton, has proven true.<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" /> Mayer also wrote that the CIA had a Russian government official working as "a human source inside the Russian government during the campaign, who provided information that dovetailed with Steele's reporting about Russia's objective of electing Trump and Putin's direct involvement in the operation."<ref name="Mayer_11/25/2019" /><ref name="APF_9/10/2019">{{cite web | title=Trump's Loose Lips Force US to Extract Spy From Kremlin | website=[[Agence France-Presse]] | date=September 10, 2019 | url=https://www.courthousenews.com/trumps-loose-lips-force-cia-to-extract-spy-from-kremlin/ | access-date=August 2, 2025}}</ref><ref name="Barnes_Goldman_Sanger_9/9/2019" /> The spy had access to Putin and could actually take pictures of documents on Putin's desk. Because of the dangers imposed by Trump's recent [[Donald Trump's disclosures of classified information|careless disclosures of classified information to Russian officials]], the CIA feared their spy was in danger, so the government official and his family were discreetly [[Extraction (military)|exfiltrated]] during a family vacation to Montenegro.<ref name="Hohmann_9/10/2019">{{cite news |last=Hohmann |first=James |title=The Daily 202: CIA exfiltration of Russian asset underscores the importance of human sources |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=September 10, 2019 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/powerpost/paloma/daily-202/2019/09/10/daily-202-cia-exfiltration-of-russian-asset-underscores-the-importance-of-human-sources/5d77182a88e0fa7bb93a8a51/ |access-date=November 27, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Sciutto_9/9/2019">{{cite news |last=Sciutto |first=Jim |title=Exclusive: US extracted top spy from inside Russia in 2017 |work=[[CNN]] |date=September 9, 2019 |url=https://www.cnn.com/2019/09/09/politics/russia-us-spy-extracted/index.html |access-date=November 27, 2019}}</ref>
In February 2019, Michael Cohen implicated Trump before the U.S. Congress, writing that in late July 2016, Trump had knowledge that [[Roger Stone]] was communicating with WikiLeaks about releasing emails stolen from the DNC in 2016.<ref name="Elfrink_Flynn_2/27/2019">{{cite news |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/nation/2019/02/27/i-am-ashamed-because-i-know-what-mr-trump-is-michael-cohen-reportedly-testify-that-trump-knew-wikileaks-plot/ |title=Michael Cohen to testify that Trump knew of WikiLeaks plot |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=February 27, 2019 |access-date=February 27, 2019 |first1=Tim |last1=Elfrink |first2=Meagan |last2=Flynn}}</ref><ref name="Daub_2/27/2019">{{cite news |url=https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/read-michael-cohens-full-prepared-testimony-on-trumps-russia-plans-wikileaks-email-dump |title=Read Michael Cohen's full prepared testimony on Trump's Russia plans, WikiLeaks email dump |work=[[PBS NewsHour]] |date=February 27, 2019 |access-date=February 27, 2019 |first=Travis |last=Daub}}</ref> Stone denied this and accused Cohen of lying to Congress.<ref name="Swan_2/27/2019">{{cite web |last=Swan |first=Betsy |title=Roger Stone Says Cohen Lied to Congress About WikiLeaks | website=[[The Daily Beast]] |date=February 27, 2019 |url=https://www.thedailybeast.com/roger-stone-says-cohen-lied-to-congress-about-wikileaks |access-date=May 24, 2020}}</ref> Stone was later convicted before being pardoned by Trump, and it was confirmed that Stone had been in contact with WikiLeaks.<ref name="Tucker_Long_Balsamo_4/29/2020" /><ref name="Samuelsohn_Gerstein_11/12/2019" />
At the [[2018 Russia–United States Summit|Helsinki summit meeting]] in July 2018, Putin was asked if he had wanted Trump to win the 2016 election. He responded "Yes, I did. Yes, I did. Because he talked about bringing the U.S.-Russia relationship back to normal."<ref name="Friedman_7/17/2018">{{cite magazine |last=Friedman |first=Uri |title=The White House Transcript Is Missing the Most Explosive Part of the Trump–Putin Press Conference |magazine=[[The Atlantic]] |date=July 17, 2018 |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/international/archive/2018/07/trump-putin-press-conference-transcript/565385/ |access-date=July 23, 2018}}</ref>
"That the Russians interfered in the election in hopes of helping Trump win" has been described as the dossier's "core theme – later widely accepted by the news media and the U.S. intelligence community".<ref name="Roig-Franzia_10/7/2024">{{cite news | last=Roig-Franzia | first=Manuel | title=The Steele dossier author promises more Trump dirt. Will anyone buy it? | newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] | date=October 7, 2024 | url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/books/2024/10/07/steele-dossier-christopher-steele-book/ | access-date=October 8, 2024}}</ref>
==== Fake news and social media misinformation ====
Source(s) for Report 166 alleged that "hackers ... had worked in Europe under Kremlin direction against the CLINTON campaign".
The [[Internet Research Agency]] (IRA) conducted an extensive campaign, including fake news and misinformation in social media, to undermine Clinton's campaign. On February 16, 2018, the IRA, along with 13 Russian individuals and two other Russian organizations, was indicted following an investigation by Special Counsel [[Robert Mueller]] with charges stemming from "impairing, obstructing, and defeating the lawful functions of government".<ref name="Swaine_Bennetts_2/17/2018">{{Cite news |last1=Swaine |first1=Jon | last2=Bennetts | first2=Marc | date=February 17, 2018 |title=Thirteen Russians criminally charged for interfering in US election, Mueller announces |work=[[The Guardian]] |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2018/feb/16/robert-mueller-russians-charged-election |access-date=October 22, 2023}}</ref>
John Sipher reported on this dossier allegation and the documentation of the Russian efforts to harm Clinton.<ref name="Sipher_9/6/2017" /> A January 2017 [[Stanford University]] study found that "fabricated stories favoring Donald Trump were shared a total of 30 million times, nearly quadruple the number of pro-Hillary Clinton shares leading up to the election."<ref name="Crawford_1/18/2017">{{cite web | last=Crawford | first=Krysten | title=Stanford study examines fake news and the 2016 presidential election | website=[[Stanford University|Stanford News]] | date=January 18, 2017 | url=https://news.stanford.edu/2017/01/18/stanford-study-examines-fake-news-2016-presidential-election/ | access-date=October 22, 2023}}</ref> Researchers at [[Oxford University]] found that "An automated army of pro-Donald J. Trump chatbots overwhelmed similar programs supporting Hillary Clinton five to one in the days leading up to the presidential election."<ref name="Markoff_11/17/2016">{{cite web | last=Markoff | first=John | title=Automated Pro-Trump Bots Overwhelmed Pro-Clinton Messages, Researchers Say | website=[[The New York Times]] | date=November 17, 2016 | url=https://www.nytimes.com/2016/11/18/technology/automated-pro-trump-bots-overwhelmed-pro-clinton-messages-researchers-say.html | access-date=October 22, 2023}}</ref> In March 2017, former FBI agent [[Clint Watts]] told Congress about websites involved in the Russian disinformation campaign "some of which mysteriously operate from Eastern Europe and are curiously led by pro-Russian editors of unknown financing".<ref name="OConnor_Schneider_4/3/2017 b393">{{cite web | last1=O'Connor | first1=Gabe | last2=Schneider | first2=Avie | title=How Russian Twitter Bots Pumped Out Fake News During The 2016 Election | website=[[NPR]] | date=April 3, 2017 | url=https://www.npr.org/sections/alltechconsidered/2017/04/03/522503844/how-russian-twitter-bots-pumped-out-fake-news-during-the-2016-election | access-date=October 22, 2023}}</ref> Aaron Blake examined two studies that indicate these efforts "made a significant difference...[and] may well have cost Clinton the presidency".<ref name="Blake_4/3/2018">{{cite news | last=Blake | first=Aaron | title=A new study suggests fake news might have won Donald Trump the 2016 election | newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] | date=April 3, 2018 | url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/the-fix/wp/2018/04/03/a-new-study-suggests-fake-news-might-have-won-donald-trump-the-2016-election/ | access-date=October 23, 2023}}</ref>
=== Manafort's and others' cooperation with Russian efforts ===
{{see also|Mueller report#Conspiracy or coordination vs collusion|Mueller special counsel investigation#Conspiracy vs collusion}} Dossier source(s) allege that Manafort, who had worked for Russian interests in Ukraine for many years,<ref name="Crowley_8/3/2016" /> "managed" a "well-developed conspiracy of co-operation between [the Trump campaign] and the Russian leadership".<ref name="Bertrand_1/15/2017" /><ref name="Borger_10/7/2017" /><ref name="Beauchamp_3/22/2017" /> The "conspiracy" is not proven, but the "co-operation" is seen as proven.
While the Mueller investigation did not "produce enough evidence"<ref name="O'Brien_4/1/2019">{{cite news |last=O'Brien |first=Timothy L. |title=No, Russiagate Isn't This Generation's WMD |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |date=April 1, 2019 |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2019-04-01/no-russiagate-isn-t-this-generation-s-wmd |access-date=December 16, 2019}}</ref> to prove the existence of a formal written or oral "conspiracy",<ref name="Mueller_report">{{cite web |first=Robert S. III |last=Mueller |date=March 2019 |title=Mueller Report: Report On The Investigation Into Russian Interference In The 2016 Presidential Election. Volumes I and II | website=[[United States Department of Justice|Justice Department]] |url=https://www.justice.gov/archives/sco/file/1373816/dl |access-date=April 2, 2023}}</ref><ref name="Morais_4/18/2019">{{cite magazine |last=Morais |first=Betsy |title=Collusion by any other name |date=April 18, 2019 |url=https://www.cjr.org/first_person/trump-mueller-collusion.php |magazine=[[Columbia Journalism Review]] |access-date=April 23, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Savage_4/20/2019">{{cite news |last1=Savage |first1=Charlie |author-link1=Charlie Savage (author) |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/04/19/us/politics/mueller-report-william-barr-excerpts.html |date=April 20, 2019 |title=How Barr's Excerpts Compare to the Mueller Report's Findings |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=April 21, 2019}}</ref> some consider the actions of Manafort,<ref name="Goodman_6/15/2018" /> Trump's welcoming of Russian help,<ref name="Megerian_4/18/2019">{{cite news |last=Megerian |first=Chris |title=Mueller finds no conspiracy, but report shows Trump welcomed Russian help |newspaper=[[Los Angeles Times]] |date=April 18, 2019 |url=https://www.latimes.com/politics/la-na-pol-mueller-report-trump-russia-collusion-20190418-story.html |access-date=December 10, 2019}}</ref> and the myriad secret contacts between other Trump campaign members and associates with Russians<ref name="Bertrand_2/11/2017" /><ref name="Yourish_Buchanan_1/26/2019">{{cite news |last1=Yourish |first1=Karen |last2=Buchanan |first2=Larry |title=Mueller Report Shows Depth of Connections Between Trump Campaign and Russians |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=January 26, 2019 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2019/01/26/us/politics/trump-contacts-russians-wikileaks.html |access-date=October 24, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Leonnig_Helderman_5/17/2019">{{cite news |last1=Leonnig |first1=Carol D. |author-link1=Carol D. Leonnig |last2=Helderman |first2=Rosalind S. |author-link2=Rosalind S. Helderman |title=Judge orders public release of what Michael Flynn said in call to Russian ambassador |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=May 17, 2019 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/judge-orders-public-release-of-what-michael-flynn-said-in-call-to-russian-ambassador/2019/05/16/1e68712a-7825-11e9-bd25-c989555e7766_story.html |access-date=May 17, 2019}}</ref> to be the alleged "co-operation" with the Russian's "'sweeping and systematic' operation in 2016 to help Trump win",<ref name="Harding_Sabbagh_11/1/2019" /> that ''The Guardian''{{'}}s Luke Harding and Dan Sabbagh describe as "Steele's central claim".<ref name="Harding_Sabbagh_11/1/2019" /><ref name="Bump_5/10/2019" />
CNN described Manafort's role in its report of intercepted communications among "suspected Russian operatives discussing their efforts to work with Manafort ... to coordinate information that could damage Hillary Clinton's election prospects. ... The suspected operatives relayed what they claimed were conversations with Manafort, encouraging help from the Russians."<ref name="Perez_Brown_Prokupecz_8/3/2017">{{cite news |last1=Perez |first1=Evan |last2=Brown |first2=Pamela |last3=Prokupecz |first3=Shimon |title=One year into the FBI's Russia investigation, Mueller is on the Trump money trail |work=[[CNN]] |date=August 3, 2017 |url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/08/03/politics/mueller-investigation-russia-trump-one-year-financial-ties/index.html |access-date=December 10, 2019}}</ref>
These reported intercepts are considered "remarkably consistent with the raw intelligence in the Steele Dossier ... [that] states that the 'well-developed conspiracy of co-operation between [the Trump campaign] and the Russian leadership ... was managed on the TRUMP side by the Republican candidate's campaign manager, Paul MANAFORT'."<ref name="Goodman_6/15/2018">{{cite web |last=Goodman |first=Ryan |title=Paul Manafort = Evidence of Collusion |publisher=[[New York University School of Law|Just Security]] |date=June 15, 2018 |url=https://www.justsecurity.org/57863/paul-manafort-evidence-collusion/ |access-date=December 10, 2019}}</ref>
=== Russian conversations confirmed ===
On February 10, 2017, CNN reported that:<ref name="Sciutto_Perez_2/10/2017">{{cite news | url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/02/10/politics/russia-dossier-update/index.html | title=US investigators corroborate some aspects of the Russia dossier | last1=Sciutto | first1=Jim | author-link1=Jim Sciutto | last2=Perez | first2=Evan | date=February 10, 2017 | agency=[[CNN]] | access-date=February 10, 2017}}</ref><ref name="Sciutto_Perez_YouTube_2/10/2017">{{cite web | last1=Sciutto | first1=Jim | author-link1=Jim Sciutto | last2=Perez | first2=Evan | title=Watch: US officials corroborate aspects of dossier | publisher=[[CNN]] | via=[[YouTube]] | date=February 10, 2017 | url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lE4s4Msk978 | access-date=August 4, 2025}}</ref><ref name="Perez_Prokupecz_Brown_10/25/2017" /> {{blockquote| For the first time, US investigators say they have corroborated some of the communications detailed in a 35-page dossier compiled by a former British intelligence agent, multiple current and former US law enforcement and intelligence officials tell CNN. ... The dossier details about a dozen conversations between senior Russian officials and other Russian individuals. ... But the intercepts do confirm that some of the conversations described in the dossier took place between the same individuals on the same days and from the same locations as detailed in the dossier, according to the officials. ... The corroboration, based on intercepted communications, has given US intelligence and law enforcement 'greater confidence' in the credibility of some aspects of the dossier as they continue to actively investigate its contents, these sources say.}}
=== ''Kompromat'' and "golden showers" allegations ===
[[Ashley Feinberg]], journalist for ''[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]'' magazine, has described the "unproved" verification status of the rumor:<ref name="Feinberg_9/25/2019" /> {{blockquote| The public first became aware of the item and/or concept we're referring to as the pee tape on Jan. 10, 2017 when ''[[BuzzFeed News|BuzzFeed]]'' first published the dossier, a collection of then-unverified intelligence memos alleging various connections between Trump and Russia. [[Steele dossier#Corroboration status with passage of time|A number of the claims have since proved true]], thanks to the Mueller report; others have not.<br/> The most salacious of the allegations—that Trump had had a urine-soaked run-in with Russian sex workers while staying in the presidential suite at the Ritz-Carlton in Moscow—falls into the unproved category.}}
After years of scrutiny, the rumor remains unproven: "neither conclusively corroborated nor conclusively disproven".<ref name="Bergmann_et_al_11/27/2018" /><ref name="Mitchell_10/14/2021">{{cite web | last=Mitchell | first=Taiyler S. | title=Trump brought up an unsubstantiated claim in the Steele dossier to the National Republican Senatorial Committee: 'I'm not into golden showers' | website=[[Business Insider]] | date=October 14, 2021 | url=https://www.businessinsider.com/trump-told-national-republican-senatorial-committee-not-into-golden-showers-2021-10 | access-date=July 23, 2024}}</ref>
==== Moscow weekend, November 8{{nbnd}}10, 2013 ====
Dossier source(s) allege that Trump "hated" Obama so much that when he stayed at the [[Ritz-Carlton Hotel Moscow|Ritz-Carlton Hotel in Moscow]], he hired the presidential suite (Report 80). Igor Danchenko alleges that "Trump was with some powerful Russian oligarchs, who brought the sex workers."<ref name="Forgrave_8/26/2024" /> There he had "a number of prostitutes to perform a 'golden showers' (urination) show in front of him"<ref name="Stein_1/10/2017" /><ref name="Price_12/21/2017" /><ref name="Prokop_4/15/2018" /> in order to defile the bed used by the Obamas on an earlier visit. The alleged incident from 2013 was reportedly filmed and recorded by the [[Federal Security Service|FSB]]<ref name="Sipher_9/6/2017" /> as ''kompromat''.<ref name="Parfitt_1/12/2017" /><ref name="Bertrand_11/10/2017" /> The [[Senate Intelligence Committee report on Russian interference in the 2016 United States presidential election|2020 Senate Intelligence Committee report]] assessed the Ritz-Carlton Hotel in Moscow as a "high counterintelligence risk environment" with Russian intelligence on staff, "government surveillance of guests' rooms", and the common presence of prostitutes, "likely with at least the tacit approval of Russian authorities". A [[Marriott International|Marriott]] executive told the committee that after Trump's 2013 stay at the hotel, he overheard two hotel employees discussing what to do with an elevator surveillance video they said showed Trump "with several women" whom one of the employees "implied to be 'hostesses'". Committee investigators interviewed the two employees, but they said they could not recall the video.<ref name="Schmidt_8/23/2020">{{cite news |last1=Schmidt |first1=Michael |title=Trump and Miss Moscow: Report Examines Possible Compromises in Russia Trips |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/08/18/us/politics/trump-russia-senate-intelligence.html |access-date=November 9, 2020 |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=August 23, 2020}}</ref>
[[Thomas Roberts (television journalist)|Thomas Roberts]], the host of the [[Miss Universe 2013|Miss Universe 2013 pageant]], confirmed that "Trump was in Moscow for one full night and at least part of another."<ref name="Kirell_4/24/2018">{{cite web |last=Kirell |first=Andrew |title=Miss Universe 2013 Host Thomas Roberts Confirms: Trump Stayed Overnight in Moscow | website=[[The Daily Beast]] |date=April 24, 2018 |url=https://www.thedailybeast.com/miss-universe-2013-host-thomas-roberts-confirms-trump-stayed-overnight-in-moscow |access-date=April 26, 2018}}</ref> According to flight records, [[Keith Schiller]]'s testimony, social media posts, and Trump's close friend, [[Aras Agalarov]], Trump arrived by private jet on Friday morning, November 8, going to the Ritz-Carlton hotel and booking in.<ref name="Silver_Pismennaya_7/13/2017">{{cite news |last1=Silver |first1=Vernon |last2=Pismennaya |first2=Evgenia |title=Trump's Two Nights of Parties in Moscow Echo Years Later |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |date=July 13, 2017 |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2017-07-13/trump-s-two-nights-of-parties-in-moscow-reverberate-years-later |access-date=March 3, 2018 |archive-date=July 14, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170714063746/https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2017-07-13/trump-s-two-nights-of-parties-in-moscow-reverberate-years-later |url-status=dead}}</ref>
The night of November 8–9, he attended Aras Agalarov's 58th birthday party at 10:00 p.m.<ref name="Silver_4/23/2018" /> He then left the birthday party early Saturday morning and, at about 1:30 a.m., Moscow time, returned to his hotel room with his bodyguard, Keith Schiller.<ref name="Corn_Isikoff_3/8/2018" /> While Trump and Schiller were walking back to Trump's hotel room, Schiller said he told Trump about an earlier offer to send five prostitutes "to Trump's hotel room that night",<ref name="Corn_Isikoff_3/8/2018" /> and Schiller said he told Trump he had rejected the offer. After Trump went to bed alone, Schiller said he stood outside Trump's door for a time and then left.<ref name="Dilanian_Allen_11/9/2017" />
During later testimony, Schiller was unable to provide an [[alibi]] for Trump's later activities during the early morning hours of November 9,<ref name="Dilanian_Allen_11/9/2017">{{cite news | last1=Dilanian | first1=Ken | author-link1=Ken Dilanian | last2=Allen | first2=Jonathan | author-link2=Jonathan Allen (journalist) | title=Bodyguard rejected Russian offer of 5 women for Trump | work=[[NBC News]] | date=November 9, 2017 | url=https://www.nbcnews.com/news/us-news/trump-bodyguard-testifies-russian-offered-trump-women-was-turned-down-n819386 | access-date=December 2, 2023}}</ref> hours described by Rob Goldstone as a "five-hour window" of time that Trump was afforded to sleep early Saturday morning".<ref name="Helderman_9/22/2018" /> This time is suspected by several writers of possibly being the time of the alleged salacious incident,{{efn|name="time_of_incident"| Possible time of incident.<ref name="Dilanian_Allen_11/9/2017" /><ref name="Helderman_9/22/2018" /><ref name="Longman_4/20/2018" /><ref name="Bergmann_et_al_11/27/2018">{{cite web | last1=Bergmann | first1=Max | last2=Venook | first2=Jeremy | last3=Moscow Project Team | title=Conspiracy Against the United States: The Story of Trump and Russia | website=[[Center for American Progress]] Action | date=November 27, 2018 | url=https://www.americanprogressaction.org/article/conspiracy-united-states-story-trump-russia/ | access-date=January 13, 2024 | quote=[Schiller] said he only stayed by the door to Trump's hotel room for part of the night, leaving open the possibility that the encounter may have occurred after Schiller left.}}</ref><ref name="Feinberg_9/25/2019" />}} and the [[United States Senate Select Committee on Intelligence|Senate Intelligence Committee]] found evidence of "additional social activity" in Trump's room at that time.<ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" />{{rp|292}}
Trump later repeatedly lied to Comey by claiming he was not in Moscow the night of November 8–9.<ref name="Longman_4/20/2018">{{cite magazine | last=Longman | first=Martin | title=Trump Lied to Comey About the Pee Tape | magazine=[[Washington Monthly]] | date=April 20, 2018 | url=https://washingtonmonthly.com/2018/04/20/trump-lied-to-comey-about-the-pee-tape/ | access-date=January 3, 2024}}</ref><ref name="Aggeler_4/13/2018_Possible" /> Comey later said: "It's always significant when someone lies to you, especially about something you're not asking about. It tends to reflect a consciousness of guilt."<ref name="Foran_4/26/2018">{{cite web | last=Foran | first=Clare | title=Comey on Trump's assertion that releasing memos was illegal: 'He's just making stuff up' | website=[[CNN]] | date=April 26, 2018 | url=https://www.cnn.com/2018/04/25/politics/james-comey-anderson-cooper-town-hall-cnn/index.html | access-date=July 29, 2024}}</ref> [[Jennifer Rubin (columnist)|Jennifer Rubin]] described Trump's fake alibi as "strong evidence of guilt".<ref name="Rubin_4/24/2018">{{cite news | last=Rubin | first=Jennifer | author-link=Jennifer Rubin (columnist) | title=A false alibi could be strong evidence of guilt | newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] | date=April 24, 2018 | url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/blogs/right-turn/wp/2018/04/24/trumps-false-alibi-could-be-strong-evidence-of-guilt/ | access-date=April 17, 2024}}</ref>
The morning of November 9, Facebook posts showed he was still at the Ritz-Carlton Hotel,<ref name="Smith_4/23/2018">{{cite web |last=Smith |first=Allan |title=Flight records obtained by Bloomberg shed new light on Trump's heavily scrutinized Moscow trip—and they seem to contradict what he told James Comey | website=[[Business Insider]] |date=April 23, 2018 |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/trump-moscow-flight-records-misled-james-comey-2018-4 |access-date=October 6, 2018}}</ref> and other sources document that he then had a busy day with various meetings and tours of Moscow.<ref name="Silver_Pismennaya_7/13/2017" /> That evening he attended the Miss Universe pageant, followed by an after-party that started at 1:00 a.m. Then, without returning to his hotel, he flew back to the U.S.<ref name="Silver_4/23/2018">{{cite news |last=Silver |first=Vernon |title=Flight Records Illuminate Mystery of Trump's Moscow Nights |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |date=April 23, 2018 |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2018-04-23/flight-records-illuminate-mystery-of-trump-s-moscow-nights |access-date=April 24, 2018}}</ref><ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" />{{rp|300}}<ref name="Corn_Isikoff_3/8/2018" /><ref name="Murray_4/20/2018">{{cite news | last=Murray | first=Mark | title=Did Trump stay overnight in Russia in 2013? Evidence points to yes | work=[[NBC News]] | date=April 20, 2018 | url=https://www.nbcnews.com/politics/donald-trump/did-trump-stay-overnight-russia-2013-evidence-points-yes-n867951 | access-date=March 22, 2024 | quote=Trump told Roberts and producer Kerrie Wudyka that he was leaving Moscow directly from the Miss Universe after party, and invited Roberts and her to fly back on his plane. Roberts and Wudyka declined the offer.}}</ref>
==== Legacy of "pee tape" rumor ====
James Comey wrote in his book ''[[A Higher Loyalty: Truth, Lies, and Leadership]]'' that Trump asked him to have the FBI investigate the "pee tape" allegation "because he wanted to convince his wife that it wasn't true".<ref name="Levitz_4/12/2018">{{cite magazine |last=Levitz |first=Eric |title=Comey: Trump Asked Me to Investigate 'Pee Tape' to Prove It Didn't Exist |magazine=[[New York (magazine)|New York]] |date=April 12, 2018 |url=https://nymag.com/intelligencer/2018/04/comey-trump-asked-me-to-prove-the-pee-tape-wasnt-real.html |access-date=September 28, 2019}}</ref> The sincerity of Trump's denials was doubted by Comey who, after Trump repeatedly lied to him about the alleged incident, came to believe the incident may have happened.<ref name="Aggeler_4/13/2018_Possible">{{cite web | last=Aggeler | first=Madeleine | title=James Comey Says It's 'Possible' Trump's Alleged Pee Tape Is Real | website=[[New York (magazine)#The Cut|The Cut]] | date=April 13, 2018 | url=https://www.thecut.com/2018/04/james-comey-pee-tape-donald-trump-real.html | access-date=September 28, 2019}}</ref>
Regarding the "golden showers" allegation, [[Michael Isikoff]] and [[David Corn]] have stated that Steele's "faith in the sensational sex claim would fade over time. ... As for the likelihood of the claim that prostitutes had urinated in Trump's presence, Steele would say to colleagues, 'It's 50–50'."<ref name="Isikoff_Corn_3/17/2018">{{cite news |last1=Isikoff |first1=Michael |author-link1=Michael Isikoff |last2=Corn |first2=David |author-link2=David Corn |date=March 17, 2018 |title=Russian Roulette: the real story behind the Steele dossier on Donald Trump |newspaper=[[Australian Financial Review]] |url=https://www.afr.com/lifestyle/arts-and-entertainment/books/russian-roulette-the-real-story-behind-the-steele-dossier-20180315-h0xj4g |access-date=May 10, 2018}}</ref> The book ''[[Russian Roulette (Isikoff and Corn book)|Russian Roulette]]'' says that Steele's confidence in the truth of "the Ritz-Carlton story was 'fifty-fifty'. He treated everything in the dossier as raw intelligence material—not proven fact."<ref name="Mayer_3/13/2018">{{cite magazine |last=Mayer |first=Jane |author-link=Jane Mayer |title=A Trump Trip to Las Vegas Adds Intrigue to the Steele Dossier |date=March 13, 2018 |magazine=[[The New Yorker]] |url=https://www.newyorker.com/news/news-desk/a-trump-trip-to-las-vegas-adds-intrigue-to-the-steele-dossier |access-date=June 1, 2020}}</ref><ref name="Blake_8/21/2020" /> In their 2019 book, the founders of Fusion GPS report that Steele received the "hotel anecdote" from seven Russian sources.<ref name="Ewing_11/22/2019">{{cite news |last=Ewing |first=Philip |title=In 'Crime In Progress,' Fusion GPS Chiefs Tell The Inside Story Of The Steele Dossier |work=[[NPR]] |date=November 22, 2019 |url=https://www.npr.org/2019/11/22/781589327/bosses-of-fusion-gps-tell-the-inside-story-of-the-steele-dossier |access-date=November 22, 2019}}</ref>
''[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]]'' journalist [[Ashley Feinberg]] investigated the pee tape rumor and linked to a 25-second video of the purported occurrence. She concluded that the tape was "fake", but "very far from being an obvious fake". A key "discrepancy", according to Feinberg, was that the video apparently showed the presidential suite as it appeared after a 2015 renovation, despite the purported occurrence being in November 2013, before the renovation occurred. The video had been in circulation since at least January 26, 2019.<ref name="Feinberg_9/25/2019">{{cite magazine |last=Feinberg |first=Ashley |title=The Pee Tape Is Real, but It's Fake |magazine=[[Slate (magazine)|Slate]] |url=https://slate.com/news-and-politics/2019/09/inside-the-convincing-fake-trump-pee-tape.html |date=September 25, 2019 |access-date=September 27, 2019}}</ref>
Describing the leaked [[Kremlin papers]] mention of ''kompromat'' on Trump, John Dobson, former British diplomat, wrote: "[T]he report confirms that the Kremlin possessed 'kompromat' on the future president, which the document claims was collected during 'certain events' that happened during [[Miss Universe 2013|Trump's trip to Moscow in November 2013]]."<ref name="Dobson_9/21/2024">{{cite web | last=Dobson | first=John | title=How Vladimir Putin uses Donald Trump to destabilise America | website=The Sunday Guardian Live | date=September 21, 2024 | url=https://sundayguardianlive.com/investigation/123686 | access-date=September 23, 2024}}</ref>
Marc Bennetts, correspondent in Russia for ''[[The Times]]'', also wrote about that mention:<ref name="Bennetts_7/16/2021">{{cite web | last=Bennetts | first=Marc | title=Vladimir Putin 'ordered spies to help mentally unstable Donald Trump win 2016 election' | website=[[The Times]] | date=July 16, 2021 | url=https://www.thetimes.com/article/vladimir-putin-ordered-spies-to-help-mentally-unstable-donald-trump-win-2016-election-fq9bwwxkm | access-date=September 23, 2024}}</ref> : {{blockquote| There was also apparent confirmation that the Kremlin possesses compromising information about Trump, 75, relating to 'certain events' during a visit to Moscow. A dossier compiled on Trump by Christopher Steele, a former MI6 officer, alleged in 2016 that Russian spies possessed video of him with prostitutes in a Moscow hotel. Trump has denied the allegation.}}
On August 25, 2024, ''Rolling Stone'' revealed that Danchenko "still believes – despite never having viewed it, despite his evidence really only being rumors and innuendo that he gathered but that many discredit – that there's a pee tape":<ref name="Forgrave_8/26/2024" /> {{blockquote| The rumor Danchenko says he heard during his half-dozen trips to Russia in 2016 goes like this: ... Trump, in Russia for the Miss Universe contest, was staying in the presidential suite at the Ritz-Carlton. It was the same suite Barack and Michelle Obama once stayed in. Trump was with some powerful Russian oligarchs, who brought the sex workers. According to Danchenko, several sources in Russia – sources he still won't reveal – told him rumors about what Trump did next: He instructed the sex workers to pee on the bed. ... 'They kind of had a laugh about it,' Danchenko continues. 'To me it sounds like this stupid college prank.' ... [He believes the] pee tape hijacked the dialogue of serious allegations about Russian influence with huge geopolitical importance. ... 'I still think there's some leverage they hold against Trump. You want to call it pee tape? Call it pee tape.'}}
==== Trump's previous knowledge of compromising tapes ====
[[File:Exhibit 38, HPSCI.jpg|thumb|upright=0.6|right|Exhibit 38. Texts between Rtskhiladze and Cohen starting on October 30, 2016.<ref name="HPSCI_3/6/2019">{{cite web | date=March 6, 2019 | title=Part 2, Deposition of Michael Cohen, March 6, 2019 | publisher=[[United States House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence]] | url=https://docs.house.gov/meetings/IG/IG00/20190520/109549/HMTG-116-IG00-20190520-SD001.pdf | pages=225–229, 235–236, 254 | access-date=October 31, 2023}}</ref>]] The [[Senate Intelligence Committee report on Russian interference in the 2016 United States presidential election|Senate Intelligence Committee report]]<ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" /> indicates that in late 2013 Trump learned of rumors about tapes of himself with prostitutes in Moscow, and "Footnote 112" in the Mueller Report suggests that Trump may have already heard that Russia had incriminating tapes of his behavior.<ref name="Mueller_report" /> "Footnote 112" describes how, on October 30, 2016, Michael Cohen exchanged a series of text messages ("Exhibit 38") with Giorgi Rtskhiladze, a friend and businessman who had worked with Cohen on Trump's real estate projects. Rtskhiladze reported that he had successfully "Stopped flow of some tapes from Russia but not sure if there's anything else. Just so u know. ..."<ref name="HIC_3/6/2019" /> Rtskhiladze told Mueller's investigators that these were "compromising tapes of Trump rumored to be held by persons associated with the Russian real estate conglomerate Crocus Group, which had helped host the 2013 Miss Universe Pageant in Russia." Cohen told investigators "he spoke to Trump about the issue after receiving the texts from Rtskhiladze".<ref name="Mueller_report" /><ref name="Kessler_4/24/2019" /> ''[[Rolling Stone]]'' reported that "Rtskhiladze's description of the tapes' content tracks with the unverified information included in the Steele dossier".<ref name="Stuart_4/18/2019">{{cite magazine |last=Stuart |first=Tessa |title=Here's What the Mueller Report Says About the Pee Tape |magazine=[[Rolling Stone]] |date=April 18, 2019 |url=https://www.rollingstone.com/politics/politics-news/pee-tape-trump-mueller-report-823755/ |access-date=November 6, 2019}}</ref>
Mueller's office "twice interviewed Rtskhiladze" (April 4, 2018, and May 10, 2018),<ref name="Mueller_report" /> and his story changed between interviews. "Footnote 112" covers both interviews. The footnote shows that Rtskhiladze later (May 10, 2018) changed his previous (April 4, 2018) testimony to Mueller<ref name="Robson_11/15/2019">{{cite web | last=Robson | first=Ruthann | author-link=Ruthann Robson | title=Sexing the Mueller Report | date=November 15, 2019 | url=https://www2.stetson.edu/law-review/wp-content/uploads/2020/12/Robson.Final_.pdf | publisher=[[Stetson Law Review]] | access-date=February 18, 2024 | quote="Footnote 112" "concluded with additional information from a May 2018 interview".}}</ref> by claiming that the "tapes were fake", but District Judge [[Christopher R. Cooper]] cast doubt on that claim. He said Rtskhiladze "undercut" his claim by speaking as if getting recorded was a real consequence of indiscretions committed around Agalarov/Crocus,<ref name="RvM_Memorandum_Opinion_9/1/2021">{{cite web | date=September 1, 2021 | title=Rtskhiladze v. Mueller, Memorandum Opinion, 20-cv-1591 (CRC) | website=[[United States District Court for the District of Columbia]] | url=https://ecf.dcd.uscourts.gov/cgi-bin/show_public_doc?2020cv1591-32}}</ref> and because Rtskhiladze's own words indicated "that the tapes may have been real":<ref name="RvM_Memorandum_Opinion_9/1/2021" /> {{blockquote| As for Rtskhiladze's professed belief that the tapes were fake, that suggestion is somewhat undercut by Rtskhiladze's statement, only present in the Senate Report, suggesting that the tapes may have been real, and that they were 'what happens when you visit crocus I guess.'}}
Both Judge Cooper<ref name="RvM_Memorandum_Opinion_9/1/2021" /> and the Senate Intelligence Committed seemed skeptical of the claim. The Senate report notes the lack of evidence for that claim in the partially blacked-out "Footnote 4281" that says "did not identify evidence of a later call from Khokhlov".<ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" />{{rp|660}}
Cohen was deposed by the [[United States House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence|House Intelligence Committee]], and he provided a history of the alleged tapes. He described the tapes stopped by Rtskhiladze as "the infamous pee tape when Mr. Trump was in Moscow for the Miss Universe Pageant". Cohen testified he became aware of the tape in "late 2013 or early 2014, shortly after the Miss Universe 2013 pageant and significantly prior to the 2016 U.S. election cycle" and told Trump about the rumor at that time. The Senate Report added that "Cohen ... would have been willing to pay [$20 million] ... to suppress the information if it could be verified, but Cohen was never shown any evidence."<ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" />{{rp|659}}<ref name="Friedman_5/23/2019">{{cite web | last=Friedman | first=Dan | date=May 23, 2019 | title=The Striking—and Overlooked—Highlights From Michael Cohen's Intelligence Committee Interviews | magazine=[[Mother Jones (magazine)|Mother Jones]] | url=https://www.motherjones.com/politics/2019/05/the-striking-and-overlooked-highlights-from-michael-cohens-intelligence-committee-interviews/ | access-date=October 31, 2023}}</ref>
In "2014 or 2015", after learning of the rumor, Cohen asked Rtskhiladze for help "to find out if the tape was real",<ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" />{{rp|658}} and on October 30, 2016, Rtskhiladze contacted Cohen with news that he had succeeded in stopping the tapes.<ref name="Kessler_4/24/2019" />
When Representative [[Jackie Speier]] asked Cohen about the tape: "It wasn't infamous then, was it?" he replied: "Yes, yes. That the tape – the conversation about the tape has gone back almost a couple months past when they were there for the Miss Universe Pageant that that tape existed." When Speier asked "So you're suggesting you've known about the rumors about this tape for many years before October 30th?" he replied that he had talked to "many people" about the pee tape over the years.<ref name="HIC_3/6/2019">{{cite web | date=March 6, 2019 | title=Deposition of Michael Cohen, Part 2 | website=[[United States House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence|House Intelligence Committee]] | url=https://docs.house.gov/meetings/IG/IG00/20190520/109549/HMTG-116-IG00-20190520-SD001.pdf}}</ref>{{rp|225–229, 235–236, 254}}<ref name="Wittes_et_al_8/21/2020">{{cite web | last=Wittes | first=Benjamin | author-link=Benjamin Wittes | title=A Collusion Reading Diary: What Did the Senate Intelligence Committee Find? | website=[[Lawfare (website)|Lawfare]] | date=August 21, 2020 | url=https://www.lawfaremedia.org/article/collusion-reading-diary-what-did-senate-intelligence-committee-find | access-date=October 17, 2023}}</ref>
It was only after the Steele dossier's publication in 2017 that Trump publicly mentioned the rumor and focused his ire on the dossier as if it were the creator of a new rumor. Some other sources did the same.<ref name="Santos_3/16/2018" /><ref name="Siddique_10/16/2023">{{cite news | last=Siddique | first=Haroon | title=Trump aims to sue London firm over dossier alleging sexual perversions | newspaper=[[The Guardian]] | date=October 16, 2023 | url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2023/oct/16/donald-trump-damages-steele-dossier-high-court-london | access-date=December 28, 2023}}</ref> Trump and some sources falsely claim Steele "made-up"<ref name="Siddique_10/16/2023" /> the rumor or that it was "born" because of the dossier.<ref name="Santos_3/16/2018">{{cite web | last=Santos | first=Brian De Los | title=Donald Trump's 'pee tape' controversy, explained | website=[[Mashable]] | date=March 16, 2018 | url=https://mashable.com/article/donald-trump-pee-tape-golden-showers-explainer | access-date=December 29, 2023 | quote=And thus, the pee tape was born.}}</ref> In fact, Trump already knew about reports, "separate from" the much later Steele dossier, of "alleged compromising tapes of him in Moscow":<ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" />{{rp|945}} {{blockquote| The Committee's Report also shows that prior to and during the campaign, Trump was informed of alleged compromising tapes of him in Moscow. These allegations are separate from Christopher Steele's reports, which were not used to support the Committee's work. The Committee found that the Russian intelligence services clearly engage in the collection of compromising information for leverage, and that there may be substance to some of the allegations regarding Trump, which leaves open an ongoing concern about Russian influence operations.}}
==== Role of Agalarovs ====
The [[Senate Intelligence Committee report on Russian interference in the 2016 United States presidential election|Senate Intelligence Committee report]] implied that [[Aras Agalarov]] and his Crocus Group were part of a Russian intelligence effort to compromise and gain leverage over Trump.<ref name="Wittes_et_al_8/21/2020" />
On June 15, 2013, five months before the 2013 Miss Universe contest in Moscow, Trump was accompanied on a visit to the Las Vegas nightclub "The Act"<ref name="Mayer_3/13/2018" /> by Crocus Group owner Aras Agalarov, his son [[Emin Agalarov|Emin]], [[Ike Kaveladze]], [[Rob Goldstone]], Michael Cohen, [[Keith Schiller]], and others, where Trump was photographed<ref name="Corn_Isikoff_3/8/2018" /> and the group stayed "for several hours". The club featured "risque performances"<ref name="Blake_8/21/2020" /> and, according to Cohen, Trump watched a golden showers performance "with delight".<ref name="Zoellner_9/6/2020">{{cite news |last=Zoellner |first=Danielle |title=Michael Cohen claims Trump watched 'with delight' during sex act performance in Vegas |newspaper=[[The Independent]] |date=September 6, 2020 |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-politics/trump-golden-showers-las-vegas-russia-michael-cohen-book-b404574.html |access-date=October 15, 2021}}</ref>
The Agalarovs were also linked to several other events involving Trump, including the invitation to share "dirt" on Clinton at the [[Trump Tower meeting]]<ref name="Sheth_3/14/2018">{{cite web |last=Sheth |first=Sonam |title=House Intelligence Democrats want to question the Ritz-Carlton in Moscow about the most salacious allegation in the Trump-Russia dossier | website=[[Business Insider]] |date=March 14, 2018 |url=http://www.businessinsider.com/house-democrats-want-to-question-ritz-carlton-moscow-trump-steele-dossier-2018-3 |access-date=April 11, 2020}}</ref> and knowledge of Trump's alleged sexual activities in Russia, both in St. Petersburg and the Moscow Ritz-Carlton. The dossier's sources reported that Aras Agalarov "would know most of the details of what the Republican presidential candidate had got up to" in St. Petersburg.<ref name="Bertrand_11/10/2017" /> In 2013, when Trump stayed at the Ritz-Carlton hotel, "multiple sources" reported that the offer to "send five women to Trump's hotel room that night"<ref name="Corn_Isikoff_3/8/2018">{{cite magazine |last1=Corn |first1=David |author-link1=David Corn |last2=Isikoff |first2=Michael |author-link2=Michael Isikoff |date=March 8, 2018 |title=What Happened in Moscow: The Inside Story of How Trump's Obsession With Putin Began |magazine=[[Mother Jones (magazine)|Mother Jones]] |url=https://www.motherjones.com/politics/2018/03/russian-connection-what-happened-moscow-inside-story-trump-obsession-putin-david-corn-michael-isikoff/ |access-date=April 15, 2018}}</ref> came from a Russian who was accompanying Emin Agalarov".<ref name="Raju_Herb_11/10/2017">{{cite news |last1=Raju |first1=Manu |last2=Herb |first2=Jeremy |url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/11/09/politics/keith-schiller-russian-offer-women-2013-moscow/index.html |title=Ex-Trump security chief testifies he rejected 2013 Russian offer of women for Trump in Moscow |work=[[CNN]] |date=November 10, 2017 |access-date=January 26, 2018}}</ref> A footnote in the Mueller Report describes how Giorgi Rtskhiladze reported that he had successfully stopped the "flow of ... compromising tapes of Trump rumored to be held by persons associated with the Russian real estate conglomerate Crocus Group" [owned by Agalarov].<ref name="Kessler_4/24/2019" />
==== Trump viewed as under Putin's influence ====
[[File:President Trump Holds a Joint Press Conference with the President of the Russian Federation.webm|thumb|right|200px|thumbtime=0:10|The [[2018 Russia–United States Summit#Press conference|press conference]] at the [[2018 Russia–United States Summit|2018 summit]] in [[Helsinki]], [[Finland]], on July 16, 2018 (English version) 46 minutes]] Dossier source(s) allege that the Russians possess kompromat on Trump that can be used to blackmail him, and that the Kremlin promised him the ''kompromat'' will not be used as long as he continues his cooperation with them.<ref name="Withnall_Sengupta_1/12/2017" /><ref name="Bertrand_1/10/2017" /> Trump's actions at the [[2018 Russia–United States Summit|Helsinki summit in 2018]] "led many to conclude that Steele's report was more accurate than not. ... Trump sided with the Russians over the U.S. intelligence community's assessment that Moscow had waged an all-out attack on the 2016 election. ... The joint news conference ... cemented fears among some that Trump was in Putin's pocket and prompted bipartisan backlash."<ref name="Bertrand_9/30/2019">{{cite web|last=Bertrand|first=Natasha|author-link=Natasha Bertrand|date=September 30, 2019|title=The Russia Hawk in the White House|url=https://www.politico.com/magazine/story/2019/09/30/fiona-hill-russia-trump-adviser-228758/|access-date=November 11, 2019|magazine=[[Politico]]}}</ref>
At the [[2018 Russia–United States Summit#Press conference|joint press conference]], when asked directly about the subject, Putin denied having any ''kompromat'' on Trump. Even though Trump was reportedly given a "gift from Putin" the weekend of the pageant, Putin argued "that he did not even know Trump was in Russia for the Miss Universe pageant in 2013 when, according to the Steele dossier, video of Trump was secretly recorded to blackmail him."<ref name="Mackey_7/16/2018">{{cite web |last=Mackey |first=Robert |title=Trump and Putin Met in Helsinki's Hall of Mirrors. Here Are the Highlights. | website=[[The Intercept]] |date=July 16, 2018 |url=https://theintercept.com/2018/07/16/live-trump-and-putin-meet-in-helsinkis-hall-of-mirrors/ |access-date=November 11, 2019}}</ref>
In reaction to Trump's actions at the summit, Senator [[Charles E. Schumer|Chuck Schumer]] (D-N.Y.) spoke in the Senate: "Millions of Americans will continue to wonder if the only possible explanation for this dangerous and inexplicable behavior is the possibility—the very real possibility—that President Putin holds damaging information over President Trump."<ref name="Blake_7/17/2018">{{cite news |last=Blake |first=Aaron |title=The growing Trump-Putin kompromat question |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=July 17, 2018 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/the-fix/wp/2018/07/17/does-putin-have-kompromat-on-trump-suddenly-its-no-longer-such-a-taboo-question-thanks-to-trump/ |access-date=November 11, 2019}}</ref>
Several operatives and lawyers in the U.S. intelligence community reacted strongly to Trump's performance at the summit. They described it as "subservien[ce] to Putin" and a "fervent defense of Russia's military and cyber aggression around the world, and its violation of international law in Ukraine" which they saw as "harmful to US interests". They also suggested he was either a "Russian asset" or a "useful idiot" for Putin,<ref name="Sheth_8/30/2019">{{cite web |last=Sheth |first=Sonam |title=US spies say Trump's G7 performance suggests he's either a 'Russian asset' or a 'useful idiot' for Putin | website=[[Business Insider]] |date=August 30, 2019 |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/spies-react-trump-g7-summit-russian-asset-2019-8 |access-date=November 12, 2019}}</ref> and that he looked like "Putin's puppet".<ref name="Sheth_8/27/2019">{{cite web |last=Sheth |first=Sonam |title=Russia came out the winner of this year's G7 summit, and Trump looked like 'Putin's puppet' | website=[[Business Insider]] |date=August 27, 2019 |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/russia-was-the-winner-g7-trump-crimea-2019-8 |access-date=November 12, 2019}}</ref> Former [[Director of National Intelligence]] James Clapper wondered "if Russians have something on Trump",<ref name="CNN_7/19/2018">{{cite news |title=Clapper: I wonder if Russians have something on Trump |work=[[CNN]] |date=July 19, 2018 |url=https://www.cnn.com/videos/politics/2018/07/19/james-clapper-trump-helsinki-behavior-putin-bpr-vpx.cnn |access-date=November 12, 2019}}</ref> and former CIA director John Brennan, who has accused Trump of "treason", tweeted: "He is wholly in the pocket of Putin."<ref name="Sevastopulo_Hille_7/20/2018">{{cite news |last1=Sevastopulo |first1=Demetri |last2=Hille |first2=Kathrin |title=Trump-Putin: Will Helsinki prove a turning point for the Republicans? |newspaper=[[Financial Times]] |date=July 20, 2018 |url=https://www.ft.com/content/19415d54-8c05-11e8-b18d-0181731a0340 |access-date=November 12, 2019}}</ref>
Former acting CIA director [[Michael Morell]] has called Trump "an unwitting agent of the Russian federation", and former CIA director [[Michael Hayden (general)|Michael V. Hayden]] said Trump was a "useful fool" who is "manipulated by Moscow".<ref name="Boot_1/13/2019">{{cite news |last=Boot |first=Max |title=Here are 18 reasons Trump could be a Russian asset |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=January 13, 2019 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/here-are-18-reasons-why-trump-could-be-a-russian-asset/2019/01/13/45b1b250-174f-11e9-88fe-f9f77a3bcb6c_story.html |access-date=November 12, 2019}}</ref> House Speaker [[Nancy Pelosi]] questioned Trump's loyalty when she asked him: "[Why do] all roads lead to Putin?"<ref name="DeBonis_Kim_10/17/2019">{{cite news |last1=DeBonis |first1=Mike |last2=Kim |first2=Seung Min |title='All roads lead to Putin': Pelosi questions Trump's loyalty in White House clash |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=October 17, 2019 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/powerpost/pelosi-recalls-clash-with-trump-says-she-was-probably-telling-him-that-all-roads-lead-to-putin/2019/10/17/fdbde8d2-f0f2-11e9-8693-f487e46784aa_story.html |access-date=November 12, 2019}}</ref>
Former [[KGB]] major [[Yuri Shvets]] asserts that Trump has been cultivated as an "asset" by Russian intelligence since 1977: "Russian intelligence gained an interest in Trump as far back as 1977, viewing Trump as an exploitable target."<ref name="Palma_2/2/2021" /><ref name="Smith_1/29/2021" />
Trump was not viewed as an actual [[intelligence agent|agent (spy)]] but as an [[Asset (intelligence)|asset]]: "We're talking about Trump being a self-interested businessman who's happy to do a favour if it works to his own best interests."<ref name="Conversation_2/5/2021">{{cite web | department=The Conversation | title=Donald Trump: More Likely Useful Idiot Than Putin's Agent | website=[[Snopes]] | date=February 5, 2021 | url=https://www.snopes.com/news/2021/02/05/donald-trump-more-likely-useful-idiot-than-putins-agent/ | access-date=December 21, 2021}}</ref>
[[Ynet]], an Israeli online news site, reported on January 12, 2017, that U.S. intelligence advised Israeli intelligence officers to be cautious about sharing information with the incoming Trump administration, until the possibility of Russian influence over Trump, suggested by Steele's report, has been fully investigated.<ref name="Bergman_1/12/2017" />
[[Max Boot]]<ref name="Boot_8/1/2020">{{cite news |last=Boot |first=Max |title=More evidence of Trump's subservience to Putin{{snd}}and we still don't know why |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=August 1, 2020 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/2020/09/01/more-evidence-trumps-subservience-putin-we-still-dont-know-why/ |access-date=September 3, 2020}}</ref> described what he sees as more "evidence of Trump's subservience to Putin", and he tied it to new government confirmations of rumors about Trump's alleged "dalliances with Russian women during visits to Moscow" that leave "him open to blackmail", rumors mentioned in the 2020 [[United States Senate Select Committee on Intelligence|Senate Intelligence Committee]] report: While the Senate Intelligence Committee report extensively explored the possibility of Russian ''kompromat'', much of the discussion was redacted in the public version of the report. Ultimately, the Senate Intelligence Committee "did not establish" that Russia had ''kompromat'' on Trump.<ref name="Blake_8/21/2020">{{cite news |last1=Blake |first1=Aaron |title=Five provocative nuggets from the Senate intel report on Trump and Russia |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2020/08/21/provocative-details-senate-intel/ |access-date=November 9, 2020 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=August 21, 2020}}</ref>
On the subject of ''kompromat'', Bruce Ohr testified to the House Judiciary and Oversight committees that on July 30, 2016, Steele told him that "Russian intelligence believed 'they had Trump over a barrel' ... [a] broader sentiment [that] is echoed in Steele's dossier".<ref name="Tucker_Day_9/1/2018">{{cite news | last1=Tucker | first1=Eric | last2=Day | first2=Chad | title=AP sources: Former spy said Russia had 'Trump over a barrel' | agency=[[Associated Press]] | date=September 1, 2018 | url=https://apnews.com/article/north-america-donald-trump-financial-markets-ap-top-news-politics-970eefea2c154b3488ffde03a8a59d22 | access-date=May 4, 2023}}</ref><ref name="Herb_8/31/2018">{{cite web | last=Herb | first=Jeremy | title=Ohr says Steele told him Russian intel believed they had Trump 'over a barrel' | website=[[CNN]] | date=August 31, 2018 | url=https://www.cnn.com/2018/08/31/politics/bruce-ohr-christopher-steele-donald-trump/index.html | access-date=May 4, 2023}}</ref> Paul Wood described the source as "another Danchenko contact, a 'former senior intelligence officer now a Kremlin official'. This was later said to be no less than a former head of Russia's foreign intelligence services. This source did not talk specifically about the "pee tape" but, Danchenko told Steele, he said they had sexual ''kompromat'' on Trump going back years. 'We've got him [[wikt:over a barrel|over a barrel]].'"<ref name="Wood_8/12/2020" />
=== Kremlin's "Romanian" hackers and use of WikiLeaks, and Trump campaign reaction ===
Dossier source(s) allege that "Romanian hackers" controlled by Putin hacked the DNC servers and that the Trump campaign cooperated with Russia.<ref name="Harding_5/10/2017" /><ref name="Borger_4/28/2017" />
Russian hackers used the [[Guccifer 2.0]] persona and claimed to be [[Romania]]n, like [[Guccifer|Marcel Lazar Lehel]], the Romanian hacker who originally used the "Guccifer" [[pseudonym]].<ref name="Franceschi-Bicchierai_6/21/2016">{{cite magazine |last=Franceschi-Bicchierai |first=Lorenzo |title=We Spoke to DNC Hacker 'Guccifer 2.0' |magazine=[[Vice (magazine)|Vice]] |date=June 21, 2016 |url=https://www.vice.com/en/article/dnc-hacker-guccifer-20-interview/ |access-date=January 22, 2020}}</ref><ref name="Sanger_Rutenberg_Lipton_7/15/2018" /><ref name="Schwartz_5/24/2013">{{cite web |last=Schwartz |first=Mathew J. |title=Report: Guccifer 2.0 Unmasked at Last | website=BankInfoSecurity |date=May 24, 2013 |url=https://www.bankinfosecurity.com/report-guccifer-20-unmasked-at-last-a-10737 |access-date=January 25, 2020}}</ref>
The [[Mueller Report]] confirmed that the dossier was correct that the Kremlin was behind the appearance of the DNC emails on WikiLeaks, noting that the Trump campaign "showed interest in WikiLeaks's releases of documents and welcomed their potential to damage candidate Clinton".<ref name="Kessler_4/24/2019">{{cite news |last=Kessler |first=Glenn |author-link=Glenn Kessler (journalist) |title=What the Steele dossier said vs. what the Mueller report said |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=April 24, 2019 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2019/04/24/what-steele-dossier-said-vs-what-mueller-report-said/ |access-date=April 25, 2019}}</ref> It was later confirmed that [[Roger Stone]] was in contact with WikiLeaks.<ref name="Tucker_Long_Balsamo_4/29/2020">{{cite web | last1=Tucker | first1=Eric | last2=Long | first2=Colleen | last3=Balsamo | first3=Michael | title=FBI documents reveal that Roger Stone was in direct communication with Wikileaks founder Julian Assange | website=[[Business Insider]] | date=April 29, 2020 | url=https://www.businessinsider.com/fbi-reveals-roger-stone-was-directly-communicating-with-julian-assange-2020-4 | access-date=August 22, 2022}}</ref><ref name="Samuelsohn_Gerstein_11/12/2019">{{cite web | last1=Samuelsohn | first1=Darren | last2=Gerstein | first2=Josh | title=What Roger Stone's trial revealed about Donald Trump and WikiLeaks | newspaper=[[Politico]] | date=November 12, 2019 | url=https://www.politico.com/news/2019/11/12/roger-stone-trial-donald-trump-wikileaks-070368 | access-date=August 22, 2022}}</ref>
==== Timing of release of hacked emails ====
Dossier source(s) allege that Carter Page "conceived and promoted" the idea of [the Russians] leaking the stolen DNC emails to WikiLeaks during the 2016 Democratic National Convention<ref name="Yglesias_Prokop_1/5/2018" /><ref name="LeTourneau_1/13/2017" /> for the purpose of swinging supporters of Bernie Sanders "away from Hillary CLINTON and across to TRUMP".<ref name="Yglesias_Prokop_1/5/2018" /><ref name="Garossino_1/14/2017" /> (Reports 95, 102)
In July 2016, in an "error-ridden message", WikiLeaks urged Russian intelligence to act swiftly to reach this timeline goal: {{As written|"If you have anything hillary related we want it in the next tweo days prefable because the DNC is approaching, and she will solidify bernie supporters behind her after."}}<ref name="Sanger_Rutenberg_Lipton_7/15/2018">{{cite news |last1=Sanger |first1=David E. |last2=Rutenberg |first2=Jim |last3=Lipton |first3=Eric |title=Tracing Guccifer 2.0's Many Tentacles in the 2016 Election |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=July 15, 2018 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/07/15/us/politics/guccifer-russia-mueller.html |access-date=December 2, 2019}}</ref> ''The New York Times'' reported that Assange told ''[[Democracy Now!]]'' "he had timed their release to coincide with the Democratic convention".<ref name="Savage_7/26/2016">{{cite news |last=Savage |first=Charlie |author-link=Charlie Savage (author) |title=Assange, Avowed Foe of Clinton, Timed Email Release for Democratic Convention |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=July 26, 2016 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2016/07/27/us/politics/assange-timed-wikileaks-release-of-democratic-emails-to-harm-hillary-clinton.html |access-date=March 21, 2020}}</ref>
The leaks started the day before the DNC national convention, a timing that was seen as suspicious by [[David Shedd]], a former [[Director of the Defense Intelligence Agency]], who said: "The release of emails just as the Democratic National Convention is getting underway this week has the hallmarks of a Russian active measures campaign."<ref name="Engel_7/26/2016">{{cite web |last=Engel |first=Pamela |title=The worst might be yet to come with the DNC email hack | website=[[Business Insider]] |date=July 26, 2016 |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/more-dnc-leaked-emails-coming-2016-7 |access-date=December 2, 2019}}</ref>
=== Manafort and kickback payments from Yanukovych ===
Dossier source(s) allege that Russia-friendly president Yanukovych, whom Manafort advised for over a decade, had told Putin he had been making supposedly untraceable<ref name="Sumter_11/16/2017" /> kickback payments to Manafort.<ref name="Sumter_11/16/2017" /> After Yanukovych fled to Russia in 2014 under accusations of corruption, a secret "black ledger" was found in the former Party of Regions headquarters. It showed that Yanukovych and his ruling political party had set aside $12.7 million in illegal and undisclosed payments to Manafort for his work from 2007 to 2012.<ref name="Kramer_McIntire_Meier_8/14/2016">{{cite news |last1=Kramer |first1=Andrew E. |last2=McIntire |first2=Mike |last3=Meier |first3=Barry |title=Secret Ledger in Ukraine Lists Cash for Donald Trump's Campaign Chief |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=August 14, 2016 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2016/08/15/us/politics/paul-manafort-ukraine-donald-trump.html |access-date=October 1, 2019}}</ref> Manafort has denied receiving the payments.<ref name="Rhodan_8/15/2016">{{cite magazine |last=Rhodan |first=Maya |title=Donald Trump's Campaign Chief Slams Report on Ukraine Payments |magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]] |date=August 15, 2016 |url=https://time.com/4452164/donald-trump-paul-manafort-ukraine/ |access-date=October 1, 2019}}</ref> Manafort was accused of receiving $750,000 in "illegal, off-the-books payments from Ukraine's pro-Russian President Viktor Yanukovych before he was toppled".<ref name="Reevell_3/21/2017">{{cite news |last=Reevell |first=Patrick |title=Ex-Trump campaign manager faces new allegations from Ukraine over 'black ledger' payments |work=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] |date=March 21, 2017 |url=https://abcnews.go.com/International/trump-campaign-manager-paul-manafort-faces-allegations-ukraine/story?id=46272679 |access-date=December 2, 2019}}</ref>
From 2006 to at least 2009, Manafort had a $10 million annual contract with Putin ally and aluminum magnate, Oleg Deripaska, a contract under which Manafort had proposed he would "influence politics, business dealings and news coverage inside the United States, Europe and former Soviet republics to benefit President Vladimir Putin's government".<ref name="Horwitz_Day_3/22/2017">{{cite news |last1=Horwitz |first1=Jeff |last2=Day |first2=Chad |date=March 22, 2017 |title=Before Trump job, Manafort worked to aid Putin |agency=[[Associated Press]] |url=https://bigstory.ap.org/article/122ae0b5848345faa88108a03de40c5a/manaforts-plan-greatly-benefit-putin-government |access-date=December 2, 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170404014639/https://bigstory.ap.org/article/122ae0b5848345faa88108a03de40c5a/manaforts-plan-greatly-benefit-putin-government |archive-date=April 4, 2017 |url-status=dead}}</ref>
=== Page met with Rosneft officials ===
[[File:Carter Page MSNBC June 2017 YouTube.png|thumb|upright=0.6|right|[[Carter Page]] (2017)]] [[File:Sechin Headshot.jpg|thumb|upright=0.6|right|[[Igor Sechin]] (2016)]] On November 2, 2017, Carter Page testified, without a lawyer, for more than six hours before the [[House Intelligence Committee]] that was investigating Russian interference in the 2016 U.S. elections. He testified about his five-day trip to Moscow in July 2016.<ref name="Prokop_2/2/2018">{{cite news |last=Prokop |first=Andrew |title=Carter Page, the star of the Nunes memo, explained | website=[[Vox (website)|Vox]] |date=February 2, 2018 |url=https://www.vox.com/policy-and-politics/2018/2/2/16956014/nunes-memo-carter-page |access-date=April 13, 2018}}</ref> According to his testimony, before leaving he informed [[Jeff Sessions]], [[J. D. Gordon]], [[Hope Hicks]], and [[Corey Lewandowski]], Trump's campaign manager, of the planned trip to Russia, and Lewandowski approved the trip, responding: "If you'd like to go on your own, not affiliated with the campaign, you know, that's fine."<ref name="Tracy_11/7/2017" /><ref name="Raju_Herb_Polantz_11/8/2017">{{cite news |date=November 8, 2017 |first1=Manu |last1=Raju |first2=Jeremy |last2=Herb |first3=Katelyn |last3=Polantz |title=Carter Page reveals new contacts with Trump campaign, Russians |work=[[CNN]] |url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/11/06/politics/carter-page-testimony-released/index.html |access-date=April 1, 2018}}</ref>
Dossier source(s) allege that Page secretly met [[Rosneft]] chairman [[Igor Sechin]] on that July trip.<ref name="Harding_1/11/2017" /> Page denied meeting Sechin or any Russian officials during that trip,<ref name="Lanktree_11/7/2017" /><ref name="Kelly_11/6/2017" /> but he later admitted under oath that he met with Sechin's senior aide, Andrey Baranov, who was Rosneft's chief of investor relations.<ref name="Afkhami_2/5/2018">{{cite web |last=Afkhami |first=Artin |title=Timeline of Carter Page's Contacts with Russia |publisher=[[New York University School of Law|Just Security]] |date=February 5, 2018 |url=https://www.justsecurity.org/46786/timeline-carter-pages-contacts-russia/ |access-date=August 12, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Robertson_2/7/2018" /> According to Harding, Baranov was "almost certainly" "relaying Sechin's wishes".<ref name="Harding_2/3/2018" /> David Corn and Michael Isikoff wrote that the FBI was not able to find evidence that Page met with Sechin or was offered a 19 percent stake in the giant energy conglomerate in exchange for the lifting of U.S. sanctions and that "Mueller's report noted that his 'activities in Russia ... were not fully explained'".<ref name="Corn_11/19/2021">{{cite magazine |last=Corn |first=David |author-link=David Corn |url=https://www.motherjones.com/politics/2021/11/the-steele-dossier-and-donald-trumps-betrayal-of-america/ |title=The Steele Dossier and Donald Trump's Betrayal of America |magazine=[[Mother Jones (magazine)|Mother Jones]] |date=November 19, 2021 |access-date=November 20, 2021}}</ref> ''[[Newsweek]]'' has listed the claim about Page meeting with Rosneft officials as "verified".<ref name="Maza_1/10/2018">{{cite news |url=https://www.newsweek.com/trump-russia-dossier-one-year-later-what-we-know-777116 |title=How true is the Trump–Russia dossier? One year later, what we know about its claims. |last=Maza |first=Cristina |date=January 10, 2018 |work=[[Newsweek]] |access-date=April 9, 2018}}</ref>
