Jane Mayer said this part of the dossier seems true, even if the name of an official may have been wrong.<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" /> Page's congressional testimony confirmed he met with Andrey Baranov, who was Rosneft's chief of investor relations,<ref name="Afkhami_2/5/2018" /> and Page conceded under questioning by [[Adam Schiff]] that the "potential sale of a significant percentage of Rosneft" might have been "briefly mentioned".<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" /><ref>{{cite web |url=https://intelligence.house.gov/uploadedfiles/cp36.pdf |title=Testimony of Carter Page |publisher=[[United States House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence]] |date=November 2, 2017 |access-date=November 24, 2021 |page=139 |archive-date=September 30, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210930121311/https://intelligence.house.gov/uploadedfiles/cp36.pdf |url-status=dead}}<br> '''MR. SCHIFF''': Dr. Page, this is my specific question: Did you or did you not discuss with Mr. Baranov in July a potential sale of a significant percentage of Rosneft?<br> '''MR. PAGE''': I can't recall any discussion.<br> '''MR. SCHIFF''': So you may have, but you don't recall.<br> '''MR. PAGE''': He may have briefly mentioned it when we were looking up from this Portugal—Ronaldo, whoever the—you know, the goals that are being scored. That may have come up. But I have no definitive recollection of that. And, certainly, what never came up, certainly, was my involvement in any—that type of a transaction.</ref> However, Page insisted that "there was never any negotiations, or any quid pro quo, or any offer, or any request even, in any way related to sanctions".<ref name="Raju_Herb_Polantz_11/7/2017">{{cite news |last1=Raju |first1=Manu |last2=Herb |first2=Jeremy |last3=Polantz |first3=Katelyn |title=Carter Page reveals new contacts with Trump campaign, Russians |work=[[CNN]] |date=November 7, 2017 |url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/11/06/politics/carter-page-testimony-released/index.html |access-date=November 24, 2021}}</ref>
CNN noted that his admissions to the House Intelligence Committee did confirm the Steele dossier was right about Page attending high-level meetings with Russians and possibly discussing "a sale of a stake in Rosneft", even though he denied doing so at the time.<ref name="Samuelsohn_Cheney_Bertrand_4/23/2019">{{cite web |last1=Samuelsohn|first1=Darren |last2=Cheney |first2=Kyle |last3=Bertrand |first3=Natasha |author-link3=Natasha Bertrand |date=April 23, 2019 |title=What you missed in the Mueller report |newspaper=[[Politico]] |url=https://www.politico.com/story/2019/04/23/mueller-report-findings-1287072 |access-date=September 10, 2022}}</ref><ref name="Cohen_6/14/2019">{{cite news |last=Cohen |first=Marshall |date=June 14, 2019 |title=Explaining Republicans' claims about 'false information' in the Trump-Russia dossier |url=https://www.cnn.com/2019/06/14/politics/trump-russia-dossier-carter-page-fisa/index.html |work=[[CNN]] |access-date=September 10, 2022 |quote=But Steele was right that Page attended high-level meetings with Russians during his trip, even though Page was denying it at the time.}}</ref> In April 2019, the Mueller Report concluded that their investigation did not establish that Page coordinated with Russia's interference efforts.<ref name="Cohen_6/14/2019" />
On February 11, 2021, Page lost a defamation suit he had filed against [[Yahoo! News]] and ''[[HuffPost]]'' for their articles that described his activities mentioned in the Steele dossier.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Page v. Oath Inc., C.A. No. S20C-07-030 CAK {{!}} Casetext Search + Citator |url=https://casetext.com/case/page-v-oath-inc-5 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220715140659/https://casetext.com/case/page-v-oath-inc-5 |url-status=dead |archive-date=July 15, 2022 |access-date=August 16, 2024 |website=casetext.com}}</ref> According to Jeff Montgomery in [[Law360]]: "Judge Craig A Karsnitz ruled that the articles ... were either true or protected under [[Communications Decency Act#Section 230|Section 230]] of the [[Communications Decency Act]]." [[Mike Leonard (journalist)|Mike Leonard]], writing for [[Bloomberg Law]], quoted Judge Karsnitz: "The article simply says that U.S. intelligence agencies were investigating reports of plaintiff's meetings with Russian officials, which Plaintiff admits is true."<ref name="multiple">Multiple sources: * {{cite web |last=Leonard |first=Mike |author-link=Mike Leonard (journalist) |date=February 11, 2021 |title=Carter Page Loses Libel Case Over 'Steele Dossier,' Russia Probe |work=[[Bloomberg Law]] |url=https://news.bloomberglaw.com/us-law-week/carter-page-loses-libel-case-over-steele-dossier-russia-probe |access-date=February 13, 2021 |archive-date=March 15, 2024 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20240315212055/https://news.bloomberglaw.com/us-law-week/carter-page-loses-libel-case-over-steele-dossier-russia-probe |ref=none}} * {{cite web |last=Montgomery |first=Jeff |date=February 11, 2021 |title=Del. Judge Nixes Carter Page Yahoo, HuffPost Defamation Suit | website=[[Law360]] |url=https://www.law360.com/corporate/articles/1354820/del-judge-nixes-carter-page-yahoo-huffpost-defamation-suit |access-date=February 13, 2021 |ref=none}}</ref>
==== Brokerage of Rosneft privatization ====
Dossier source(s) allege that Sechin "offered PAGE/TRUMP's associates the brokerage of up to a 19 per cent (privatised) stake in Rosneft" (worth about $11 billion) in exchange for Trump lifting the [[International sanctions during the Ukrainian crisis|sanctions against Russia]] after his election.<ref name="Bertrand_11/6/2017" /><ref name="Weindling_1/11/2017" /><ref name="Withnall_Sengupta_1/12/2017" /><ref name="Bertrand_1/27/2017" /><ref name="Tracy_11/7/2017" />
According to Harding, Sechin and Divyekin set this offer up as a [[carrot and stick]] scheme, in which the carrot was the brokerage fee ("in the region of tens and possibly hundreds of millions of dollars"), and the stick was [[blackmail]] over purported "damaging material on Trump" held by the Russian leadership.<ref name="Harding_2/3/2018">{{cite web |last=Harding |first=Luke |author-link=Luke Harding |title=Why Carter Page Was Worth Watching |magazine=[[Politico]] |date=February 3, 2018 |url=https://www.politico.com/magazine/story/2018/02/03/carter-page-nunes-memo-216934/ |access-date=November 30, 2019}}</ref>
About a month after Trump won the election, according to ''The Guardian'', Carter Page traveled to Moscow "shortly before the company announced it was selling a 19.5% stake" in Rosneft. He met with top Russian officials at Rosneft but denied meeting Sechin. He also complained about the effects of the sanctions against Russia.<ref name="Kirchgaessner_4/23/2017">{{cite news |last=Kirchgaessner |first=Stephanie |title=Russia 'targeted Trump adviser in bid to infiltrate campaign' |newspaper=[[The Guardian]] |date=April 23, 2017 |url=https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2017/apr/23/russia-tried-to-use-trump-advisers-to-influence-us-election-report |access-date=November 28, 2019}}</ref>
On December 7, 2016, Putin announced that a 19.5% stake in Rosneft was sold to [[Glencore]] and a [[Qatar]] fund. Public records showed the ultimate owner included "a [[Cayman Islands]] company whose beneficial owners cannot be traced", with "the main question" being "Who is the real buyer of a 19.5 percent stake in Rosneft? ... the Rosneft privatization uses a structure of shell companies owning shell companies."<ref>Multiple sources: * {{cite news |last=Golubkova |first=Katya |title=How Russia sold its oil jewel: without saying who bought it |work=[[Reuters]] |date=January 24, 2017 |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-russia-rosneft-privatisation-insight-idUSKBN1582OH |access-date=November 29, 2019 |ref=none}} * {{cite news |last1=Mazneva |first1=Elena |last2=Arkhipov |first2=Ilya |title=Russia Sells $11 Billion Stake in Rosneft to Glencore, Qatar |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |date=December 7, 2016 |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2016-12-07/glencore-qatar-fund-buy-russia-s-rosneft-stake-for-11-billion |access-date=November 29, 2019 |ref=none}} * {{cite news |last=Klein |first=Michael |title=Cayman company involved in $11B Russian oil privatization |newspaper=[[List of newspapers in the Cayman Islands|Cayman Compass]] |date=January 31, 2017 |url=https://www.caymancompass.com/2017/01/31/cayman-company-involved-in-11b-russian-oil-privatization/ |access-date=November 29, 2019 |ref=none}}</ref>
Michael Horowitz's 2019 inspector general report "said Steele's claims about Page 'remained uncorroborated' when the wiretaps ended in 2017".<ref name="Cohen_11/18/2021" /><ref name="Wemple_1/3/2020">{{cite news |last=Wemple |first=Erik |author-link=Erik Wemple |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/2020/01/03/dear-cnn-what-parts-steele-dossier-were-corroborated/ |title=Dear CNN: What parts of the Steele dossier were corroborated? |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=January 3, 2020 |access-date=December 3, 2021 |quote=However, the report released Dec. 9 by Michael Horowitz, the Justice Department's inspector general, says this about the dossier and Page: 'We determined that prior to and during the pendency of the FISAs, the FBI was unable to corroborate any of the specific substantive allegations against Carter Page contained in the election reporting [i.e., dossier] and relied on in the FISA applications.'}}</ref>
==== Trump's attempts to lift sanctions ====
The dossier says Page, claiming to speak with Trump's authority, had confirmed that Trump would lift the existing sanctions against Russia if he were elected president.<ref name="Withnall_Sengupta_1/12/2017" /> On December 29, 2016, during the transition period between the election and the inauguration, National Security Advisor designate Flynn spoke to Russian Ambassador [[Sergei Kislyak]], urging him not to retaliate for newly imposed sanctions; the Russians took his advice and did not retaliate.<ref name="Miller_Entous_Nakashima_2/2017">{{cite news |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/national-security/national-security-adviser-flynn-discussed-sanctions-with-russian-ambassador-despite-denials-officials-say/2017/02/09/f85b29d6-ee11-11e6-b4ff-ac2cf509efe5_story.html |title=National security adviser Flynn discussed sanctions with Russian ambassador, despite denials, officials say |first1=Greg |last1=Miller |first2=Adam |last2=Entous |first3=Ellen |last3=Nakashima |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=February 10, 2017 |access-date=April 1, 2018}}</ref>
Within days after the inauguration, new Trump administration officials ordered State Department staffers to develop proposals for immediately revoking the economic and other sanctions.<ref name="Isikoff_6/1/2017" /> One retired diplomat later said, "What was troubling about these stories is that suddenly I was hearing that we were preparing to rescind sanctions in exchange for, well, nothing."<ref name="Dilanian_3/4/2018">{{cite news |url=https://www.nbcnews.com/politics/white-house/former-diplomats-trump-team-sought-lift-sanctions-russia-n767406 |title=Former Diplomats: Trump Team Sought to Lift Sanctions on Russia |last=Dilanian |first=Ken |author-link=Ken Dilanian |date=June 1, 2017 |work=[[NBC News]] |access-date=March 4, 2018}}</ref> The staffers alerted Congressional allies who took steps to codify the sanctions into law. The attempt to overturn the sanctions was abandoned after Flynn's conversation was revealed and Flynn resigned.<ref name="Isikoff_6/1/2017">{{cite news |url=https://www.yahoo.com/news/trump-administrations-secret-efforts-ease-russia-sanctions-fell-short-231301145.html |title=How the Trump administration's secret efforts to ease Russia sanctions fell short |last=Isikoff |first=Michael |author-link=Michael Isikoff |date=June 1, 2017 |work=[[Yahoo! News]] |access-date=March 4, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Siegel_6/2/2017">{{cite news | last=Siegel | first=Robert | title=Trump Administration Made Secret Efforts To Ease Russia Sanctions |work=[[NPR]] | date=June 2, 2017 | url=https://www.npr.org/2017/06/02/531269090/trump-administration-made-secret-efforts-to-ease-russia-sanctions | access-date=August 28, 2022}}</ref><ref name="Sipher_9/6/2017" /> In August 2017, Congress passed a bipartisan bill to impose new sanctions on Russia. Trump reluctantly signed the bill but then refused to implement it.<ref name="Porter_1/30/2018">{{cite news |url=https://www.newsweek.com/trump-refuses-impose-new-sanctions-russia-because-threats-are-enough-794831 |title=Trump refuses to impose new sanctions on Russia |last=Porter |first=Tom |date=January 30, 2018 |work=[[Newsweek]] |access-date=March 4, 2018}}</ref> After Trump hired Manafort, his approach toward Ukraine changed; he said he might recognize Crimea as Russian territory and might lift the sanctions against Russia.<ref name="Crowley_8/3/2016" />
Among those sanctioned were Russian oligarchs like [[Oleg Deripaska]], "who is linked to Paul Manafort", parliament member [[Konstantin Kosachev]], banker [[Aleksandr Torshin]], and Putin's son-in-law. Preparation for the sanctions started already before Trump took office.<ref name="Gordon_Hall_Kumar_Stone_4/6/2018">{{cite news |last1=Gordon |first1=Greg |last2=Hall |first2=Kevin G. |last3=Kumar |first3=Anita |last4=Stone |first4=Peter |title=Sanctioned Russians include dossier figure and banker linked to NRA |agency=[[The McClatchy Company|McClatchy DC Bureau]] |date=April 6, 2018 |url=https://www.mcclatchydc.com/news/nation-world/national/article208180169.html |access-date=March 15, 2019}}</ref> In January 2019, Trump's Treasury Department lifted the sanctions on companies formerly controlled by Deripaska. Sanctions on Deripaska himself remained in effect.<ref name="CNBC_1/27/2019">{{cite news |title=US lifts sanctions on Rusal and other firms linked to Russian oligarch Deripaska |work=[[CNBC]] |date=January 27, 2019 |url=https://www.cnbc.com/2019/01/27/u-s-lifts-sanctions-on-rusal-other-firms-linked-to-russias-deripaska.html |access-date=March 15, 2019 |archive-date=April 1, 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190401093634/https://www.cnbc.com/2019/01/27/u-s-lifts-sanctions-on-rusal-other-firms-linked-to-russias-deripaska.html |url-status=dead}}</ref>
=== Cohen and alleged Prague visit ===
Dossier source(s) allege that Cohen and three colleagues met Kremlin officials in the Prague offices of [[Rossotrudnichestvo]] in August 2016,<ref name="Stone_Gordon_4/13/2018" /><ref name="Withnall_Sengupta_1/12/2017" /><ref name="Cormier_5/5/2017" /> to arrange for payments to the hackers, cover up the hack,<ref name="Harding_5/10/2017" /><ref name="Borger_4/28/2017" /> and "cover up ties between Trump and Russia, including Manafort's involvement in Ukraine".<ref name="Sumter_11/16/2017" /> [[McClatchy]] reported in 2018 that a phone of Cohen's was traced to the Prague area in late summer 2016.<ref name="Stone_Gordon_12/27/2018" /> The April 2019 [[Mueller Report]] states "Cohen had never traveled to Prague".<ref name="Bort_Dickinson_Kroll_4/18/2019">{{cite magazine |last1=Bort |first1=Ryan |last2=Dickinson |first2=Tim |last3=Kroll |first3=Andy |title=6 Scandals the Mueller Report Puts to Rest |magazine=[[Rolling Stone]] |date=April 18, 2019 |url=https://www.rollingstone.com/politics/politics-news/6-scandals-the-mueller-report-puts-to-rest-824441/ |access-date=September 2, 2021}}</ref> The December 2019 Horowitz Report stated that the FBI "concluded that these allegations against Cohen" in the dossier "were not true".<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|176}}
According to the ''Wall Street Journal'', Olga Galkina was Steele's source for the hacking accusations against Webzilla, and the source of the allegations about a secret meeting in Prague involving Michael Cohen and three colleagues.<ref name="Cullison_Gauthier-Villars_10/28/2020" />
In April 2018, [[The McClatchy Company|McClatchy DC Bureau]], citing two sources, reported that investigators working for Mueller "have traced evidence that Cohen entered the Czech Republic through Germany, apparently during August or early September 2016",<ref name="Stone_Gordon_4/13/2018" /> a claim that ''[[The Spectator]]'' reported in July 2018 was "backed up by one intelligence source in London".<ref name="Cockburn_7/3/2018">{{cite magazine |last=Cockburn |title=What does Michael Cohen know? |magazine=[[The Spectator]] |date=July 3, 2018 |url=https://usa.spectator.co.uk/2018/07/what-does-michael-cohen-know/ |access-date=July 4, 2018 |archive-date=July 4, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180704092856/https://usa.spectator.co.uk/2018/07/what-does-michael-cohen-know/ |url-status=dead }}</ref>
In August 2018, ''The Spectator'' reported that "one intelligence source" said "Mueller is examining 'electronic records' that would place Cohen in Prague."<ref name="Wood_8/25/2018">{{cite magazine |last=Wood |first=Paul |author-link=Paul Wood (journalist) |title=Trumpworld is spinning out of control |magazine=[[The Spectator]] |date=August 25, 2018 |url=https://www.spectator.co.uk/2018/08/trumpworld-is-spinning-out-of-control/ |access-date=September 29, 2018}}</ref> In December 2018, McClatchy reported that a phone of Cohen's had "pinged" [[Cell site|cellphone towers]] in the Prague area in late summer 2016, citing four sources, leading to foreign intelligence detecting the pings.<ref name="Stone_Gordon_12/27/2018" /> McClatchy also reported that during that time an [[Eastern European]] intelligence agency had intercepted communications between Russians, one of whom mentioned that Cohen was in Prague.<ref name="Stone_Gordon_12/27/2018">{{cite news |last1=Stone |first1=Peter |last2=Gordon |first2=Greg |title=Cell signal puts Cohen outside Prague around time of purported Russian meeting |agency=[[The McClatchy Company|McClatchy DC Bureau]] |date=December 27, 2018 |url=https://www.mcclatchydc.com/news/investigations/article219016820.html |access-date=December 27, 2018}}</ref>
The ''Washington Post'' sent a team of reporters to Prague in an attempt to verify that Cohen had been there for the purposes alleged in the Dossier. According to reporter Greg Miller in November 2018, they "came away empty".<ref name="C-SPAN_11/16/2018">{{cite news |title=WaPo National Security reporter says CIA and FBI sources doubt major Russia dossier allegation about Cohen and Prague |work=[[C-SPAN]] |date=November 16, 2018 |url=http://www.c-span.org/video/?c4768355/wapo-national-security-reporter-cia-fbi-sources-doubt-major-russia-dossier-allegation-cohen-prague |access-date=March 26, 2019}}</ref>
In April 2019, ''The New York Times'' reported that when the FBI attempted to verify the dossier's claims, the Prague allegation "appeared to be false", as "Cohen's financial records and C.I.A. queries to foreign intelligence services revealed nothing to support it."<ref name="Shane_Goldman_Rosenberg_4/19/2019" />
Also in April 2019, the [[Mueller Report]] mentioned that "Cohen had never traveled to Prague"<ref name="Bort_Dickinson_Kroll_4/18/2019" /> and presented no evidence of the alleged Prague meeting,<ref name="Cullison_Gauthier-Villars_10/28/2020" /><ref name="Drobnic_Holan_7/26/2019">{{cite web |last=Drobnic Holan |first=Angie |date=July 26, 2019 |title=PolitiFact's Mueller Report Book Club, Volume 2 | website=[[PolitiFact]] |url=https://www.politifact.com/article/2019/jul/26/politifacts-mueller-report-book-club-volume-2/ |access-date=September 2, 2021}}</ref> thus contradicting the dossier and the McClatchy report.<ref name="Bauder_4/20/2019">{{cite news |last=Bauder |first=David |title=Fake news? Mueller isn't buying it |agency=[[Associated Press]] |date=April 20, 2019 |url=https://apnews.com/article/donald-trump-ap-top-news-us-news-russia-elections-06c88719c3634e66a15adbbc658978b0 |access-date=September 2, 2021}}</ref> Glenn Kessler, fact-checker for ''The Washington Post'', has said that "Mueller does not indicate he investigated whether Cohen traveled to Prague; he simply dismisses the incident in Cohen's own words".<ref name="Kessler_4/24/2019" /> McClatchy responded to the Mueller Report by stating that it did not refer to evidence that Cohen's phone had pinged in or near Prague.<ref name="Hall_4/18/2019">{{cite news |last=Hall |first=Kevin G. |title=Mueller report states Cohen was not in Prague. It is silent on whether a Cohen device pinged there |agency=[[The McClatchy Company|McClatchy DC Bureau]] |date=April 18, 2019 |url=https://www.mcclatchydc.com/news/investigations/article229424084.html |access-date=April 19, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Blake_4/22/2019">{{cite news |last=Blake |first=Aaron |title=What the media got right{{snd}}and wrong{{snd}}about the Mueller report |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=April 22, 2019 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2019/04/22/what-media-got-right-wrong-about-mueller-report/ |access-date=August 3, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Shane_Goldman_Rosenberg_4/19/2019" /> McClatchy stood by its December 2018 reporting, stating that there was a "possibility that Cohen was not there but one of the many phones he used was".<ref name="Hall_4/18/2019" />
The Associated Press described a December 2019 Horowitz Report mention of an inaccuracy in the dossier regarding Michael Cohen that may have been the Prague allegation.<ref name="Tucker_4/16/2020">{{cite news |last=Tucker |first=Eric |title=New information adds to questions about Russia probe dossier |agency=[[Associated Press]] |date=April 16, 2020 |url=https://apnews.com/article/donald-trump-politics-russia-trump-investigations-latin-america-d6960fe493d0eb65791778f2f5a4c131 |access-date=September 4, 2021}}</ref> [[Matt Taibbi]] wrote that news reports of the Cohen-Prague allegation were "either incorrect or lacking factual foundation".<ref name="Taibbi_12/17/2019">{{cite magazine |last=Taibbi |first=Matt |title=Five Questions Still Remaining After the Release of the Horowitz Report |magazine=[[Rolling Stone]] |date=December 17, 2019 |url=https://www.rollingstone.com/politics/politics-features/horowitz-report-russia-investigation-questions-remaining-928081/ |access-date=September 4, 2021}}</ref> CNN interpreted the Horowitz Report as saying that the dossier's Cohen-Prague allegation was untrue.<ref name="Polantz_12/9/2019">{{cite news |last=Polantz |first=Katelyn |title=How the FBI attempted to verify a salacious allegation in the Steele dossier |work=[[CNN]] |date=December 9, 2019 |url=https://www.cnn.com/2019/12/09/politics/fbi-steele-dossier/index.html |access-date=September 4, 2021}}</ref>
In August 2020, the testimony of David Kramer was publicized, where he said Steele was uncertain about the "alleged Cohen trip to Prague". Kramer said: "it could have been in Prague, it could have been outside of Prague. He also thought there was a possibility it could have been in Budapest ... [but Steele] never backed off the idea that Cohen was in Europe."<ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" />{{rp|866}} In October 2021, "When asked why Cohen would not admit to the alleged meeting despite already being convicted of other crimes, Steele replied: 'I think it's so incriminating and demeaning. ... And the other reason is he might be scared of the consequences'."<ref name="Tillman_10/18/2021">{{cite web | last=Tillman | first=Rachel | title=Christopher Steele, author of Trump dossier, defends report | work=[[Spectrum News NY1]] | date=October 18, 2021 | url=https://www.ny1.com/nyc/all-boroughs/news/2021/10/18/christopher-steele-dossier-trump-russia-investigation | access-date=October 21, 2021}}</ref>
=== Republican position on Russian conflict with Ukraine and related sanctions ===
In 2015, Trump had taken a hard line in favor of Ukraine's independence from Russia. He initially denounced Russia's annexation of Crimea as a "land grab" that "should never have happened", and called for a firmer U.S. response, saying "We should definitely be strong. We should definitely do sanctions."<ref name="Crowley_8/3/2016">{{cite web |url=https://www.politico.com/story/2016/08/trump-manafort-ukraine-crimea-russia-226573 |title=Trump changed views on Ukraine after hiring Manafort |last=Crowley |first=Michael |date=August 3, 2016 |newspaper=[[Politico]] |access-date=March 3, 2018}}</ref>
With the hirings of Paul Manafort and Carter Page, Trump's approach toward Ukraine reversed. Manafort had worked for Russian interests in Ukraine for many years, and after hiring Manafort as his campaign manager, Trump said he might recognize Crimea as Russian territory and might lift the sanctions against Russia.<ref name="Crowley_8/3/2016" /> At the time Trump appointed Carter Page as a foreign policy advisor, Page was known as an outspoken and strongly pro-Russian, anti-sanctions person whose views aligned with Trump's, and who had complained that his own, as well as his Russian friends', business interests were negatively affected by the sanctions imposed on Russia because of its aggression in Ukraine and its interference in the 2016 elections.<ref name="Isikoff_9/23/2016">{{cite web |last=Isikoff |first=Michael |author-link=Michael Isikoff |title=U.S. intel officials probe ties between Trump adviser and Kremlin |work=[[Yahoo! News]] |date=September 23, 2016 |url=https://www.yahoo.com/news/u-s-intel-officials-probe-ties-between-trump-adviser-and-kremlin-175046002.html |access-date=November 24, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Mider_3/30/2016">{{cite news |last=Mider |first=Zachary |title=Trump's New Russia Adviser Has Deep Ties to Kremlin's Gazprom |work=[[Bloomberg News]] |date=March 30, 2016 |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2016-03-30/trump-russia-adviser-carter-page-interview |access-date=November 24, 2019}}</ref>
Dossier source(s) allege that "the Trump campaign agreed to minimize US opposition to Russia's incursions into Ukraine".<ref name="Bertrand_9/22/2017">{{cite web |last=Bertrand |first=Natasha |author-link=Natasha Bertrand |title=Former Trump adviser: I gave the campaign 'the chance to intervene' in controversial Ukraine platform change | website=[[Business Insider]] |date=September 22, 2017 |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/trump-russia-campaign-platform-rnc-jd-gordon-2017-9 |access-date=January 23, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Bertrand_1/15/2017" /> Harding considers this allegation to have been confirmed by the actions of the Trump campaign: "This is precisely what happened at the Republican National Convention last July, when language on the US's commitment to Ukraine was mysteriously softened."<ref name="Harding_5/10/2017" /> ''The Washington Post'' reported that "the Trump campaign orchestrated a set of events" in July 2016 "to soften the language of an amendment to the Republican Party's draft policy on Ukraine."<ref name="Rogin_7/18/2016">{{cite news |last=Rogin |first=Josh |title=Trump campaign guts GOP's anti-Russia stance on Ukraine |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=July 18, 2016 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/global-opinions/trump-campaign-guts-gops-anti-russia-stance-on-ukraine/2016/07/18/98adb3b0-4cf3-11e6-a7d8-13d06b37f256_story.html |access-date=March 22, 2019}}</ref> In July 2016, the [[Republican National Convention]] did make changes to the Republican Party's platform on Ukraine: initially the platform proposed providing "lethal weapons" to Ukraine, but the line was changed to "appropriate assistance".
[[NPR]] reported that "Diana Denman, a Republican delegate who supported arming U.S. allies in Ukraine, has told people that Trump aide J.D. Gordon said at the Republican Convention in 2016 that Trump directed him to support weakening that position in the official platform."<ref name="Johnson_12/4/2017">{{cite news |last=Johnson |first=Carrie |title=2016 RNC Delegate: Trump Directed Change To Party Platform On Ukraine Support |work=[[NPR]] |date=December 4, 2017 |url=https://www.npr.org/2017/12/04/568310790/2016-rnc-delegate-trump-directed-change-to-party-platform-on-ukraine-support |access-date=January 23, 2018}}</ref> [[J. D. Gordon]], who was one of Trump's national security advisers during the campaign, said he had advocated for changing language because that reflected what Trump had said.<ref name="Bertrand_2/11/2017" /><ref name="Murray_Acosta_Schleifer_3/4/2017">{{cite news |last1=Murray |first1=Sara |last2=Acosta |first2=Jim |last3=Schleifer |first3=Theodore |title=More Trump advisers disclose meetings with Russia's ambassador |url=https://edition.cnn.com/2017/03/02/politics/russia-donald-trump-meetings-ambassador/ |date=March 4, 2017 |access-date=April 10, 2017 |work=[[CNN]]}}</ref> Although the Trump team denied any role in softening the language, Denman confirmed that the change "definitely came from Trump staffers".<ref name="Bertrand_2/5/2017">{{cite web |last=Bertrand |first=Natasha |author-link=Natasha Bertrand |title=Trump's first big test with Putin harkens back to one of the most controversial elements of his campaign | website=[[Business Insider]] |date=February 5, 2017 |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/russia-putin-trump-ukraine-2017-2 |access-date=March 22, 2019}}</ref>
[[Kyle Cheney (journalist)|Kyle Cheney]] of ''[[Politico]]'' sees evidence that the change was "on the campaign's radar" because Carter Page congratulated campaign members in an email the day after the platform amendment: "As for the Ukraine amendment, excellent work."<ref name="Cheney_4/1/2018">{{cite web |last=Cheney |first=Kyle |title=Mueller interested in 2016 convention episode dismissed by House GOP |newspaper=[[Politico]] |date=April 1, 2018 |url=https://www.politico.com/story/2018/05/01/mueller-republican-convention-ukraine-563701 |access-date=December 20, 2018}}</ref> Paul Manafort falsely said that the change "absolutely did not come from the Trump campaign".<ref name="Mak_Corse_8/3/2016">{{cite web |url=https://www.thedailybeast.com/trump-campaign-changed-ukraine-platform-lied-about-it |title=Trump Campaign Changed Ukraine Platform, Lied About It |last1=Mak |first1=Tim |last2=Corse |first2=Alexa |date=August 3, 2016 | website=[[The Daily Beast]] |access-date=March 3, 2018}}</ref> Trump told [[George Stephanopoulos]] that people in his campaign were responsible for changing the Republican party's platform stance on Ukraine, but he denied personal involvement.<ref name="ABC_Trump_Stephanopoulos_7/31/2016">{{cite news |title='This Week' Transcript: Donald Trump, Vice President Joe Biden, and Ret. Gen. John Allen |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/week-transcript-donald-trump-vice-president-joe-biden/story?id=41020870 |date=July 31, 2016 |work=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] |series=[[This Week (ABC TV series)|This Week]] |access-date=April 18, 2018}}</ref>
=== Relations with Europe and NATO ===
[[File:Владимир Путин (18-06-2023) (cropped).jpg|thumb|upright=0.6|right|[[Vladimir Putin]] (2023)]] Dossier source(s) allege that, as part of a ''[[quid pro quo]]'' agreement, "the TRUMP team had agreed ... to raise US/NATO defense commitments in the Baltics and Eastern Europe to deflect attention away from Ukraine, a priority for PUTIN who needed to cauterise the subject."<ref name="Bertrand_1/15/2017" /> Aiko Stevenson, writing in ''HuffPost'', noted that some of Trump's actions seem to align with "Putin's wish list", that "includes lifting sanctions on Russia, turning a blind eye towards its aggressive efforts in the Ukraine, and creating a divisive rift amongst western allies."<ref name="Stevenson_1/18/2017" /> During the campaign Trump "called NATO, the centrepiece of Transatlantic security, 'obsolete', championed the disintegration of the EU, and said that he is open to lifting sanctions on Moscow."<ref name="Stevenson_1/18/2017">{{cite web |last=Stevenson |first=Aiko |title=President Trump: The Manchurian Candidate? | website=[[HuffPost]] |date=January 18, 2017 |url=https://www.huffingtonpost.com/aiko-stevenson/president-trump-the-manchurian-candidate_b_14236674.html |access-date=January 21, 2018}}</ref> Harding adds that Trump repeatedly "questioned whether US allies were paying enough into Nato coffers".<ref name="Harding_5/10/2017" /> [[Jeff Stein (author)|Jeff Stein]], writing in ''[[Newsweek]]'', described how "Trump's repeated attacks on NATO have ... frustrated ... allies ... [and] raised questions as to whether the president has been duped into facilitating Putin's long-range objective of undermining the European Union."<ref name="Stein_12/21/2017">{{cite news |last=Stein |first=Jeff |date=December 21, 2017 |title=Putin's Man in the White House? Real Trump Russia Scandal is Not Mere Collusion, U.S. Counterspies Say |work=[[Newsweek]] |url=https://www.newsweek.com/trump-putin-man-white-house-russia-investigation-scandal-moscow-kremlin-755321 |access-date=March 9, 2018}}</ref>
Nancy LeTourneau tied dossier allegations with Trump's attacks on NATO and reminded readers of "what Vladimir Putin wanted when, back in about 2011, he started courting Donald Trump as basically a Russian asset". She then quoted the dossier:<ref name="LeTourneau_6/28/2018">{{cite web | last=LeTourneau | first=Nancy | title=Trump and the Russians Disagree on One Thing | website=[[Washington Monthly]] | date=June 28, 2018 | url=http://washingtonmonthly.com/2018/06/28/trump-and-the-russians-disagree-on-one-thing/ | access-date=April 11, 2023}}</ref> {{blockquote| [The Trump operation's] aim was to sow discord and disunity within the U.S. itself, but more especially within the Transatlantic alliance which was viewed as inimical to Russia's interests. Source C, a senior Russian financial official, said the Trump operation should be seen in terms of Putin's desire to return to Nineteenth Century 'Great Power' politics anchored upon countries' interests rather than the ideals-based international order established after World War Two.}}
Trump's appearances at meetings with allies, including NATO and G7, have frequently been antagonistic; according to the ''Los Angeles Times'', "The president's posture toward close allies has been increasingly and remarkably confrontational this year, especially in comparison to his more conciliatory approach to adversaries, including Russia and North Korea."<ref name="Stokols_7/11/2018">{{cite news |last=Stokols |first=Eli |title=Trump disrupts NATO summit with blasts at allies, especially Germany, and new defense spending demands |newspaper=[[Los Angeles Times]] |date=July 11, 2018 |url=https://www.latimes.com/politics/la-na-pol-trump-nato-20180711-story.html |access-date=July 23, 2018}}</ref>
=== Spy withdrawn from Russian embassy ===
Dossier source(s) allege that "a leading Russian diplomat, Mikhail KULAGIN"{{sic}} participated in U.S. election meddling, and was recalled to Moscow because the Kremlin was concerned his role in the meddling would be exposed. The BBC later reported that U.S. officials in 2016 had identified Russian diplomat Mikhail Kalugin as a spy and that he was under surveillance, thus "verifying" a key claim in the dossier.<ref name="Wood_3/30/2017" /> Kalugin was the head of the economics section at the Russian embassy. He returned to Russia in August 2016.<ref name="Borger_10/7/2017" /> McClatchy reported that the FBI was investigating whether Kalugin played a role in the election interference. Kalugin has denied the allegations.<ref name="Borger_10/7/2017" /><ref name="Hall_Stone_Gordon-Goldstein_2/15/2017">{{cite news |last1=Hall |first1=Kevin G |last2=Stone |first2=Peter |last3=Gordon |first3=Greg |last4=Goldstein |first4=David |title=Russian diplomat under U.S. scrutiny in election meddling speaks |agency=[[The McClatchy Company|McClatchy DC Bureau]] |date=February 15, 2017 |url=https://www.mcclatchydc.com/news/nation-world/national/article132761149.html |access-date=January 27, 2018}}</ref>
=== Botnets and porn traffic by hackers ===
The validity of the accusation that Aleksej Gubarev's "XBT/Webzilla and its affiliates had been using [[botnet]]s and porn traffic to transmit viruses, plant bugs, steal data and conduct 'altering operations' against the Democratic Party leadership"<ref name="Goldstein_Hall_Gordon_2/3/2017" /> has been affirmed by an unsealed report by [[FTI Consulting]], which was commissioned by ''BuzzFeed'' for use in the defamation suit(s) Gubarev had filed against others.<ref name="Rosenberg_3/14/2019">{{cite news |last=Rosenberg |first=Matthew |author-link=Matthew Rosenberg |title=Tech Firm in Steele Dossier May Have Been Used by Russian Spies |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |date=March 14, 2019 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2019/03/14/us/politics/gubarev-steele-dossier-trump-russia.html |access-date=March 15, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Hall_3/14/2019">{{cite news |last=Hall |first=Kevin G. |title=Expert in Trump dossier trial says tech firm's services were used in hack of Democrats |agency=[[The McClatchy Company|McClatchy DC Bureau]] |date=March 14, 2019 |url=https://www.mcclatchydc.com/news/politics-government/article227819279.html |access-date=March 15, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Helderman_Hamburger_Nakashima_3/15/2019">{{cite news |last1=Helderman |first1=Rosalind S. |author-link1=Rosalind S. Helderman |last2=Hamburger |first2=Tom |author-link2=Tom Hamburger |last3=Nakashima |first3=Ellen |title=Documents shed light on Russian hacking of Democratic Party leaders |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=March 15, 2019 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/documents-shed-light-on-russian-hacking-of-democratic-party-leaders/2019/03/14/dd00ad68-4694-11e9-8aab-95b8d80a1e4f_story.html |access-date=March 15, 2019}}</ref>
The report by FTI Consulting said:<ref name="Rosenberg_3/14/2019" /> {{blockquote| Mr. Gubarev's companies have provided gateways to the internet for cybercriminals and Russian state-sponsored actors to launch and control large scale malware campaigns over the past decade. Gubarev and other XBT executives do not appear to actively prevent cybercriminals from using their infrastructure.}}
Cybersecurity and intelligence expert Andrew Weisburd has said both Gubarev and the dossier "can be right": "Their explanation is entirely plausible, as is the Steele Dossier's description of Mr. Gubarev as essentially a victim of predatory officers of one or more Russian intelligence services. ... Neither ''BuzzFeed'' nor Steele have accused Gubarev of being a willing participant in wrongdoing."<ref name="Hall_9/21/2018" /> XBT has denied the allegations, and "findings do not prove or disprove claims made about XBT in the dossier, but show how the company could have been used by cyber criminals, wittingly or unwittingly".<ref name="Hall_9/21/2018" />
According to the ''Wall Street Journal'', Steele's source for the hacking accusations against Webzilla was Olga Galkina, who was involved in a "messy dispute" with the firm "after being fired in November 2016".<ref name="Cullison_Gauthier-Villars_10/28/2020" />
== Dossier's veracity and Steele's reputation ==
{{see also|#Discrepancies between sources and their allegations}} Steele and the dossier became "the central point of contention in the political brawl raging around"<ref name="Hamburger_Helderman_2/6/2018" /> the [[Special Counsel investigation (2017–present)|Special counsel investigation]] into [[Russian interference in the 2016 United States elections]]. Russian intelligence agencies may have sought to create doubt about the veracity of the dossier.<ref name="Mendick_1/20/2019">{{cite news |last=Mendick |first=Robert |date=January 20, 2019 |title=Kremlin accused of laying false trail linking Sergei Skripal to ex-MI6 officer behind Trump dossier |url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/2019/01/20/kremlin-accused-laying-false-trail-linking-sergei-skripal-ex/ |access-date=January 20, 2019 |newspaper=[[The Daily Telegraph]]}}</ref> Those who believed Steele considered him a hero who tried to warn about the Kremlin's meddling in the election, and people who distrusted him considered him a "hired gun" used to attack Trump.<ref name="Hamburger_Helderman_2/6/2018" /> Glenn Kessler described the dossier as "a political [[Rorschach test]]. Depending on your perspective, it's either a hoax used to defame a future president or a credible guide to allegations about Trump's involvement with Russia."<ref name="Kessler_4/24/2019" /> According to former ''New York Times'' reporter [[Barry Meier]], some [[MI6]] officials said that Steele "had a tendency to become obsessed and go down rabbit holes chasing targets of questionable value".<ref name="Meier_Spooked" />{{rp|237}}
Following the dossier's release, Steele completely avoided on-camera interviews until he participated in an ABC News documentary that was aired on [[Hulu]] on October 18, 2021. In that documentary, Steele maintained that his sources were credible and that it was typical in intelligence investigations to "never get to the point where you're 99% certain of the evidence to secure a conviction". Steele also acknowledged that one of his sources had faced repercussions; he confirmed that the source was still alive, but he would not provide further details.<ref name="Pellish_Herb_10/18/2021">{{cite news |last1=Pellish |first1=Aaron |last2=Herb |first2=Jeremy |title=Ex-intel official who created controversial Trump Russia dossier speaks out |work=[[CNN]] |date=October 18, 2021 |url=https://www.cnn.com/2021/10/17/politics/christopher-steele-dossier-trump/index.html |access-date=October 18, 2021}}</ref>
Some dossier assertions were later accepted as fact:<ref name="Perez_Prokupecz_Brown_10/25/2017" /> {{blockquote| While the most salacious allegations in the dossier haven't been verified, its broad assertion that Russia waged a campaign to interfere in the election is now accepted as fact by the US intelligence community. CNN also reported earlier this year that US investigators have corroborated some aspects of the dossier, specifically that some of the communications among foreign nationals mentioned in the memos did actually take place.}}
With the passage of time and further revelations from various investigations and sources, it is becoming clearer that the overall thrust of the dossier was accurate:<ref name="Donn_6/29/2018">{{cite news |last=Donn |first=Jeff |title=Some questions in Trump–Russia dossier now finding answers |date=June 29, 2018 |agency=[[Associated Press]] |url=https://apnews.com/55b8a55d5a7545b99d0f915affb7d852 |access-date=June 29, 2018}}</ref> {{blockquote| Some of the dossier's broad threads have now been independently corroborated. U.S. intelligence agencies and the special counsel's investigation into Russian election interference did eventually find that Kremlin-linked operatives ran an elaborate operation to promote Trump and hurt Democratic opponent Hillary Clinton, as the dossier says in its main narrative.}}
Shepard Smith said: "Some of the assertions in the dossier have been confirmed. Other parts are unconfirmed. None of the dossier, to Fox News's knowledge, has been disproven."<ref name="Hutzler_8//16/2018">{{cite news |url=https://www.newsweek.com/fox-news-host-contradicts-sean-hannity-trump-over-dossier-claims-1076388 |title=Fox News Host Contradicts Sean Hannity, Trump Over Dossier Claims |last=Hutzler |first=Alexandra |date=August 16, 2018 |work=[[Newsweek]] |access-date=August 18, 2018}}</ref> In some cases, public verification is hindered because the information is classified.<ref name="Berke_6/8/2017">{{cite web |last=Berke |first=Jeremy |title=Comey's cryptic answer about the infamous Trump dossier makes it look likely it could be verified | website=[[Business Insider]] |date=June 8, 2017 |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/comey-steele-trump-russia-dossier-will-be-verified-2017-6 |access-date=January 23, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Truscott_8/18/2018">{{cite web |last=Truscott IV |first=Lucian K. |date=August 18, 2018 |title=The people on Trump's list aren't enemies, they are witnesses. The secrets they have aren't secrets anymore. They're evidence | website=[[Salon.com|Salon]] |url=https://www.salon.com/2018/08/18/the-people-on-trumps-list-arent-enemies-they-are-witnesses/ |access-date=August 18, 2018}}</ref>
According to Ranking Member of the House Intelligence Committee [[Adam Schiff]], a major portion of the dossier's content is about Russian efforts to help Trump, and those allegations "turned out to be true".<ref name="Tau_11/15/2017" />
After the [[Mueller Report]] was released, Joshua Levy, counsel for Fusion GPS, issued this statement:<ref name="Kessler_4/24/2019" /> {{blockquote| The Mueller Report substantiates the core reporting and many of the specifics in Christopher Steele's 2016 memoranda, including that Trump campaign figures were secretly meeting Kremlin figures, that Russia was conducting a covert operation to elect Donald Trump, and that the aim of the Russian operation was to sow discord and disunity in the US and within the Transatlantic Alliance. To our knowledge, nothing in the Steele memoranda has been disproven.}}
The [[United States Department of Justice Office of the Inspector General|Inspector General]] investigation by [[Michael E. Horowitz]], published December 9, 2019, expressed doubts about the dossier's reliability and sources:<ref name="Wemple_12/18/2019">{{cite news |last=Wemple |first=Erik |author-link=Erik Wemple |title=CNN lands an interview with its own contributor |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=December 18, 2019 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/opinions/2019/12/18/cnn-lands-an-interview-with-its-own-contributor/ |access-date=December 27, 2019}}</ref><ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|172}} {{blockquote| The FBI concluded, among other things, that although consistent with known efforts by Russia to interfere in the 2016 U.S. elections, much of the material in the Steele election reports, including allegations about Donald Trump and members of the Trump campaign relied upon in the Carter Page FISA applications, could not be corroborated; that certain allegations were inaccurate or inconsistent with information gathered by the Crossfire Hurricane team; and that the limited information that was corroborated related to time, location, and title information, much of which was publicly available.}}
According to the May 2023 [[Durham special counsel investigation#Final report|Durham report]], "the FBI was not able to corroborate a single substantive allegation contained in the Steele Reports, despite protracted efforts to do so."<ref name="Durham_5/12/2023" />{{rp|99}}
=== Reputation in the U.S. intelligence community ===
The process of evaluating Steele's information has been explained by [[E. W. Priestap|Bill Priestap]], at the time the Assistant Director of the [[FBI Counterintelligence Division]]:<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|102}} {{blockquote| We did not ever take the information he provided at face value. ... We went to great lengths to try to independently verify the source's credibility and to prove or disprove every single assertion in the dossier. ... We absolutely understood that the information in the so-called dossier could be inaccurate. We also understood that some parts could be true and other parts false. We understood that information could be embellished or exaggerated. We also understood that the information could have been provided by the Russians as part of a disinformation campaign.}}
"Independent evidence about Trump as a potential victim of sexual blackmail emerged soon after the publication of the dossier."<ref name="Manne_2/20/2017">{{cite web | last=Manne | first=Robert | title=Decoding Trump and the Russians | website=[[La Trobe University]] | date=February 20, 2017 | url=https://www.latrobe.edu.au/news/articles/2017/opinion/decoding-trump-and-the-russians | access-date=December 15, 2023 | quote=Originally published] on February 18, 2017, by ''[[The Australian]]''.}}</ref> On January 12, 2017, [[Paul Wood (journalist)|Paul Wood]], of [[BBC News]], wrote that Steele was "not the only source" for claims about Russian ''kompromat'' on Trump and that multiple intelligence sources were privately reporting about it before<!-- Wood wrote about this two days after the dossier's publication.--> the publication of the dossier:<ref name="Wood_compromising_1/12/2017" /> {{blockquote| Back in August, a retired spy told me he had been informed of its existence by 'the head of an East European intelligence agency'. Later, I used an intermediary to pass some questions to active duty CIA officers dealing with the case file – they would not speak to me directly. I got a message back that there was "more than one tape", "audio and video", on "more than one date", in "more than one place" – in the Ritz-Carlton in Moscow and also in St Petersburg – and that the material was "of a sexual nature". The claims of Russian ''kompromat'' on Mr Trump were "credible", the CIA believed.}}
While also mentioning that "nobody should believe something just because an intelligence agent says it",<ref name="Lange_1/11/2017">{{cite news |url=https://theweek.com/speedreads/672669/bbc-claims-second-source-backs-trump-dossier |title=BBC claims a second source backs up Trump dossier |date=January 11, 2017 |first=Jeva |last=Lange |work=[[The Week]] |access-date=April 1, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Hope_Rothfeld_Cullison_1/11/2017" /> Wood added that "the CIA believes it is credible that the Kremlin has such ''kompromat''—or compromising material—on the next US commander in chief" and "a joint taskforce, that includes the CIA and the FBI, has been investigating allegations that the Russians may have sent money to Mr Trump's organisation or his election campaign".<ref name="Wood_compromising_1/12/2017">{{cite news |last=Wood |first=Paul |author-link=Paul Wood (journalist) |title=Trump 'compromising' claims: How and why did we get here? |work=[[BBC News]] |date=January 12, 2017 |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-38589427 |access-date=February 19, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Drum_1/12/2017">{{cite magazine |last=Drum |first=Kevin |author-link=Kevin Drum |url=https://www.motherjones.com/kevin-drum/2017/01/bbcs-paul-wood-there-are-four-sources-possible-trump-russia-blackmail |title=BBC's Paul Wood: There are four sources for claims of possible Trump–Russia blackmail |magazine=[[Mother Jones (magazine)|Mother Jones]] |date=January 12, 2017 |access-date=April 1, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Lange_1/11/2017" />
On January 12, 2017, Susan Hennessey, a former [[National Security Agency]] lawyer now with the [[Brookings Institution]], said: "My general take is that the intelligence community and law enforcement seem to be taking these claims seriously. That itself is highly significant. But it is not the same as these allegations being verified. Even if this was an intelligence community document—which it isn't—this kind of raw intelligence is still treated with skepticism."<ref name="Tracy_1/11/2017" /><ref name="Hennessey_Wittes_1/12/2017">{{cite news |first1=Susan |last1=Hennessey |first2=Benjamin |last2=Wittes |title=Why Are the Trump Allegations Hanging Around When They Haven't Been Substantiated? |date=January 12, 2017 | website=[[Lawfare (website)|Lawfare]] |url=https://www.lawfaremedia.org/why-are-trump-allegations-hanging-around-when-they-havent-been-substantiated |access-date=April 1, 2018}}</ref> Hennessey and [[Benjamin Wittes]] wrote that "the current state of the evidence makes a powerful argument for a serious public inquiry into this matter".<ref name="Hennessey_Wittes_1/12/2017" />
On February 10, 2017, CNN reported that some communications between "senior Russian officials and other Russian individuals" described in the dossier had been corroborated by multiple U.S. officials. They "took place between the same individuals on the same days and from the same locations as detailed in the dossier". Some persons were known to be "heavily involved" in collecting information that could hurt Clinton and aid Trump. CNN was unable to confirm whether the conversations were related to Trump. Sources told CNN some conversations had been "intercepted during routine intelligence gathering", but refused to reveal the content of conversations or specify which communications were intercepted because the information was classified. U.S. officials said the corroboration gave "US intelligence and law enforcement 'greater confidence' in the credibility of some aspects of the dossier as they continue to actively investigate its contents". They also reported that American intelligence agencies had examined Steele and his "vast network throughout Europe and found him and his sources to be credible".<ref name="Sciutto_Perez_2/10/2017" />
On March 30, 2017, Paul Wood reported that the FBI was using the dossier as a roadmap for its investigation.<ref name="Bertrand_3/30/2017">{{cite web |last=Bertrand |first=Natasha |author-link=Natasha Bertrand |title=The FBI is reportedly using the explosive Trump–Russia dossier as a 'roadmap' for its investigation | website=[[Business Insider]] |date=March 30, 2017 |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/trump-russia-dossier-fbi-investigation-2017-3 |access-date=April 19, 2017}}</ref> On January 13, 2019, Sonam Sheth reported that the Senate Intelligence Committee was also using it as a roadmap for their investigation into Russia's election interference.<ref name="Sheth_1/13/2019">{{cite web |last=Sheth |first=Sonam |title=Grading the Steele dossier 2 years later: what's been corroborated and what's still unclear | website=[[Business Insider]] |date=January 13, 2019 |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/steele-dossier-allegations-trump-russia-mueller-investigation-2019-1 |access-date=May 9, 2020}}</ref>
On April 18, 2017, CNN reported that, according to U.S. officials, information from the dossier had been used as part of the basis for getting the [[FISA]] warrant to monitor Page in October 2016.<ref name="Perez_Prokupecz_Raju_4/18/2017" /><ref name="Nakashima_Barrett_Entous_4/11/2017" /> The Justice Department's inspector general revealed in 2019 that in the six weeks prior to its receipt of Steele's memos, the FBI's Crossfire Hurricane team "had discussions about the possibility of obtaining FISAs targeting Page and Papadopoulos, but it was determined that there was insufficient information at the time to proceed with an application to the court."<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|101}} The IG report described a changed situation after the FBI received Steele's memos and said the dossier then played a central and essential role in the seeking of FISA warrants on [[Carter Page]]<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" /> in terms of establishing FISA's low bar<ref name="Sanchez_12/11/2019" /> for [[probable cause]]: "FBI and Department officials told us the Steele reporting 'pushed [the FISA proposal] over the line' in terms of establishing probable cause."<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|412}}<ref name="Herridge_Hymes_Segers_Quinn_12/9/2019">{{cite news |last1=Herridge |first1=Catherine |last2=Hymes |first2=Clare |last3=Segers |first3=Grace |last4=Quinn |first4=Melissa |title=Justice Department watchdog releases report on origins of Russia investigation |work=[[CBS News]] |date=December 9, 2019 |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/ig-report-release-justice-department-watchdog-report-origins-russia-investigation-today-2019-12-09-live-updates/ |access-date=December 10, 2019}}</ref>
[[Mimi Rocah]], [[Dan Goldman]], and [[Barbara McQuade]] debunked three false arguments made by ''[[National Review]]'''s [[Andrew C. McCarthy]] against the FBI's use of the dossier when seeking a FISA warrant on Carter Page. They explained why the FBI was justified in doing so and would have been "derelict" if it had not: "[McCarthy] misses the point. Even if the specific details in the Steele dossier are not directly confirmed, the fact that other evidence unrelated to the dossier corroborates the dossier's main allegations is sufficient to support a finding of probable cause."<ref name="Rocah_Goldman_McQuade_7/25/2018" />
Officials told CNN this information would have had to be independently corroborated by the FBI before being used to obtain the warrant,<ref name="Perez_Prokupecz_Raju_4/18/2017">{{cite news |last1=Perez |first1=Evan |last2=Prokupecz |first2=Shimon |last3=Raju |first3=Manu |title=FBI used dossier allegations to bolster Trump–Russia investigation |work=[[CNN]] |date=April 18, 2017 |url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/04/18/politics/fbi-dossier-carter-page-donald-trump-russia-investigation/index.html |access-date=April 19, 2017}}</ref><ref name="Nakashima_Barrett_Entous_4/11/2017">{{cite news |first1=Ellen |last1=Nakashima |first2=Devlin |last2=Barrett |first3=Adam |last3=Entous |title=FBI obtained FISA warrant to monitor former Trump adviser Carter Page |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=April 11, 2017 |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/national-security/fbi-obtained-fisa-warrant-to-monitor-former-trump-adviser-carter-page/2017/04/11/620192ea-1e0e-11e7-ad74-3a742a6e93a7_story.html |access-date=April 24, 2018}}</ref> but CNN later reported "it's now clear that this level of verification never materialized".<ref name="Cohen_11/18/2021" /> Steele told the FBI that Person 1 was a "boaster" and "egoist" who "may engage in some embellishment",<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|163}}<ref name="Klein_10/19/2021" /> "caveats about his source" that the FBI omitted from its FISA application.<ref name="Klein_10/19/2021" /> In his testimony before Congress, Glenn Simpson "confirmed that the FBI had sources of its own and that whatever the FBI learned from Steele was simply folded into its ongoing work".<ref name="Dreyfuss_1/12/2018">{{cite magazine |last=Dreyfuss |first=Bob |author-link=Bob Dreyfuss |date=January 12, 2018 |title=Secrets of the Trump–Russia Dossier |magazine=[[The Nation]] |url=https://www.thenation.com/article/secrets-of-the-trump-russia-dossier/ |access-date=August 16, 2018 |archive-date=August 17, 2018 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180817060136/https://www.thenation.com/article/secrets-of-the-trump-russia-dossier/ |url-status=dead}}</ref>
The Senate Intelligence Committee criticized the FBI and found the FBI gave the allegations "unjustified credence" before first understanding "Steele's past reporting record":<ref name="SICv5_8/18/2020" />{{rp|xiv}} {{blockquote| Regarding the Steele Dossier, FBI gave Steele's allegations unjustified credence, based on an incomplete understanding of Steele's past reporting record. FBI used the Dossier in a FISA application and renewals and advocated for it to be included in the ICA before taking the necessary steps to validate assumptions about Steele's credibility. Further, FBI did not effectively adjust its approach to Steele's reporting once one of Steele's subsources provided information that raised serious concerns about the source descriptions in the Steele Dossier. The Committee further found that Steele's reporting lacked rigor and transparency about the quality of the sourcing.}}
British journalist [[Julian Borger]] wrote on October 7, 2017, that "Steele's reports are being taken seriously after lengthy scrutiny by federal and congressional investigators", at least Steele's assessment that Russia had conducted a campaign to interfere in the 2016 election to Clinton's detriment; that part of the Steele dossier "has generally gained in credibility, rather than lost it".<ref name="Borger_10/7/2017" />
On October 11, 2017, it was reported that Senator [[Sheldon Whitehouse]] ([[Democratic Party (United States)|D]]-[[Rhode Island]]), a member of the [[Senate Judiciary Committee]] (SJC), had said: "As I understand it, a good deal of his information remains unproven, but none of it has been disproven, and considerable amounts of it have been proven."<ref name="Hosenball_Landay_10/11/2017">{{cite news |last1=Hosenball |first1=Mark |last2=Landay |first2=Jonathan |title=U.S. congressional panels spar over 'Trump dossier' on Russia contacts |work=[[Reuters]] |date=October 11, 2017 |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-trump-russia-dossier/u-s-congressional-panels-spar-over-trump-dossier-on-russia-contacts-idUSKBN1CG02L |access-date=December 29, 2017}}</ref>
On October 25, 2017, James Clapper said that "some of the substantive content of the dossier we were able to corroborate in our Intelligence Community assessment which from other sources in which we had very high confidence."<ref name="Eisen_Goodman_9/24/2018">{{cite web |last1=Eisen |first1=Norman L. |last2=Goodman |first2=Ryan |title=We Have Nothing to Fear But FEAR Itself |publisher=[[New York University School of Law|Just Security]] |date=September 24, 2018 |url=https://www.justsecurity.org/60832/fear-fear/ |access-date=May 22, 2019}}</ref><ref name="Tatum_10/26/2017">{{cite news |last=Tatum |first=Sophie |title=Clapper on dossier: 'Doesn't matter who paid for it' |work=[[CNN]] |date=October 26, 2017 |url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/10/25/politics/james-clapper-ebof-cnn-tv/index.html |access-date=May 22, 2019}}</ref>
On October 27, 2017, [[Robert S. Litt]], a former lawyer for the [[Director of National Intelligence]], was quoted as stating the dossier "played absolutely no role" in the intelligence community's determination that Russia had interfered in the 2016 U.S. presidential election.<ref name="Bertrand_10/27/2017">{{cite web |last=Bertrand |first=Natasha |author-link=Natasha Bertrand |url=https://www.businessinsider.com/robert-litt-says-dossier-played-no-role-in-intel-community-russia-assessment-2017-10 |title=Former intel official: Trump–Russia dossier 'played no role' in our analysis of Russian meddling | website=[[Business Insider]] |date=October 27, 2017 |access-date=October 29, 2017}}</ref>
On November 15, 2017, [[Adam Schiff]] said much of the dossier's content is about Russian efforts to help Trump, and those allegations "turned out to be true", something later affirmed by the [[Russian interference in the 2016 United States elections#January 2017 Intelligence Community Assessment|January 6, 2017, intelligence community assessment]] released by the [[Office of the Director of National Intelligence|ODNI]].<ref name="Tau_11/15/2017">{{cite news |last=Tau |first=Byron |author-link=Byron Tau |title=Rep. Schiff Rejects Efforts to Dismiss Dossier on Trump |newspaper=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |date=November 15, 2017 |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/rep-schiff-rejects-efforts-to-dismiss-dossier-on-trump-1510777602 |access-date=July 22, 2018}}</ref>
On December 7, 2017, commentator [[Jonathan Chait]] wrote that as "time goes by, more and more of the claims first reported by Steele have been borne out", with the mainstream media "treat[ing] [the dossier] as gossip" whereas the intelligence community "take it seriously".<ref name="Chait_12/7/2017">{{cite magazine |last=Chait |first=Jonathan |author-link=Jonathan Chait |title=The Steele Dossier on Trump and Russia Is Looking More and More Real |magazine=[[New York (magazine)|New York]] |date=December 7, 2017 |url=https://nymag.com/daily/intelligencer/2017/12/the-steele-dossier-on-trump-is-looking-more-and-more-real.html |access-date=December 28, 2017}}</ref>
On January 29, 2018, a House Intelligence Committee minority report stated that "multiple independent sources ... corroborated Steele's reporting".<ref name="Eisen_Goodman_9/24/2018" />
On January 29, 2018, [[Mark Warner]], the top Democrat on the Senate Intelligence Committee, said "little of that dossier has either been fully proven or conversely, disproven".<ref name="Wheeler_2/1/2018">{{cite web |last1=Wheeler |first1=Marcy |title=Democrats Embraced a Flawed Dossier{{snd}}And Gave Republicans an Opening |url=https://www.politico.com/magazine/story/2018/02/01/russia-steele-dossier-democrats-republicans-216921/ |magazine=[[Politico]] |date=February 1, 2018 |access-date=August 22, 2022}}</ref><ref name="Glasser_1/29/2018">{{cite web |first=Susan B. |last=Glasser |title=Mark Warner: The Full Transcript |url=https://www.politico.com/magazine/story/2018/01/29/mark-warner-the-full-transcript-216543/ |magazine=[[Politico]] |date=January 29, 2018 |access-date=August 22, 2022}}</ref>
John Sipher, who served 28 years as a clandestine CIA agent, including heading the agency's Russia program, said investigating the allegations requires access to non-public records. He said "[p]eople who say it's all garbage, or all true, are being politically biased", adding he believes that while the dossier may not be correct in every detail, it is "generally credible" and "In the intelligence business, you don't pretend you're a hundred per cent accurate. If you're seventy or eighty per cent accurate, that makes you one of the best." He said the Mueller investigation would ultimately judge its merits.<ref name="Mayer_3/12/2018" /> Sipher has written that "Many of my former CIA colleagues have taken the [dossier] reports seriously since they were first published."<ref name="Sipher_9/11/2017" />
During his April 15, 2018, ABC News interview with [[George Stephanopoulos]], former FBI Director [[James Comey]] described Steele as a "credible source, someone with a track record, someone who was a credible and respected member of an allied intelligence service during his career, and so it was important that we try to understand it, and see what could we verify, what could we rule in or rule out."<ref name="Keneally_4/15/2018">{{cite news |last=Keneally |first=Meghan |title=Comey says he believes the source of the Steele 'dossier' to be 'credible' |work=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] |date=April 15, 2018 |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/comey-believes-source-steele-dossier-credible/story?id=54488781 |access-date=April 17, 2018}}</ref>
In May 2018, former career intelligence officer [[James Clapper]] believed that "more and more" of the dossier had been validated over time.<ref name="O'Hehir_5/26/2018">{{cite web |last=O'Hehir |first=Andrew |date=May 26, 2018 |title=James Clapper on Donald Trump, Edward Snowden, torture and "the knowability of truth". Obama's intelligence chief on the Steele dossier (mostly true), the Trump danger and whether he lied to Congress | website=[[Salon.com|Salon]] |url=https://www.salon.com/2018/05/26/james-clapper-on-donald-trump-edward-snowden-torture-and-the-knowability-of-truth/ |access-date=September 1, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Bowden_5/26/2018">{{cite news |last=Bowden |first=John |title=Clapper: 'More and more' of Steele dossier proving to be true |newspaper=[[The Hill (newspaper)|The Hill]] |date=May 26, 2018 |url=https://thehill.com/policy/national-security/389522-clapper-more-and-more-of-steele-dossier-proving-to-be-true |access-date=May 27, 2018}}</ref>
James Comey told the Office of the Inspector General that:<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|101}} {{blockquote| in his view, the "heart of the [Steele] reporting was that there's a massive Russian effort to influence the American election and weaponize stolen information." Comey said he believed those themes from the Steele reporting were "entirely consistent with information developed by the [USIC] wholly separate and apart from the [Steele] reporting", as well as consistent with what "our eyes and ears could also see".}}
When DOJ Inspector General [[Michael E. Horowitz]] issued a report in December 2019 on the Crossfire Hurricane investigation, the report noted that the "FBI Intel Section Chief told us that the CIA viewed the Steele reporting as 'internet rumor'".<ref name="OIG_12/9/2019" />{{rp|178}}<ref name="BBC News 2019">{{cite news |title=Former British spy rejects Trump inquiry findings |work=[[BBC News]] |date=December 10, 2019 |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-us-canada-50720350 |access-date=January 30, 2020}}</ref>
=== Varied observations of dossier's veracity ===
Steele, the author of the dossier, said he believes that 70–90% of the dossier is accurate,<ref name="Borger_11/15/2017" /><ref name="Isikoff_Corn_3/17/2018" /> although he gives the "golden showers" allegation a 50% chance of being true.<ref name="Isikoff_Corn_3/17/2018" /> In testimony to Congress, Simpson quoted "Steele as saying that any intelligence, especially from Russia, is bound to carry intentional disinformation, but that Steele believes his dossier is 'largely not disinformation'."<ref name="Donn_6/29/2018" /> Steele has countered the suggestion that the Russians deliberately fed his sources misinformation that would undermine Trump: "The ultimate Russian goal was to prevent Hillary Clinton from becoming president, and therefore, the idea that they would intentionally spread embarrassing information about Trump—true or not—is not logical."<ref name="Ewing_11/22/2019" />
Other observers and experts have had varying reactions to the dossier. Generally, "former intelligence officers and other national-security experts" urged "skepticism and caution" but still took "the fact that the nation's top intelligence officials chose to present a summary version of the dossier to both President Obama and President-elect Trump" as an indication "that they may have had a relatively high degree of confidence that at least some of the claims therein were credible, or at least worth investigating further".<ref name="Tracy_1/11/2017">{{cite news |first=Abigail |last=Tracy |url=https://www.vanityfair.com/news/2017/01/donald-trump-russia-report-intelligence-community |title=What Intelligence Experts Think of the Explosive Trump–Russia Report | website=[[Vanity Fair (magazine)|Vanity Fair]] |date=January 11, 2017 |access-date=July 31, 2017}}</ref>
Vice President [[Joe Biden]] told reporters that, while he and Obama were receiving a briefing on the extent of election hacking attempts, there was a two-page addendum that addressed the contents of the Steele dossier.<ref name="Hensch_1/12/2017">{{cite news |url=https://thehill.com/policy/national-security/intelligence/313999-biden-intel-officials-warned-us-of-trump-dossier |title=Biden: Intel officials warned us of Trump dossier |last=Hensch |first=Mark |date=January 12, 2017 |newspaper=[[The Hill (newspaper)|The Hill]] |access-date=February 1, 2017}}</ref> Top intelligence officials told them they "felt obligated to inform them about uncorroborated allegations about President-elect Donald Trump out of concern the information would become public and catch them off-guard".<ref name="Lederman_1/12/2017">{{cite news |last=Lederman |first=Josh |date=January 12, 2017 |title=Biden: Intel officials told us Trump allegations might leak |agency=[[Associated Press]] |url=https://apnews.com/152579d488a740b2a8d39fbe2f34506d/biden-intel-officials-told-us-trump-allegations-might-leak |access-date=February 19, 2018}}</ref>
On January 11, 2017, ''[[Newsweek]]'' published a list of "13 things that don't add up" in the dossier, writing that it was a "strange mix of the amateur and the insightful" and stating that it "contains lots of Kremlin-related gossip that could indeed be, as the author claims, from deep insiders—or equally gleaned" from Russian newspapers and blogs.<ref name="Matthews_1/11/2017">{{cite news |last=Matthews |first=Owen |url=https://europe.newsweek.com/donald-trump-vladimir-putin-russia-intelligence-dossier-hacking-541626?rm=eu |title=Thirteen things that don't add up in the Russia-Trump intelligence dossier |date=January 11, 2017 |work=[[Newsweek]] |access-date=February 1, 2017}}</ref> Former UK ambassador to Russia Sir [[Tony Brenton]] said certain aspects of the dossier were inconsistent with British intelligence's understanding of how the Kremlin works, commenting: "I've seen quite a lot of intelligence on Russia, and there are some things in [the dossier] which look pretty shaky."<ref name="SkyNews_1/13/2017">{{cite news |url=https://news.sky.com/story/trump-dossier-shaky-ex-british-envoy-to-russia-says-10726703 |title=Trump dossier 'shaky', former British envoy to Russia says |date=January 13, 2017 |work=[[Sky News]] |access-date=February 28, 2017}}</ref>
In his June 2017 Senate Intelligence Committee testimony, former FBI director [[James Comey]] said "some personally sensitive aspects" of the dossier were "salacious and unverified" when he briefed Trump on them on January 6, 2017.<ref name="Comey_6/8/2017">{{cite news |last=Comey |first=James |author-link=James Comey |url=https://www.cnn.com/2017/06/07/politics/james-comey-memos-testimony/index.html |title=James Comey's prepared testimony |work=[[CNN]] |date=June 8, 2017 |access-date=January 1, 2018}}</ref><ref name="Kruzel_6/21/2017">{{cite web | last=Kruzel | first=John | title=Fact-checking Fox News analyst's pro-Trump 'The Russia Hoax' | website=[[Politifact]] | date=June 21, 2017 | url=https://www.politifact.com/article/2018/aug/02/fact-checking-fox-news-analysts-pro-trump-book-rus/ | access-date=May 3, 2025}}</ref> Comey also said he could not say publicly whether any of the allegations in the dossier had been confirmed.<ref name="Berke_6/8/2017" />
Trump and his supporters have challenged the veracity of the dossier because it was funded in part by the Clinton campaign and the DNC, while Democrats assert the funding source is irrelevant.<ref name="Vogel_10_25_2017">{{cite news |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/10/25/us/politics/steele-dossier-trump-expained.html |title=The Trump Dossier: What We Know and Who Paid for It |first=Kenneth P. |last=Vogel |author-link=Kenneth P. Vogel |date=October 25, 2017 |newspaper=[[The New York Times]] |access-date=April 26, 2018}}</ref>
In June 2019, investigators for Inspector General Michael E. Horowitz found Steele's testimony surprising<ref name="Bertrand_7/9/2019">{{cite web |last=Bertrand |first=Natasha |author-link=Natasha Bertrand |title=Trump dossier author Steele gets 16-hour DOJ grilling |newspaper=[[Politico]] |date=July 9, 2019 |url=https://www.politico.com/story/2019/07/09/christopher-steele-trump-dossier-doj-1403318 |access-date=October 27, 2019}}</ref> and his "information sufficiently credible to have to extend the investigation".<ref name="Hosenball_7/9/2019">{{cite news |last=Hosenball |first=Mark |title=Trump 'dossier' author grilled by Justice Department watchdogs |work=[[Reuters]] |date=July 9, 2019 |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/us-usa-trump-russia-idUSKCN1U410I |access-date=October 27, 2019}}</ref>
In November 2019, the founders of Fusion GPS published a book about the dossier and had this to say about its veracity:<ref name="Nicholas_11/21/2019">{{cite magazine |last=Nicholas |first=Peter |title=The Real Story Behind the Steele Dossier |magazine=[[The Atlantic]] |date=November 21, 2019 |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2019/11/steele-dossier-fusion-gps/602341/ |access-date=November 22, 2019}}</ref> {{blockquote| After three years of investigations, a fair assessment of the memos would conclude that many of the allegations in the dossier have been borne out. Some proved remarkably prescient. Other details remain stubbornly unconfirmed, while a handful now appear to be doubtful, though not yet disproven.}}
David A. Graham of ''[[The Atlantic]]'' has noted that in spite of Trump's "mantra that 'there was no collusion' ... it is clear that the Trump campaign and later transition were eager to work with Russia, and to keep that secret."<ref name="Graham_1/10/2018">{{cite magazine |last=Graham |first=David A. |title=What 'Fire and Fury' Shares With the Steele Dossier |magazine=[[The Atlantic]] |date=January 10, 2018 |url=https://www.theatlantic.com/politics/archive/2018/01/trump-dossier-michael-wolff-simpson-testimony/550175/ |access-date=February 2, 2018}}</ref>
Trump and Barr claimed that Mueller exonerated Trump and that there was "no collusion", but those claims were false:<ref name="Bump_5/29/2019">{{cite news | last=Bump | first=Philip | title=Trump's mantra was once 'no collusion, no obstruction.' It isn't anymore. | newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] | date=May 29, 2019 | url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2019/05/29/trumps-mantra-was-once-no-collusion-no-obstruction-it-isnt-anymore/ | access-date=September 29, 2024}}</ref> {{blockquote| As Mueller made clear in the public statement he offered Wednesday – his first of substance since being appointed as special counsel – Trump's summary was not an accurate one. The special counsel's report explicitly rejected analysis of "collusion," a vague term that lacks a legal meaning. Instead of a lack of "collusion" between Trump's campaign and Russia, Mueller said that "there was insufficient evidence to charge a broader conspiracy."}}
Former CIA Director John Brennan stated that Trump's claims of "no collusion" with Russia were "hogwash":<ref name="Brennan_8/16/2018">{{cite news | last=Brennan | first=John O. | title=Opinion – John Brennan: President Trump's Claims of No Collusion Are Hogwash | newspaper=[[The New York Times]] | date=August 16, 2018 | url=https://www.nytimes.com/2018/08/16/opinion/john-brennan-trump-russia-collusion-security-clearance.html | access-date=August 18, 2018}}</ref> {{blockquote| The only questions that remain are whether the collusion that took place constituted criminally liable conspiracy, whether obstruction of justice occurred to cover up any collusion or conspiracy, and how many members of 'Trump Incorporated' attempted to defraud the government by laundering and concealing the movement of money into their pockets.}}
[[Adam Goldman]] and [[Charlie Savage (author)|Charlie Savage]] of ''The New York Times'' have described the impact of some of the flaws in the dossier:<ref name="Goldman_Savage_7/25/2020" /> {{blockquote| But its flaws have taken on outsized political significance, as Mr. Trump's allies have sought to conflate it with the larger effort to understand Russia's covert efforts to tilt the 2016 election in his favor and whether any Trump campaign associates conspired in that effort. Mr. Mueller laid out extensive details about Russia's covert operation and contacts with Trump campaign associates but found insufficient evidence to bring any conspiracy charges.}}
On October 17, 2021, in Steele's first major interview with ABC News, George Stephanopoulos asked him if he thought the "pee tape" was real. Steele answered that it "probably does exist", but he "wouldn't put 100 percent certainty on it". When he was asked why the Russians hadn't released it, he replied "It hasn't needed to be released. ... I think the Russians felt they'd got pretty good value out of Donald Trump when he was president of the U.S."<ref name="Bruggeman_Mosk_10/17/2021" />
== Investigations ==
On July 31, 2016, the existing FBI investigation into suspicious Russian activities and election interference suddenly morphed into the creation of the [[Crossfire Hurricane (FBI investigation)|Crossfire Hurricane investigation]] into possible Trump campaign involvement in the Russian interference. Neither investigation was triggered by the dossier,<ref name="Kruzel_7/23/2018" /><ref name="Mueller_report" /><ref name="Goldman_Savage_11/22/2019" /><ref name="Levine_9/18/2018" /> but the dossier is still the subject of the [[Russia investigation origins conspiracy theory]], a false narrative pushed by Trump, the GOP, and Fox News.
In January 2018, ABC News described how the FBI would not open an investigation into the Trump campaign based on one document like Steele's unverified report but the FBI still needed to investigate its allegations "rather than accept them as evidence".<ref name="Levine_1/12/2018" /> The Russian interference investigation was opened because of previously existing concerns: John Brennan testified he was already "aware of intelligence and information about contacts between Russian officials and U.S. persons that raised concerns" and it was that knowledge that "served as the basis for the FBI investigation to determine whether such collusion [or] cooperation occurred".<ref name="Levine_1/12/2018" /> ABC wrote that "For the FBI, the dossier was essentially just another tip" that must be investigated.<ref name="Levine_1/12/2018" />
